fieldData.featured-image-title: MyMedicare for Locum GPs in Australia: Patient Registration, BBPIP Loadings, and Billing in 2026
fieldData.post-body:
MyMedicare is now the single biggest policy reform shaping Australian general practice in 2026, and locum GPs are firmly inside the tent. Patient registration changes how bulk billing incentives flow, how chronic disease management can be billed, and how telehealth is delivered, even when the booked GP is short term locum cover. If you are taking GP locum work this year, you will need to understand how MyMedicare changes the rules around your provider number, the practice you are sitting in, and the dollars that land in your account at the end of each shift. This article walks through what MyMedicare actually is, how it interacts with locum GP work, and the practical steps to keep your billings clean in 2026.

What MyMedicare Actually Is

MyMedicare is a voluntary patient registration model run by the Department of Health, Disability and Ageing. It was designed to formalise the relationship between a patient and their usual general practice, in line with the broader move toward voluntary patient enrolment that the Strengthening Medicare Taskforce recommended back in 2023. The system is now active across the country, and patient registration has been progressively rolled out alongside new MBS items and incentive payments that only flow through the registered practice.

Two Layers: Practice Registration and Patient Registration

There are two registrations to keep straight. First, the practice itself registers in MyMedicare via the Health Professional Online Services (HPOS) portal. Second, individual patients register at that practice. A patient becomes eligible to register once they have had two face to face appointments at the practice in the last 24 months, or one face to face appointment if they live in a Modified Monash 2 to 7 location. Patients in residential aged care, on home care packages, or under 18 in some defined cohorts have separate eligibility rules.

The GPs Who Can Be Registered Against

A patient can nominate a usual GP at their registered practice, and that GP needs to be a vocationally registered GP, a non vocationally registered GP, or a GP registrar with a valid Medicare provider number for that location. Locum GPs working at a MyMedicare practice can absolutely deliver services to those registered patients; the locum simply does not become the patient's nominated usual GP.

Why MyMedicare Matters for Locum GPs

If you are a locum GP, your booking is usually with the practice, not the patient. That means the practice is the entity earning loadings, incentives, and quarterly payments under MyMedicare, while you bill MBS items under your own provider number for the work you actually do. The interaction matters because three of the highest dollar streams in general practice now sit behind MyMedicare registration.

Bulk Billing Practice Incentive Program (BBPIP)

BBPIP went live for eligible bulk billed services in 2025 and continues into 2026. Practices that register in MyMedicare and bulk bill every eligible service for every Medicare eligible patient in a calendar quarter receive a 12.5 per cent loading on top of the MBS rebates for those eligible items. The payment is made to the practice in arrears. As a locum, your bulk billed consult under your provider number contributes to that loading, which is one reason MyMedicare practices often prefer locums who bulk bill cleanly and avoid mixed billing for Medicare eligible patients during their shifts. If your booking is at a BBPIP practice, expect this to be raised in your shift brief.

Chronic Disease Management and the Practice Lock

From 1 July 2025, GP Chronic Condition Management Plans (GPCCMPs) replaced the old GP Management Plan and Team Care Arrangement items. Where a patient is registered in MyMedicare, those plans must be created or reviewed at the registered practice. As a locum, this means you can write or review a GPCCMP for a patient registered at the practice you are covering, but you cannot pull a patient's plan with you to a different clinic or use it as the basis for billing at another site. If a patient is not registered in MyMedicare, the plan can be written anywhere they consent to be seen. Make sure you confirm a patient's MyMedicare status before billing GPCCMP items.

Telehealth and the Eligible Telehealth Practitioner Rule

Most non referred GP telehealth items now require either an established clinical relationship between the patient and the practitioner or practice, or that the patient is registered in MyMedicare. The rule was previously called the "12 month established clinical relationship" rule, and as of late 2025 the language was updated to "eligible telehealth practitioner". For locum GPs this is the cleanest part of the policy. If you are covering a session at a MyMedicare practice and the patient is registered there, the patient meets the requirement automatically, even if you have never seen that patient before. If the patient is not registered, you can still bill telehealth items only where you or the practice has the relevant face to face history with that patient.

How MyMedicare Changes Your Locum Income

MyMedicare does not change your underlying MBS rebates, but it can absolutely change which services you can comfortably bill, and it can change the way practices price your locum day rate.

Loadings, Caps, and the New Bulk Billing Maths

Practices participating in BBPIP have a financial reason to keep your shift fully bulk billed for Medicare eligible patients, because mixed billing during your shift forfeits the 12.5 per cent loading on those services for the quarter. If you are used to private billing in a metro setting, expect to see more of these MyMedicare practices set up with shift instructions to bulk bill across the board. Some practices will quote you a higher locum rate, in the range of 65 to 70 per cent of billings, in exchange for a clean bulk billing arrangement that protects their loading.

Day Rates Versus Percentage of Billings

In 2026, locum GP packages typically sit between 60 and 75 per cent of billings, with rural and remote work paying flat day rates of $1,800 to $2,500 plus flights and accommodation. MyMedicare practices in metro and outer metro locations are increasingly offering hybrid arrangements: a guaranteed minimum day rate that covers your worst case scenario, with an upside if your billings exceed a threshold. The reason is that the practice now has visibility into expected patient flow through registered patients, which makes day rate forecasting easier than it was under the old system.

Workforce Incentive Program Effects

The Workforce Incentive Program (WIP) Practice Stream now factors registered patient counts into the practice's Standardised Whole Patient Equivalent (SWPE) calculation. While WIP payments go to the practice, not the locum, your services build the SWPE. For a locum covering a long term block at a regional MyMedicare practice, the practice may be more willing to offer accommodation or vehicle benefits because your continuity supports their WIP payment.

Practical Steps Before Your Next Locum Booking

Before you start your next locum shift in 2026, run through this short checklist with the practice manager. First, confirm whether the practice is registered in MyMedicare. Second, ask whether the practice is enrolled in BBPIP for the current quarter, and whether bulk billing is required for all Medicare eligible patients during your shift. Third, check whether the patient list for your session has been flagged with MyMedicare registration status, so you know which patients can be billed for GPCCMP and telehealth items without an existing face to face relationship from you. Fourth, verify your provider number is correctly linked to the practice location in HPOS, otherwise your claims may get rejected or attributed to the wrong site.

If the practice cannot tell you their MyMedicare or BBPIP status with confidence, that is a signal that compliance risk is sitting with you. Ask for a written shift brief that confirms billing expectations before you start. This protects you against Medicare audit risk and against rate disputes at the end of the shift.

Common Questions Locum GPs Are Asking in 2026

Can a locum register patients into MyMedicare? Yes, if the patient meets the eligibility criteria and has had the required face to face appointments with the practice. The registration is to the practice, with you as the GP completing it during a consult. Does my locum work count toward a patient's eligibility for MyMedicare registration at that practice? Yes, your face to face consult at the practice counts toward the two visit threshold.

What happens if I cover a patient who is registered at a different practice? You can still see and bill the patient under standard MBS items, but you cannot bill GPCCMP items, and certain non referred telehealth items will require the patient to have an existing relationship with you or your current practice. Treat the consult as you would any other locum interaction, and document clearly that the patient is registered elsewhere.

What if I am the absent GP and a locum is covering my list under my provider number? The Department of Health has confirmed that bulk billed claims under your provider number while you are away count toward BBPIP, but you remain responsible for those claims under your provider number. Make sure your locum cover arrangement is documented in writing and that your locum understands they are billing under your number and your name.

How StatDoctor Can Help

StatDoctor was built so that locum GPs and the practices booking them can see each other directly, without an agency sitting in the middle and eating the margin. On StatDoctor, every shift posted by a practice includes the information you actually need before you accept: location, hours, billing model, accommodation, and the shift specific notes that practice managers used to email back and forth with recruiters. If you are a locum GP looking at MyMedicare practices, you can filter by metro versus rural, by bulk billing model, and by clinic system, so you can match shifts to your billing comfort zone in seconds.

Practices on StatDoctor can post their MyMedicare status and BBPIP enrolment as part of the shift brief, so locums know exactly what they are walking into. Locums get paid faster, practices pay less than they would through a traditional agency, and both sides keep control of their own information. Download StatDoctor on iOS or Android, set up your provider details, and start browsing live GP locum shifts across Australia in under five minutes.
fieldData.post-summary: MyMedicare is reshaping general practice in 2026. Here is what locum GPs need to know about BBPIP, GPCCMP plans, and telehealth rules.

fieldData.featured-image-title: Locum GP Work in Residential Aged Care in Australia 2026
fieldData.post-body:
Australia's residential aged care sector is one of the fastest growing areas of demand for general practitioners, and locum GPs are increasingly stepping into the gap. Whether you cover a single nursing home for a colleague's annual leave, or run a full week of facility visits across a regional town, the work is steady, well remunerated, and genuinely meaningful. With the General Practice in Aged Care Incentive (GPACI) now in its second full year, plus a refreshed family of MBS items for residential aged care attendances, locum GPs in 2026 have a clearer set of rules and a stronger income opportunity than ever before. This guide walks you through the rates, the eligibility traps, and how to make locum aged care work pay for you.

Why aged care needs locum GPs in 2026

Australia has more than 2,800 residential aged care facilities housing roughly 190,000 permanent residents at any given time. RACGP data shows that around one in three GPs visit a residential aged care facility at least monthly, but the workforce is aging faster than it is being replaced, and many regional and outer metropolitan facilities now rely on locum cover for at least part of the year. The December and January window, when regular visiting GPs take leave, is particularly hungry for cover. If you are a locum GP with a Medicare provider number and clean AHPRA registration, you can pick up RACF work in nearly every state, often paid at 100 percent of the MBS schedule fee plus an after hours loading where applicable.

How locum GPs are paid for aged care work

There are three income streams that stack on top of each other when you do locum aged care work in Australia: standard MBS attendances, flag fall and travel items, and the General Practice in Aged Care Incentive. Understanding each one is the difference between a profitable week and a missed opportunity.

Standard MBS attendance items

For routine in hours visits to a residential aged care facility, you use the dedicated RACF attendance item family rather than standard surgery items. As of 2026, the four core items you will bill are 90020 (Level A, $20.05), 90035 (Level B, $43.90), 90043 (Level C, around $84.85), and 90051 (Level D, around $124.90). These apply irrespective of Modified Monash Model location. After hours work uses a separate family of items. Item 5028, the standard after hours Level B in an RACF, attracts $111.70 in 2026, and after hours Level C work in an RACF (item 5049) is paid at a higher rate again. After hours items are reimbursed at 100 percent of the schedule fee with no 15 percent patient gap, which is one of the reasons aged care after hours visits are financially attractive for locums willing to take on evening or weekend shifts.

Flag fall and travel items

You can also claim a separate "flag fall" or call out item for the first patient seen at an RACF on a given visit, which is paid in addition to the consultation item. The flag fall recognises the time spent travelling to the facility, donning PPE, and accessing the clinical record system. If you see ten residents on one trip, you bill ten consultations plus one flag fall, which adds roughly $50 to $60 to the visit on top of the normal item fees. If you make a separate trip later the same day for a clinically urgent patient, you may be able to claim a second flag fall, but Medicare audit teams scrutinise these claims, so document the clinical reason carefully.

The General Practice in Aged Care Incentive (GPACI)

GPACI commenced on 1 July 2024 and is the cornerstone of aged care primary care funding in 2026. It pays $300 per patient per year to the responsible provider for delivering eight regular services and two care plans across a 12 month period, plus a separate $130 per patient per year payment to the responsible practice. Payments flow quarterly. Rural loadings of up to 50 percent apply for MMM 3 to MMM 7 facilities, lifting the per patient payment for the most remote regions. The catch for locums is that GPACI flows to a single registered responsible GP and practice. If you are filling in for a regular GP for a few weeks, the GPACI payment usually still goes to them, not to you. To capture GPACI yourself, you generally need to be the patient's MyMedicare nominated provider, hold the responsible provider role across the relevant quarter, and personally complete the qualifying services.

Setting yourself up to do locum aged care work

Before you accept your first RACF locum assignment, there is a short checklist you need to complete. AHPRA general registration is non negotiable, and most facilities will also want to see your medical indemnity certificate of currency, an annual flu vaccination record, evidence of CPR certification, and a National Police Check valid within three years. Larger aged care providers run their own credentialing portals on top of this, with NDIS Worker Screening Checks, immunisation records, and mandatory training modules to upload before you can step on site.

MyMedicare and provider numbers

You need a separate Medicare provider number for each location you bill from. If you visit three nursing homes in a week, that is potentially three provider numbers to apply for, although in practice many locums use the host practice's main billing arrangement. MyMedicare registration adds another layer of complexity. To access GPACI as the responsible provider, both you and your practice must be MyMedicare registered, and the patient must have nominated you as their preferred provider in MyMedicare. Without that nomination, you can still bill MBS items normally, but the GPACI envelope is closed to you.

Indemnity considerations for aged care work

Residential aged care work attracts higher claim rates than standard general practice, particularly around end of life decisions, falls, and medication errors. Avant, MIPS, and MIGA all cover RACF work under standard GP policies, but you should confirm with your insurer that aged care consults are included within your declared scope of practice. If you intend to perform voluntary assisted dying assessments, manage syringe drivers in palliative care, or prescribe psychotropics for behavioural and psychological symptoms of dementia, declare these activities at policy renewal so you are not underinsured for the actual work you do.

Typical locum GP rates for aged care work in 2026

Locum aged care rates in Australia in 2026 vary widely by state, remoteness, and whether you bill independently or through a service agreement with the host practice. As a working guide:

  • Metro Melbourne or Sydney aged care locum sessions sit between $130 and $170 per hour, often topped up with a percentage of billings above a daily threshold.
  • Regional Victoria, NSW, and Queensland aged care locum work pays $160 to $200 per hour, with travel and accommodation included for assignments of three days or more.
  • Rural and remote MMM 5 to 7 facilities pay $200 to $260 per hour, often with a daily guarantee of $1,800 to $2,400 and a vehicle provided on site.
  • Northern Territory and remote Western Australia aged care, including Aboriginal residential aged care services, pays $260 to $320 per hour, with flights, accommodation, and a per diem stacked on top.

A full week of regional aged care locum work, billed under a service agreement at 70 percent of billings, can clear $9,000 to $14,000 in gross income before tax, depending on patient volume and after hours load. Add GPACI and rural loadings for longer term assignments, and aged care can become one of the most reliably profitable streams of locum GP work in the country.

Common pitfalls for locum GPs in aged care

The most frequent reason locum GP claims are rejected by Medicare is using the wrong item number for the location. Standard surgery items 23, 36, and 44 cannot be billed in an RACF, even if the consultation looks identical to one you would do in your rooms. You must use the 90020 series for in hours and the 5000 series for after hours. The second most common issue is double billing of the flag fall when you make multiple trips on the same day without clinical justification, which audit teams flag quickly. Beyond billing, locums also miss out on GPACI because they fail to confirm whether they are the registered responsible provider for the quarter. If you are stepping in for a regular GP for a short period, expect that the GPACI payment will flow to them, and price your locum rate accordingly. Finally, do not assume nursing home staff will run the visit for you. Bring your own kit including a stethoscope, otoscope, blood pressure cuff, and a reliable laptop or tablet for clinical software access. Many regional RACFs do not have docked workstations available for visiting GPs, and reception staff may be too stretched to print a patient list at short notice.

How StatDoctor Can Help

StatDoctor connects locum GPs directly with hospitals, GP clinics, and aged care providers across Australia, with no agency middleman taking a cut of your hourly rate. You can browse open aged care shifts in your preferred regions, see the actual hourly rate offered, view credentialing requirements, and book the assignment in minutes from your phone. Practices and aged care groups that post on StatDoctor confirm location, expected patient load, indemnity requirements, and after hours expectations upfront, so you know exactly what you are walking into before you say yes. If you are ready to add residential aged care to your locum portfolio in 2026, download the StatDoctor app on iOS or Android, complete your profile, and start receiving matched shifts in your preferred regions today.
fieldData.post-summary: A practical 2026 guide for locum GPs covering residential aged care in Australia: MBS items, GPACI payments, rates, and credentialing.
fieldData.author: 68e360c1b6fe0fae2425baeb
fieldData.name: Locum GP Work in Residential Aged Care in Australia: GPACI, MBS Items, and Pay Rates in 2026

fieldData.featured-image-title: Locum Rheumatology Jobs in Australia: Pay, Demand, and How to Start
fieldData.post-body:
Rheumatology is one of the most stretched specialties in Australia, with a documented shortfall of more than 300 adult rheumatologists and a training pipeline that simply cannot keep up with demand. For consultants who hold FRACP qualifications in rheumatology, that gap has translated into a steady pipeline of locum work across hospital outpatient clinics, day infusion units, and private practices. If you are a rheumatology trainee close to fellowship or a consultant looking for flexible work, locum rheumatology in Australia offers strong rates, regional variety, and the chance to fill a genuine clinical need.

Why locum rheumatology demand is rising in 2026

The most recent rheumatology workforce study, published in the Internal Medicine Journal in 2025, modelled demand against actual supply and found Australia needs roughly 682 adult and 61 paediatric rheumatologists to deliver an acceptable standard of care. The current workforce is short by an estimated 302 adult and 41 paediatric rheumatologists. To close that gap, the Australian Rheumatology Association has called for the specialist training rate to lift from 18 FTE per year to 32 FTE per year, and that change has not yet flowed through.

Several pressures sit underneath those numbers. The population is older, biologic and targeted synthetic DMARD use has expanded into more conditions, gout admissions continue to climb, and rheumatoid arthritis prevalence keeps rising. At the same time, a meaningful portion of the existing workforce is part time or planning to reduce hours within two years. When you combine static training output with growing patient demand and an ageing workforce, hospitals end up relying on short-term cover to keep clinics running.

What locum rheumatologists actually do

Locum rheumatology contracts in Australia tend to fall into a handful of clinical patterns. Knowing which one you are walking into helps you set rates, plan your week, and avoid surprises on day one.

Hospital outpatient cover

Public hospitals in regional centres often need locums to keep general rheumatology clinics running when their permanent consultant is on leave. You will typically run a mix of new patient referrals and review appointments, manage biologic prescribing under the PBS Section 100 framework, and liaise with infusion day units. Expect 10 to 20 patients in a full clinic day depending on case complexity.

Inpatient consult service

Tertiary hospitals occasionally cover gaps in their inpatient consult roster with short-term locum support. The work is consultative: vasculitis, connective tissue disease flares, septic joint workups, gout, and steroid management questions from medical and orthopaedic teams. These contracts often pair with one or two outpatient clinic sessions.

Private practice locum cover

Private rheumatologists going on long leave sometimes engage a locum to keep their books open. You bill under your own provider number against the rooms, with an agreed split. Patient continuity matters here, so good handover notes and a willingness to use the practice software are non-negotiable.

Day infusion supervision

Some private and public day units need a credentialed rheumatologist on site or contactable while infusions of rituximab, tocilizumab, infliximab, or belimumab are running. These are often half-day commitments that pair well with a clinic schedule.

Locum rheumatology pay rates in Australia

Locum consultant rates in Australia vary by state, hospital, urgency, and remoteness. Across all consultant specialties, the average locum rate sits at about 229 dollars per hour or 1,834 dollars per day, but rheumatology often pays above the average because supply is so tight.

For 2026, you can expect the following ranges as a rough guide:

  • Metro public hospital outpatient locum: 200 to 260 dollars per hour
  • Regional public hospital locum: 230 to 320 dollars per hour, often with travel and accommodation paid
  • Remote and rural placements (MM 5 to 7): 280 to 400 dollars per hour, plus full travel cover
  • Private practice locum cover: typically 60 to 70 percent of billings, structured as a service fee
  • Day infusion supervision: 180 to 250 dollars per hour, often shorter sessions

Hospitals that have struggled to fill a roster for months will pay at the top of these ranges. If you can offer a block of consecutive weeks rather than a single day, you are in a much stronger negotiating position.

Where the locum rheumatology work is concentrated

Distribution drives demand. Most rheumatologists in Australia work in capital cities, which means regional and rural hospitals carry the heaviest unmet need. The locations that come up most often on locum rosters are the regional referral centres in northern and western New South Wales, Far North Queensland (Townsville, Cairns, Mackay), regional Western Australia (Bunbury, Geraldton, Albany), the Sunshine Coast and Gold Coast for short cover, Hobart and Launceston in Tasmania, Darwin and Alice Springs in the Northern Territory, and regional Victoria including Bendigo, Ballarat, and Shepparton.

If you are willing to take on outreach clinics, the Rural Health Outreach Fund and similar state-funded programs subsidise specialist visits to remote locations and can pay travel, accommodation, and an attendance fee on top of your professional billings.

Qualifications and credentialing you will need

To work as a locum rheumatologist in Australia you need three things in order: AHPRA specialist registration in physician medicine with rheumatology recognition, an active FRACP qualification with advanced training in rheumatology, and credentialing at the specific facility where you are working.

AHPRA specialist registration

Most local consultants will already be registered. If you are an international rheumatologist applying through the Specialist Pathway, the Royal Australasian College of Physicians (RACP) assesses your qualifications for substantially comparable, partially comparable, or not comparable status. A substantially comparable assessment typically means a one-year peer review period before full fellowship is granted. International applicants should check the new expedited specialist pathway introduced in 2024, which has cut waiting times for doctors trained in approved jurisdictions including the UK and Ireland.

Hospital credentialing

Each Local Health District or hospital network runs its own credentialing process. Expect to provide your CV, AHPRA certificate, FRACP fellowship certificate, current medical indemnity certificate, three referees, criminal history check, working with children check (where relevant), and recent immunisation records. Build a digital pack you can email in one go, because credentialing can otherwise stretch out for weeks.

Provider number and prescriber number

If you are billing Medicare items or prescribing PBS-listed biologics, you need both an active Medicare provider number for the location you are working at and a current prescriber number. Section 100 biologics in particular require careful attention because the prescription must be tied to the right hospital pharmacy or Highly Specialised Drug arrangement.

How to break in as a locum rheumatologist

If you are coming up to fellowship or you have just finished advanced training, the path into locum work is easier than most people assume. Start by building relationships with your training network. Many hospitals will hire a fresh consultant for short blocks if they trained there, and that becomes your reference base for future work. Make sure your medical indemnity covers private locum work as well as public salaried work, because the policies are usually structured differently.

From there, you have three main options to find work. You can go through traditional locum agencies, which handle compliance and roster matching but typically charge the hospital a 25 to 35 percent margin on top of your rate. You can apply directly through hospital recruitment portals, which is slower but cuts out the agency margin. Or you can use a digital platform like StatDoctor, where hospitals post available shifts, and you connect with them directly.

Whichever route you take, be specific about what you will and will not do. If you are happy doing general rheumatology but not paediatric rheumatology, say so up front. If you need teleconferenced infusion supervision rather than on-site, flag it before contracts are signed. Locum work is much more sustainable when expectations are clear from the first email.

How StatDoctor can help

StatDoctor is an Australian platform built to connect locum doctors directly with hospitals and clinics, with no agency middlemen between you and the role. For rheumatologists, that means you keep more of your hourly rate, you talk directly with the people who run the unit you are covering, and you can see exactly what the hospital is offering before you commit. You can filter shifts by state, by remoteness, and by duration, so a Friday clinic in Geelong is just as easy to find as a four-week block in Townsville.

Download the StatDoctor app to set up your profile, upload your credentials once, and start matching with rheumatology locum opportunities across Australia. Rheumatology demand is unlikely to ease in 2026, and the consultants who work it well will be the ones who pick the right shifts at the right rate.
fieldData.post-summary: Locum rheumatology pay rates, hotspots, credentialing, and how to break in. Australia is short over 300 adult rheumatologists in 2026.
fieldData.author: 68e360c1b6fe0fae2425baeb

fieldData.featured-image-title: Income Protection Insurance for Locum Doctors in Australia 2026
fieldData.post-body:
Most locum doctors spend hours comparing pay rates between hospitals, but very few sit down and properly review their income protection insurance. That is a problem, because as a locum you have almost no employer safety net. If you cannot work for a few months due to illness or injury, the income tap turns off completely. This guide walks you through how income protection works in Australia in 2026, what doctor specific policies actually cover, and how to structure the cover so it pays out when you need it.

Why income protection matters more for locum doctors

Salaried hospital doctors typically receive ten or more days of paid sick leave each year, plus access to long service leave and employer funded death and disability cover through their public sector super fund. As a locum on an ABN, none of that applies. If you take a week off sick, you are paying yourself out of your own savings. Take three months off after a back injury or a cancer diagnosis, and the financial pressure becomes severe.

Income protection insurance fills this gap. It pays you a monthly benefit, usually up to 70 percent of your pre-disability income under post 2021 contracts, while you are too unwell to work. The benefit is taxable as income, but the premiums are usually tax deductible against your locum earnings, which softens the cost. For self employed locums, this is one of the few products that genuinely earns its keep.

How income protection actually works

Two policy settings drive almost everything else: the waiting period and the benefit period. Get these right and your cover does its job. Get them wrong and you may either pay too much in premiums or be left short when a claim happens.

The waiting period

The waiting period is how long you must be unable to work before payments start. Common options sit at 14 days, 30 days, 60 days, 90 days, and 1 year. As a locum, your buffer is your own emergency savings, so if you are running with three months of expenses set aside, a 30 or 60 day waiting period is often the sensible balance. Shorter waits mean higher premiums. AIA Priority Protection offers a 14 day option, while TAL Lifetime Protection uses 2 week, 4 week, 13 week, and 2 year intervals.

The benefit period

The benefit period is how long the insurer keeps paying you if you stay off work. Typical options are 2 years, 5 years, or to age 65. The to age 65 option is the gold standard because most catastrophic claims (such as cancer, major mental health conditions, or musculoskeletal injuries that prevent procedural work) can persist for years. A 2 year benefit period is significantly cheaper, but it is only really suitable if you have other assets to fall back on.

Definition of disability

For doctors, the definition of disability inside the policy is critical. An own occupation definition pays out if you cannot perform the duties of your specific medical role. An any occupation definition only pays if you cannot perform any work you are reasonably suited to by training. The difference matters: a surgeon who develops a tremor may not be able to operate, but could probably work as a GP or in a non procedural role. Under any occupation, the surgeon would not get paid. Under own occupation, they would. Look for own occupation cover, particularly if you work in a procedural specialty.

The APRA reforms and what changed in 2021

If you bought income protection before October 2021, your policy may be more generous than anything you can buy today. APRA introduced sweeping reforms to Individual Disability Income Insurance products to address sustainability problems in the sector. Three big things changed for new contracts.

First, agreed value contracts were phased out. New policies are indemnity, meaning the insurer assesses your income at claim time, not at policy start. Second, replacement ratios are capped at 70 percent of pre-disability earnings for the first 6 months, with reduced amounts after that. Third, contracts must be reviewed at least every 5 years rather than being guaranteed renewable for life on the original terms.

Practically, this means if you are a senior locum already on an old agreed value policy, hold on to it. If you are buying new cover, focus on getting the structure right and topping up with savings to bridge any income gap.

Doctor specific products in Australia

Several insurers offer products with features specifically tuned for the medical profession. These can include broader own occupation definitions for procedural specialists, higher monthly benefit caps, and shorter underwriting requirements through medical association partnerships.

Avant Life Insurance offers cover designed around the realities of medical practice, with own occupation and specialty specific definitions. AIA Priority Protection and TAL Lifetime Protection are widely sold to doctors through specialist financial advisers and have well developed underwriting for medical risks. NobleOak markets directly to healthcare professionals with discounted premiums for low risk groups. Older brands such as MLC On Track and AMP Elevate are also still active in the doctor market through advisers.

The right product depends on your specialty, your existing health profile, and whether you are buying through an adviser or directly. A specialist medical financial adviser will typically compare quotes from at least three insurers before recommending a structure.

How much cover do you actually need

Start with your average monthly income from locum work over the past 12 months. As a contractor on an ABN, this should be your invoiced income before tax and after legitimate business expenses. Multiply by 70 percent to find the maximum monthly benefit you can insure under post 2021 rules.

For example, a locum emergency physician invoicing 35,000 dollars per month gross, with 5,000 dollars of business expenses, has roughly 30,000 dollars of pre disability earnings. The maximum insurable monthly benefit is around 21,000 dollars. You may not need that full amount. Ask yourself what monthly figure would cover your fixed costs, including mortgage, school fees, super contributions, and basic living expenses, then work back from there.

Tax deductibility for locum doctors

The Australian Taxation Office treats income protection premiums as a deductible expense for individuals, because the benefit replaces assessable income. If you pay the premium personally, claim the deduction on your individual tax return. If you pay it through a Pty Ltd company, the deduction sits in the company. Premiums for life and TPD cover, by contrast, are generally not deductible.

One caveat: only the income protection portion of a bundled policy is deductible. If your insurer combines life, TPD, and income protection in one premium statement, ask for the breakdown so your accountant can claim correctly.

Inside super or outside super

You can hold income protection inside your super fund or outside it through an individual policy. Inside super has cashflow benefits because premiums come from your super balance rather than your bank account, but the cover is usually narrower, with stricter definitions of disability and shorter benefit periods. Outside super generally gives you better quality cover with own occupation definitions, broader features, and proper specialist underwriting.

Many locum doctors hold a small amount inside super as a baseline, then layer a fully featured outside super policy on top. Speak with a specialist medical adviser before deciding, because the right answer depends on your age, your health, and how much you contribute to super each year.

Common mistakes locum doctors make

The most expensive mistake is going without cover. The second most expensive is buying default super cover and assuming it is enough. Default policies often have any occupation definitions and 2 year benefit periods, which would not protect a 35 year old surgeon for the rest of their career. Other common errors include underinsuring during early career years when income is rising rapidly, forgetting to update the cover after a specialty change, and cancelling cover during a quiet income period only to find it is harder and more expensive to reapply later when health issues have appeared.

How StatDoctor can help

StatDoctor connects locum doctors directly with hospitals and clinics across Australia, with no agency commissions chipping away at your rate. That means more take home pay, which translates into stronger savings, better super contributions, and the ability to afford the right level of income protection cover. The StatDoctor app is free to download and gives you direct access to live shifts in your specialty across every state and territory. Browse jobs, set your rate, and get paid faster, all from your phone. Download StatDoctor on the App Store or Google Play and start booking shifts that work around your life.
fieldData.post-summary: How locum doctors in Australia structure income protection insurance: waiting periods, benefit periods, APRA rules, and tax tips.

fieldData.featured-image-title: Medical Indemnity Insurance for Locum Doctors in Australia 2026
fieldData.post-body:
Medical indemnity is one of those non-negotiable line items in every locum doctor's budget, yet most of us only think about it when the renewal invoice lands. If you are doing any private work, mixing public hospital shifts with GP locums, or assisting on theatre lists, the policy you choose can cost you thousands of dollars and shape what kind of work you can legally take on. This guide walks through how indemnity works for locums in 2026, what the three main medical defence organisations actually offer, and where the government's Premium Support Scheme can claw back some of the cost.

Why Locum Doctors Need Their Own Indemnity Cover

If every shift you work is at a public hospital and you are formally employed as a VMO or salaried registrar, the hospital's indemnity arrangements will usually cover you for clinical work performed within scope. The catch is that locum doctors rarely live in such a clean world. The moment you take on a private GP locum, do private operating lists, run a telehealth side practice, or work for a locum agency that classifies you as a contractor, hospital indemnity stops being enough.

AHPRA's professional indemnity insurance registration standard requires every registered medical practitioner to have arrangements in place that cover the full scope of their practice, including retroactive claims, run-off cover, and civil liability. If you cannot demonstrate that you meet the standard, you cannot lawfully practise. For locums, that means holding a private indemnity policy is almost always the safer default, even if some of your work is technically employer indemnified.

The Big Three: Avant, MIPS, and MIGA

Three medical defence organisations dominate the Australian market. Each is a mutual or member based body, which means premiums are designed to cover claims rather than generate shareholder profit. The differences are in coverage scope, claims philosophy, education benefits, and how they treat locum work.

Avant

Avant is the largest medical indemnity insurer in Australia, formed in 2007 through the merger of MDAV and United Medical Protection. It currently covers more than half of all Australian doctors and administers the Premium Support Scheme on behalf of the Federal Government. Avant's locum proposition is straightforward: a single membership covers a flexible mix of public, private, and telehealth work, with retroactive cover and run-off cover included. Avant also runs an extensive 24/7 medico-legal advisory service, which is the feature most members say they value when something goes wrong at 2am on a Saturday shift.

MIPS

The Medical Indemnity Protection Society (MIPS) is a not for profit mutual that has built a strong following among junior doctors and GPs. MIPS membership classifications automatically extend to most locum healthcare work as long as the locum agency or hospital has confirmed in writing that you are indemnified for your engagement. If your locum agency does not provide indemnity, MIPS expects you to upgrade your classification so that the work is properly covered. The society publishes its full coverage handbook online, which makes it easier than most to verify what is in and out of scope before you sign a contract.

MIGA

MIGA, formerly MDA SA, has a smaller but loyal member base and is often praised for personalised claims handling. For 2026, MIGA continues to offer significant premium discounts for your first six years of private practice, plus 50,000 Qantas Points when you sign up before 30 June 2026. MIGA covers selected private locum work and private surgical assistance, but its standard policy excludes private telehealth, cosmetic medicine, neurosurgery, obstetrics, and bariatric surgery. Locums who want telehealth or procedural cover need to declare it during the application process so MIGA can price the risk correctly.

How Much Does Medical Indemnity Cost in 2026?

Premiums vary widely because they are priced on your specialty, claims history, billings, and the type of work you do. As a rough guide for 2026, you can expect the following annual costs before any government subsidy:

  • General practice without procedural work: $5,000 to $9,000 per year
  • General practice with minor procedures, skin cancer, or shared antenatal care: $9,000 to $15,000 per year
  • Emergency medicine consultants: $7,000 to $12,000 per year
  • Anaesthetics consultants: $10,000 to $20,000 per year
  • Private obstetrics: $80,000 to $130,000 per year before subsidies
  • Cosmetic and aesthetic medicine: $15,000 to $40,000 per year

Locum only doctors who do not run a private practice and only accept hospital indemnified shifts can sometimes secure premiums under $1,000 per year, but you must be able to clearly demonstrate that your work falls entirely within an employer's indemnity. This is where contracts matter. A locum agency contract that lists you as a sole trader contractor without explicit indemnity language is a red flag for any insurer.

The Premium Support Scheme and Other Government Subsidies

The Premium Support Scheme (PSS) is an Australian Government subsidy that pays 60 percent of the difference between your medical indemnity premium and 7.5 percent of your gross private medical income. In practice, that means once your premium climbs above 7.5 percent of your private billings, the Commonwealth steps in and covers most of the excess. The scheme is administered by your indemnity provider, so Avant, MIPS, MIGA, and MDA National all run their own PSS application processes.

Two related schemes are worth knowing about. The Run-Off Cover Scheme (ROCS) provides free indemnity cover after you retire, take maternity leave, or leave clinical practice, removing the need to keep paying premiums for tail end claims. The High Cost Claims Scheme (HCCS) covers 50 percent of any claim payment above $300,000, which is what keeps obstetric and procedural premiums survivable. None of these schemes are automatic for new members, so it is worth asking your insurer to walk you through eligibility at sign up and again at renewal.

What Locum Indemnity Cover Actually Includes

A standard locum policy typically covers civil liability for clinical care, AHPRA notifications, coronial inquests, employer investigations, peer review processes, and Medicare audits. Most policies also include 24/7 medico-legal advice, which is by far the benefit members use most. Some MDOs throw in additional perks like CPD allowances, free educational webinars, and discounted income protection insurance, so it is worth comparing the full bundle rather than just the headline premium.

Coverage gaps that catch locums out include cosmetic procedures performed off label, telehealth provided to patients outside Australia, work in Pacific Island health programs without specific endorsement, voluntary work at sporting events, and any clinical opinions you give to friends or family in writing. If you do work that sits in any of these grey areas, declare it before you do it. Insurers are generally happy to extend cover for a small additional premium, but they will not retroactively cover an undeclared activity if a claim arises.

Choosing the Right Provider as a Locum

The right provider depends on what your work actually looks like, not just on price. If your work is mostly private GP, including procedural skin work or shared maternity, a provider with a strong rural and procedural track record makes sense. If you are a junior doctor doing public locums with the occasional GP shift, a basic membership with one of the mutuals will usually be the most cost effective option. If you are mixing telehealth with face to face work, ask each insurer how they handle private telehealth specifically, because it is the area where coverage differs the most between providers.

A few practical tips when comparing providers. Get written quotes from at least two MDOs each year, since loyalty is rarely rewarded with the best price. Ask each provider how they classify locum agency contractor work and whether you need to upgrade your classification. Confirm in writing that retroactive cover is included for prior years, especially if you are switching providers, because losing retroactive cover during a transition is one of the most common ways a previously safe doctor ends up uninsured. Check whether the policy covers Medicare audits and PSR investigations, which are increasingly common in 2026 as the Department of Health steps up its compliance program. Finally, keep your insurer updated when your scope of practice changes, even mid year, so you do not discover a coverage gap at the moment you need to lodge a notification.

How StatDoctor Can Help

At StatDoctor we work with thousands of locum doctors across Australia, and indemnity is one of the most common questions we get asked when doctors join the platform. While we do not sell indemnity ourselves, we make sure every locum shift posted through StatDoctor is paired with a clear indemnity arrangement from the hospital or clinic, and we flag any role where you would need to rely solely on your own private cover. That clarity saves you time, reduces risk, and makes it easier to choose the right MDO for the work you actually take on.

If you want to see locum shifts that are pre vetted for indemnity arrangements, fair rates, and credentialing speed, download the StatDoctor app from the App Store or Google Play. You can browse open shifts in your specialty and state, message the hospital directly, and accept work in minutes, with no agency fees and no middle layer mark ups. Your indemnity stays with you, and the work fits around your scope and your life.
fieldData.post-summary: Compare Avant, MIPS, and MIGA medical indemnity for locum doctors in Australia. 2026 premiums, PSS subsidies, and what cover includes.

fieldData.post-body:
Locum gastroenterology jobs in Australia are some of the most lucrative and reliably available specialist roles in the country in 2026. Hospitals from Cairns to Bunbury are competing for endoscopists to clear backlogged scope lists, cover registrar gaps, and keep up with an ageing population that needs more colonoscopies than ever. If you hold FRACP gastroenterology credentials and a flexible diary, you can earn well above $250 per hour and pick where you want to work. This guide walks you through the pay, the demand, the registration process, and the best way to find your next assignment.

Why Locum Gastroenterology Jobs Are in High Demand in Australia

Australia has a structural shortage of gastroenterologists, and the gap is widening. Demand for endoscopy is climbing on three fronts at the same time, while the workforce is concentrated in capital cities. That mismatch is exactly what drives the locum market.

The bowel cancer screening engine

The National Bowel Cancer Screening Program (NBCSP) now invites every Australian aged 45 to 74 to complete a free immunochemical faecal occult blood test every two years. A positive result triggers a follow-up colonoscopy. Public Health Research and Practice modelling projects annual colonoscopy use will exceed 780,000 by 2030, with the NBCSP accounting for 10 to 14 per cent of that volume. Hospitals are already struggling to meet the recommended 30 to 120 day windows for category 2 and 3 colonoscopies, and locums are the pressure release valve.

A specialist workforce stuck in the capitals

Australian Institute of Health and Welfare data shows that in 2022 there were 160 specialist FTE per 100,000 people in Major Cities, compared with just 53 in Remote and Very Remote areas. The skew is even sharper in endoscopy. Published data on endoscopist distribution found capital city local government areas have anywhere from 0.94 to 19.83 endoscopists per 10,000 population, while rural local government areas have 0 to 0.21. South Australia is a case in point: Adelaide has 45 endoscopists serving a city population of 22,690, while rural SA has just four endoscopists for 223,666 people.

A retiring senior workforce

A significant portion of Australia's gastroenterology workforce is approaching retirement age. Internationally, only 600 new gastroenterologists are entering practice annually against around 1,000 retiring or scaling back, and Australia faces a similar trend. With FRACP advanced training requiring at least 36 months of supervised experience after basic training, the supply pipeline cannot expand quickly. Locum coverage is filling the gap.

Locum Gastroenterology Pay Rates in Australia in 2026

Locum gastroenterology rates vary widely depending on whether you are taking a clinical-only role, a procedural role with endoscopy lists, or a hybrid VMO arrangement. The numbers below reflect publicly advertised 2026 ranges from major Australian agencies and hospital recruitment portals.

  • Consultant locum gastroenterologist hourly rates: $128 to $400 per hour, with most metropolitan public hospital roles paying $180 to $280 per hour.
  • Daily rates with procedural component: $2,000 to $3,000 per day, often with flights, hire car, and accommodation included.
  • Premium regional and remote rates: up to $3,500 per day for hard-to-fill weekend or after-hours endoscopy lists.
  • Queensland metropolitan consultant locum benchmarks lead the nation, averaging $258 per hour according to industry pay surveys.
  • Resident-level cover on a gastroenterology unit (basic physician trainee or equivalent) starts around $121 per hour.

Procedural volume matters. A locum doing two scope lists a day in a private setting can comfortably bill above what a salaried public consultant earns in a fortnight. If you are running endoscopy lists, ask whether the rate is inclusive of MBS billing rights or whether you are on a flat day rate with the hospital retaining the procedural fees.

Where the Locum Gastroenterology Jobs Are

Geography is your single biggest lever for pay. The further you go from Sydney, Melbourne, or Brisbane, the higher the day rate and the more generous the package.

Regional New South Wales and Victoria

Health districts such as Murrumbidgee, Mid North Coast, Hunter New England, and Western NSW LHD regularly advertise gastroenterology locum cover, often for two to four week blocks tied to specific endoscopy list requirements. Wagga Wagga, Coffs Harbour, Tamworth, Bendigo, and Ballarat are the most consistently advertised regional centres.

Queensland and the Northern Territory

Townsville, Cairns, Mackay, and Rockhampton run high-volume scope services and almost always need locum cover, particularly during school holidays and peak leave periods. The Northern Territory offers some of the highest day rates in the country, with Royal Darwin Hospital and Alice Springs Hospital both periodically advertising consultant gastroenterology locum positions with travel and remote allowances on top.

Western Australia and Tasmania

WA Country Health Service contracts gastroenterologists into hub hospitals like Bunbury, Albany, and Geraldton on fly-in fly-out arrangements. Tasmania, particularly the Launceston and North West regions, runs short-list procedural locum blocks where rates are competitive because the supply of in-state gastroenterologists is so thin.

What You Need to Work as a Locum Gastroenterologist in Australia

The path to a locum role is well defined, but the credentialing burden is heavier than for general physician work because of the procedural component.

Registration and qualifications

You will need full specialist registration with the Australian Health Practitioner Regulation Agency (AHPRA) in the specialty of internal medicine, with a recognised speciality field of gastroenterology and hepatology. For Australian-trained doctors, this means holding FRACP through the RACP Advanced Training program in gastroenterology, which requires at least 24 months of training in Australia or Aotearoa New Zealand and 36 months total of approved professional experience. International medical graduates can apply through the Specialist Pathway, which involves a comparability assessment by the RACP. The fastest route is the Expedited Specialist Pathway introduced in late 2024 for doctors trained in the UK, Ireland, US, Canada, and New Zealand.

Indemnity and credentialing

Procedural specialists need indemnity cover that explicitly includes endoscopy and any therapeutic interventions you plan to perform. Your insurer will ask you to declare procedural volumes and any complications. Hospitals will require a credentialing pack including procedural logbooks, evidence of recent recertification, and references confirming current scope of practice. Allow three to six weeks for credentialing at any new health service.

Medicare provider numbers

If you plan to bill Medicare for any endoscopy work in private rooms or day procedure centres, you need a provider number for each location. Common MBS items you will be working with include 32222 to 32229 for colonoscopy variants and 30473, 30475, and 30478 for gastroscopy. Section 19AB restrictions still apply to many IMGs, so factor in your Distribution Priority Area status when planning where to work.

Choosing Between Public, Private, and VMO Locum Work

Locum gastroenterology in Australia falls into three broad models, and the right one depends on what you want from the job.

Public hospital locum

You take a defined block, work to the hospital roster, and are paid an hourly or daily rate by the health service. You do not bill Medicare directly. This is the cleanest arrangement administratively and is well suited to early-career consultants or doctors testing out a region before relocating.

Private hospital VMO sessions

You become a Visiting Medical Officer at a private hospital, run your own endoscopy lists, and bill Medicare and the patient or insurer directly. Income is higher but you are responsible for your own accounting, billing, and chasing unpaid invoices. Many locum gastroenterologists combine a couple of regular VMO days with public hospital locum blocks elsewhere.

Hybrid arrangements

Some health services offer hybrid contracts where you cover the public clinic in the morning and run a procedural list in the affiliated private day surgery in the afternoon, with separate billing arrangements for each. Read the fine print on indemnity, kickbacks, and after-hours obligations before signing.

How to Maximise Your Locum Gastroenterology Income in 2026

The doctors earning the most from locum work tend to follow a few simple rules.

First, build a portfolio of two or three regular client hospitals rather than chasing one-off shifts. Repeat work means faster credentialing, less travel friction, and stronger negotiating power. Second, set your rate floor in writing and stick to it. Hospitals expect to negotiate, and locums who quote a single non-negotiable number often end up underpaid. Third, look at your tax structure. Sole trader status works for occasional locuming, but if you are billing more than $200,000 a year through procedural work you should talk to a medical accountant about whether a Pty Ltd structure makes sense, factoring in the Personal Services Income (PSI) rules. Fourth, claim your travel and accommodation properly. If a hospital is paying for flights and a hire car, that is not assessable income. If you are paying out of pocket and being reimbursed, the tax treatment is different again.

Finally, do not underestimate the lifestyle benefits. Locum gastroenterology is one of the few specialist career paths where you can credibly work nine months of the year on premium rates and still match a full-time consultant's income, leaving you twelve weeks for research, travel, family, or training the next generation.

How StatDoctor Can Help

StatDoctor connects locum gastroenterologists directly with hospitals and clinics across Australia, with no agency commission eating into your day rate. You set your availability, the hospitals see your profile, and bookings land straight in your calendar. We handle the credentialing reminders and contract paperwork so you can focus on the scopes and the patients.

If you are an Australian-trained gastroenterologist looking to fill gaps in your roster, an IMG newly through the Specialist Pathway, or a senior consultant moving to a portfolio career, StatDoctor is built for you. Download the StatDoctor app, set up your specialist profile in under ten minutes, and start receiving role matches from health services that genuinely need your skill set.
fieldData.post-summary: Australia faces a real gastroenterology shortage in 2026. Discover locum pay rates up to $3,000 a day, demand hotspots, and how to start.

fieldData.featured-image-title: Doctor Home Loans for Locum Doctors in Australia 2026
fieldData.post-body:
If you locum through an ABN or pick up irregular shifts across multiple hospitals, the standard home loan process can feel like it was designed for someone else. Doctor home loans solve a lot of that. In 2026, the major banks and a handful of specialist lenders treat eligible medical professionals as their lowest risk borrowers, which means waived Lenders Mortgage Insurance, higher loan-to-value ratios, and policies that actually understand locum income. This guide walks through how the products work, which lenders take locum income, and what to prepare before you apply.

What Is a Doctor Home Loan?

A doctor home loan is a residential mortgage that medical professionals can access on more favourable terms than the general public. The headline benefit is the waiver of Lenders Mortgage Insurance (LMI), an upfront cost that ordinarily applies whenever a borrower has a deposit of less than 20% of the property value. For an Australian doctor buying a $1.5 million home with a 10% deposit, that LMI waiver alone can save between $25,000 and $45,000.

The rationale is straightforward. Lenders treat doctors, dentists, and a defined list of allied health professionals as low default risk because of long career trajectories, strong income growth, and high job security. As a result, the major banks have built dedicated medico policies that bend several of their normal rules in your favour.

The 2026 Doctor Home Loan Benefits, in Plain Numbers

The current suite of benefits across the major lenders includes:

  • LMI waiver up to 95% LVR for owner-occupier purchases. Some lenders, including BOQ Specialist and Macquarie, will go to 100% LVR for doctors with the right profile, although anything above 90% is usually case by case and may sit on a separate loan account at a slightly higher rate.
  • Discounted interest rates, typically 0.10% to 0.20% below the standard professional package rate, applied for the life of the loan rather than as a teaser period.
  • Annual package fee waivers on some products, saving around $395 per year.
  • Higher borrowing capacity through generous income shading rules. Many medico policies will accept 100% of overtime, on call allowances, and locum income, where the standard policy would shade these by 20% or more.
  • Investment property LVRs of up to 90%, also without LMI, on most medico policies.

The combined effect is that a young registrar or recently fellowed specialist can buy a more expensive home, sooner, and with lower upfront costs than almost any other borrower in the country.

Are Locum Doctors Eligible for Doctor Home Loans?

Yes, with caveats. Locum doctors are not excluded from doctor home loan policies, but the way your income is structured changes the documents the bank will ask for and the way it assesses your borrowing capacity.

PAYG Locums

If you are a PAYG locum, perhaps employed casually by a hospital network or paid through a recruitment agency on an employee basis, lenders will assess you on payslips and a recent employment letter, just like any salaried doctor. The medico benefits apply in full and serviceability assessments use 100% of your income.

ABN Sole Trader Locums

If you locum through an ABN as a sole trader, you sit in the same bucket as a self-employed doctor. Standard policy at most lenders requires two years of personal tax returns and two years of ATO Notices of Assessment to prove your income is stable. The lender will usually take an average of the two years, or in some cases use the lower of the two, as the figure that drives your borrowing capacity.

Pty Ltd Locums

If you locum through a Pty Ltd company, the bank will want two years of company financials and personal tax returns, plus an accountant's letter. They will then add back items like depreciation and director's superannuation contributions to arrive at a true earnings figure that reflects what the business is producing for you personally.

The "One Doc" Option for Newer Locums

Several specialist lenders, including BOQ Specialist and a small group of non-bank lenders, now offer one year self-employed doctor home loans, often marketed as "One Doc" products. These let you apply with a single year of tax returns and Notices of Assessment, plus a current contract or schedule of upcoming locum bookings to demonstrate continuity of income. The interest rate sits modestly above the equivalent full-doc product, but for a doctor who has only recently left a salaried role to locum full time, the option to buy a home this year rather than next can be worth far more than the rate gap.

Which Lenders Offer Doctor Home Loans for Locums in Australia?

The following lenders all run dedicated medico policies in 2026, and all will consider locum income provided the documentation supports it.

NAB Medico, through the Medfin division, offers up to 95% LVR with no LMI for owner-occupier loans and 90% LVR for investment loans. NAB will accept a wide range of medical professions, including pharmacists and optometrists in addition to doctors and dentists.

CBA Medico, through Commonwealth Bank's medical and professionals lending team, offers up to 95% LVR with no LMI and a discounted package rate.

Westpac Medico, available through Westpac Group banks including St George, offers similar 95% LVR no LMI terms with package rate discounts.

ANZ Doctors offers a comparable product set, though specific eligibility lists and LVR caps vary by application and are reviewed individually.

BOQ Specialist remains the most flexible for high LVR and locum income, with up to 100% LVR for owner-occupiers in some scenarios and a strong record of approving complex locum income profiles.

Macquarie Bank has a dedicated medical lending team that competes hard on rate and on policy flexibility for ABN locums, particularly for doctors with two clean years of returns.

Mortgage brokers who specialise in medical clients can usually access all of the above, plus several non-bank options that are sometimes more accommodating to newer locums or borrowers with only one year of self-employment behind them.

Documents to Prepare Before You Apply

The application will move much faster if you assemble the following before your first conversation with a lender or broker.

For PAYG locums: the last three months of payslips, your most recent employment contract, and three months of bank statements.

For ABN sole trader locums: two years of personal tax returns, two years of Notices of Assessment, your last two BAS lodgements if you are GST registered, and a year-to-date profit and loss statement from your accountant if you are partway through a financial year.

For Pty Ltd locums: two years of company financials, two years of personal tax returns, two years of Notices of Assessment, an accountant's letter confirming your income, and an ASIC company extract.

For all applicants: proof of AHPRA registration, evidence of your current Medical Board fellowship or training status, and details of any private health insurance, income protection, or life insurance you hold.

A Common Pitfall: Explaining Locum Gaps

If you take regular two or three week holidays between locum contracts, be ready to explain it. Lenders sometimes flag long gaps as periods of unemployment and shade your assessed income downward. A short letter from your accountant noting that the gaps are planned leave, not idle months, often resolves this without any reduction to your assessed income.

How Much Can a Locum Doctor Borrow in 2026?

Borrowing capacity in Australia is governed by each bank's serviceability calculator, which applies a buffer of 3% on top of the actual interest rate to test whether you can still afford the loan if rates rise. As a rule of thumb, a single doctor with no dependants and minimal other debts can borrow around 5 to 6 times their gross annual income, and a couple where both partners earn well can sometimes go higher.

In practical terms, an unfellowed registrar earning $180,000 in locum income might borrow around $900,000 to $1.1 million. A fellowed specialist locum earning $450,000 might borrow $2.5 million or more, depending on lifestyle costs and any existing investment debts.

How StatDoctor Can Help

StatDoctor connects locum doctors directly with hospitals and GP clinics, which means more consistent shift bookings and a cleaner paper trail for any future home loan application. Every shift you take through the platform is logged with the hiring facility, the hours worked, and the rate paid, so when a lender asks for evidence of income continuity you can produce a clean shift history rather than scrambling through old emails and bank statements.

Download the StatDoctor app from the App Store or Google Play to see the locum shifts available near you, set the rates you are willing to accept, and start building the income record that will support your next mortgage application. If you are a hospital or clinic looking to fill shifts without the costs of a traditional agency, get in touch through statdoctor.app to set up your account.
fieldData.post-summary: Doctor home loans give eligible medical professionals access to waived Lenders Mortgage Insurance, discounted rates, and higher LVRs, but locum doctors need to navigate income verification carefully. This 2026 guide covers the major lenders, the documents you need, and how locum income on PAYG, ABN, or Pty Ltd is assessed.
fieldData.author: 68e360c1b6fe0fae2425baeb
fieldData.name: Doctor Home Loans for Locum Doctors in Australia: How to Access Specialist Mortgages and Waived LMI in 2026
