fieldData.post-body:
If you locum through a company or trust, the way you pay tax in 2026 may need a closer look. Personal Services Income (PSI) is one of the most misunderstood areas of tax law for locum doctors, and the ATO has just sharpened its compliance focus through Practical Compliance Guideline PCG 2025/5, released on 1 December 2025. This guide explains how the PSI rules apply to locum doctors, walks through the four tests that decide whether you escape them, and sets out what the new ATO position means for your structure heading into the 2026/27 financial year.

What Is Personal Services Income, and Why Does It Matter to Locum Doctors?

Personal Services Income is income that is mainly a reward for your personal effort, skill, or expertise as an individual. The ATO uses a simple "more than 50%" rule. If more than half the value of what you invoice is for the labour you personally performed (rather than for the use of equipment, materials, or staff you employ), the income is PSI.

For locum work, this is almost always the case. When you take a 12 hour ED shift in regional Victoria or a week of GP cover in the Pilbara, the hospital or clinic is paying for your hands and your clinical judgement. The bed, the EMR, the practice nurse, and the consulting room belong to them. That makes nearly every locum invoice a PSI invoice by default.

Why does it matter? Because if the PSI rules apply to you and you are working through a company or trust, the ATO will treat the net income as if it were yours personally. You cannot retain profits at the 25% small business company tax rate, you cannot split income with a spouse who does not perform the work, and your deduction list shrinks to what an employee in the same role could claim.

When Does PSI Apply to Locum Doctors?

The PSI rules apply by default to any individual, company, partnership, or trust that earns Personal Services Income, unless the entity qualifies as a Personal Services Business (PSB).

For locum doctors, that means three common scenarios usually trigger the rules:

You are a sole trader invoicing hospitals or practices through your own ABN. The income is PSI, but in most cases the practical effect is limited because you are already taxing it at your marginal rate.

You operate through a Pty Ltd company that invoices for your locum services. Without PSB status, all net income must be paid out to you personally, you cannot retain profits in the company, and you cannot pay a wage to a non working spouse without it being attributed back to you.

You operate through a discretionary trust. The same attribution applies. The trust cannot distribute PSI to anyone other than the doctor who performed the work.

The rules do not apply if your locum income flows from a true Personal Services Business, which is what the four tests below are designed to identify.

The Four PSI Tests and How Locums Pass Them

To be a Personal Services Business, you need to satisfy at least one of four tests. The catch is that for three of them, you also need to meet what the ATO calls the 80% rule, covered in the next section. Only the Results Test stands alone.

The Results Test

You pass the Results Test if at least 75% of your PSI in the year satisfies all three of the following: you are paid to produce a specific result, you supply your own tools and equipment, and you are liable to fix defects at your own cost.

Most locum work fails this test. You are paid for hours worked, the hospital supplies the equipment, and clinical errors are dealt with through indemnity rather than rework. A small number of procedural locums (for example, a contract to run a specific theatre list with your own instrument set) might pass, but it is uncommon.

The Unrelated Clients Test

This is the test most locum doctors rely on. You pass if you receive PSI from two or more unrelated clients in the income year, and you were engaged because you made offers to the public (a website, a profile on a platform, advertising, or being listed in a referrer database).

Working a single ongoing locum block at one hospital for a year will not pass. Spreading your year across three different health services almost certainly will, provided you can show how you made yourself available to the market.

The Employment Test

You pass if you engage one or more other entities (employees or contractors who are not associates) to perform at least 20% of the principal work by market value. Almost no locum doctors meet this test, because the principal work is your own clinical labour and you cannot subcontract a shift to your spouse.

The Business Premises Test

You pass if you maintain business premises that are used exclusively for your work, are physically separate from your home and from your clients, and from which you conduct most of your services. Again, this is rare for locums, since you work at the hospital or clinic that engages you.

The 80% Rule for Locum Income

The Unrelated Clients, Employment, and Business Premises tests are only available if you also pass the 80% rule. That rule says no more than 80% of your PSI in the year can come from a single client and its associates.

For a locum who works for one rural health service for ten months and picks up a few weekends elsewhere, the math gets tight. If 85% of your invoices are paid by the same Local Health District, you fail the 80% rule and you lose access to the Unrelated Clients Test, even if you technically had four other clients on the books.

If you do fail the 80% rule, you can still apply to the ATO for a PSB Determination, but it is at the Commissioner's discretion and rarely granted to medical practitioners on the basis of locum work alone. The simpler fix is to plan your year so that no single client tips past 80% of total billings.

PCG 2025/5: The 2026 Compliance Shift Every Locum With a Company Should Know About

On 1 December 2025, the ATO published PCG 2025/5, its updated compliance approach to PSI and Part IVA of the Income Tax Assessment Act 1936. The guideline applies to any arrangement where income generated mainly from one individual's efforts is booked, retained, or distributed through a company, trust, or partnership.

The most important takeaway for locum doctors is this. Even if you pass one of the four tests and qualify as a Personal Services Business, the ATO can still apply Part IVA if your structure is being used to retain profits at the company tax rate or to divert income to associates. Passing the PSB tests is no longer a complete shield.

The guideline introduces a risk framework. You sit in the low risk zone if your structure broadly reflects commercial reality, which the ATO describes as a position where:

  • You are paid market appropriate remuneration as the working individual.
  • Profits are ultimately taxed in the hands of the person doing the work.
  • Associates are only paid for genuine services at commercial rates.
  • Profits are retained inside the company only temporarily for genuine working capital needs.
  • Superannuation contributions are made for you as an employee at SG rates or above.

You move into a higher risk zone if you retain large profits in the company, pay a non working spouse a director's fee, or distribute trust income to adult children who do not perform medical work.

The ATO has flagged that it will not apply Part IVA to past arrangements where the taxpayer makes a genuine attempt to move into a low risk position by 30 June 2027. That gives you the 2026/27 financial year to review your structure and, if needed, restructure salaries, distributions, and retained earnings before the grace period closes.

Practical Steps to Stay on the Right Side of the PSI Rules in 2026

If you are a locum doctor with a company or trust, a clean position before EOFY 2027 looks something like this. Run the four tests early in the year, ideally in July, and document which one you expect to pass. If you are relying on the Unrelated Clients Test, keep a record of every client engagement and every offer to the public, including your StatDoctor profile, your locum agency listings, and any direct outreach to medical workforce units.

Pay yourself a market salary that reflects what an employed doctor in the same role would earn, including the 12% superannuation guarantee that took effect on 1 July 2025. Avoid distributing residual profits to non working family members. If your accountant is recommending income splitting that does not pass a commercial reality sniff test, ask them how it sits against PCG 2025/5 specifically.

Review your invoicing. If you are heavily concentrated with one health service, plan a few additional engagements before year end to keep below the 80% threshold. And if your existing structure is at the high risk end of the spectrum, the window to restructure without Part IVA exposure closes on 30 June 2027.

How StatDoctor Can Help

StatDoctor connects locum doctors directly with hospitals and GP clinics across Australia. By giving you visibility over multiple health services and shifts at the same time, the platform makes it far easier to spread your year across unrelated clients, document your offers to the public, and stay on the right side of the 80% rule.

Every shift booked through StatDoctor is invoiced cleanly to the hiring facility, with no agency markup sitting between you and the client. That keeps your tax and PSI position transparent, and gives you a digital trail of separate engagements that supports a Personal Services Business position if the ATO ever asks.

You will still need a good medical accountant to sign off on your structure, but you will have the workflow to make their job easy. Download the StatDoctor app from the App Store or Google Play, complete your profile in under ten minutes, and start picking up shifts that work for your tax position as well as your clinical interests. Nothing in this article is tax advice, and you should always speak to a registered tax agent who understands locum work before changing your structure.
fieldData.post-summary: Personal Services Income rules decide how locum doctors with a Pty Ltd or trust pay tax. This 2026 guide walks through the four PSI tests, the 80% rule, and the new ATO compliance position under PCG 2025/5 ahead of the 30 June 2027 deadline.
fieldData.author: 68e360c1b6fe0fae2425baeb
fieldData.name: Personal Services Income (PSI) Rules for Locum Doctors in Australia: A Practical Guide for 2026

fieldData.post-body:
Ophthalmology has quietly become one of the most lucrative locum specialties in Australia, and 2026 is opening more doors than ever for consultants who are willing to travel. With around 84% of ophthalmologists working in major cities and only 16% covering regional Australia, the gap between supply and demand keeps widening. If you hold FRANZCO Fellowship, are working through the Specialist International Medical Graduate (SIMG) pathway, or are simply curious about whether locum work suits your career stage, this guide walks you through pay, demand, registration, and the practical mechanics of getting on the road.

Why locum ophthalmology demand is surging in 2026

Australia has one of the lowest per capita ophthalmologist counts in the OECD, and the workforce is heavily concentrated in capital city CBDs. Roughly 29% of Australians live in regional areas, but they are served by only about 16% of the ophthalmology workforce. The result is multi-month wait lists for cataract surgery, glaucoma reviews, and paediatric clinics across rural Local Health Districts (LHDs).

RANZCO has responded by launching the Regionally Enhanced Training Network (RETN), which sends roughly two-thirds of trainee time into regional Australia from the 2026 selection round. That is a long-term fix. In the short term, hospitals continue to rely on locum and visiting consultants to clear lists, run outreach clinics, and cover parental leave or sabbaticals.

For you, the locum doctor, this translates into reliable demand for cataract lists, general clinic blocks, and on-call cover, especially in regional Queensland, regional New South Wales, the Northern Territory, Tasmania, and parts of Western Australia.

How much do locum ophthalmologists earn in Australia?

Locum ophthalmology pay sits at the higher end of the consultant market because the specialty is procedural, output is highly visible (cleared cataract lists), and travel is often required to access the work. Rates vary by employer type, urgency, and whether you are billing through a public VMO arrangement or running a private day surgery list.

Public hospital VMO and locum rates

Sessional VMO rates for ophthalmologists across the major states sit broadly between $260 and $360 per hour in 2026, with daily locum rates from agencies and hospitals typically landing in the $2,500 to $3,500 per day band for a standard 9 to 10 hour day. Short notice cover and remote postings can push past $4,000 per day once travel and accommodation loadings are included.

On call work for after hours emergencies (open globe injuries, retinal detachments, post operative complications) is usually paid as an availability allowance plus a recall rate. Expect $400 to $700 per night for availability, and recall rates that match or slightly exceed daytime hourly rates.

Private day surgery lists and outreach

If you take fee for service work in a private day surgery, you bill Medicare and any health fund gap directly. A high volume cataract list of 8 to 12 cases can comfortably generate $5,000 to $9,000 in billings before practice fees, depending on lens type, complexity, and whether you are doing combined procedures. Outreach lists funded under the Rural Health Outreach Fund and the Visiting Optometrists Scheme add travel and accommodation on top.

Daily take home after costs

The headline rate is not the take home rate. As a sole trader or Pty Ltd locum you will need to deduct indemnity insurance (often $15,000 to $35,000 a year for an ophthalmologist depending on procedural mix), travel time, accommodation, and superannuation contributions. Most consultants who locum 8 to 12 weeks per year add $200,000 to $400,000 to their gross income, with rural and remote postings sitting at the higher end.

Where the locum ophthalmology hotspots are in 2026

The demand map follows the workforce shortage. The most consistent locum opportunities right now are in:

Regional Queensland, particularly Cairns, Townsville, Mackay, Rockhampton, and Toowoomba. Queensland Health funds significant outreach work into Western Queensland and the Torres Strait, and the cataract surgery wait list pressure is among the highest in the country.

Regional New South Wales, including Tamworth, Dubbo, Orange, Wagga Wagga, and Coffs Harbour. The Western NSW LHD and Hunter New England LHD both run rotating consultant blocks that suit locum cover.

Northern Territory, where Alice Springs and the Top End rely on visiting consultants for almost all subspecialty work. Indigenous eye health programs, including those linked to The Fred Hollows Foundation and the Indigenous Australians' Health Programme, fund regular outreach blocks.

Tasmania, where the public ophthalmology service is small and regularly looks for locum cover for parental leave and sabbatical gaps in Hobart, Launceston, and the North West.

Regional Western Australia, including Bunbury, Geraldton, and the Kimberley, where Lions Outback Vision and the WA Country Health Service both engage visiting consultants.

Registration and credentialing requirements

You cannot pick up a locum ophthalmology booking on enthusiasm alone. The credentialing checklist is long but predictable.

Australian and New Zealand trained Fellows

If you hold FRANZCO and full AHPRA specialist registration, you can work anywhere in Australia. You will still need to clear hospital credentialing for each new site, which usually means submitting your CV, recent CPD log, indemnity certificate, vaccination evidence, two professional referees, and a National Police Check. Build in two to six weeks for credentialing at a new hospital, and longer for the larger metropolitan health services.

You will also need to keep your CPD home (RANZCO is the recognised CPD home for ophthalmology) up to date. The Medical Board requires you to log a minimum of 50 hours of CPD across reviewing performance, measuring outcomes, and educational activities each year.

Specialist International Medical Graduates

If you trained overseas, RANZCO assesses your training and experience for comparability with an Australian ophthalmologist. Applications are accepted between 1 February and 1 September each year. There are two main pathways:

Specialist Recognition, which allows you to practice anywhere in Australia or New Zealand once the assessment is complete. SIMGs found partially comparable complete a period of upskilling, while those found substantially comparable complete a period of oversight before being eligible for full Fellowship.

Area of Need (AoN) plus Specialist Recognition, which can be used when you have already been offered a designated AoN position. This pathway gets you working sooner but ties you to that specific role until you transition to specialist recognition.

Note the 10 year Medicare moratorium that applies to most SIMGs. For 10 years from your initial Australian medical registration you can only access Medicare provider numbers in Districts of Workforce Shortage. The good news for locum consultants is that almost every regional and rural location is a District of Workforce Shortage, so the moratorium tends to align with where the demand actually is.

Visa pathway for international consultants

The most common visa for an SIMG locum ophthalmologist is the subclass 482 Skills in Demand visa, sponsored by either a hospital, a private practice, or an approved labour hire arrangement. The 186 Employer Nomination Scheme and 494 Skilled Employer Sponsored Regional visas are also options once you are settled. Each carries different obligations around length of stay, regional commitment, and pathway to permanent residency.

What a typical locum ophthalmology block looks like

A common pattern is a Monday to Friday block at a regional public hospital, anchored around two operating lists and three to four clinics. A typical week might run a cataract list on Monday morning, a general clinic on Monday afternoon, theatre lists on Tuesday and Thursday, a glaucoma or medical retina clinic on Wednesday, and an injection clinic and discharge ward round on Friday morning before flying home.

Some consultants prefer fly in fly out (FIFO) arrangements where they fly in on Sunday evening, work through Friday lunchtime, and return home for the weekend. Others prefer two week or four week blocks, particularly where remote outreach travel is involved. The right rhythm depends on family commitments, your subspecialty, and your appetite for travel.

Practical tips for maximising your income

Treat your first locum block as an extended interview for the next one. Hospitals that find a reliable consultant tend to rebook them every quarter, often at preferential rates compared with agency placements. The doctors who earn the most as locums tend to do three things consistently:

First, they invest in efficient operating lists. Having your own surgical preferences, comfort with multifocal and toric lenses, and the ability to clear 10 to 12 cataracts in a half day list makes you the consultant the hospital wants back. Second, they manage their finances proactively, registering for GST, lodging quarterly Business Activity Statements on time, and putting 30% to 40% of every invoice aside for tax. Third, they keep credentialing up to date across multiple sites, so they can say yes to short notice work without scrambling for paperwork.

If you are new to locum work, pick one or two hospital networks within a single state for your first six months. The repeat bookings, established credentialing, and known team dynamics make the work easier and the income more predictable than chasing one off bookings across the country.

How StatDoctor can help

StatDoctor connects ophthalmologists directly with hospitals and clinics across Australia, without the agency markup that can quietly take 25% to 35% of your billable rate. You set your rates, choose your locations, and the hospital pays you directly. That means more take home pay for you, and lower workforce costs for the health service you cover.

Whether you are a Sydney based consultant looking to add a regional block to your year, a SIMG working through specialist recognition in an Area of Need post, or a hospital workforce manager trying to keep theatre lists running through summer, StatDoctor gives you a faster, more transparent way to fill the gap. Download the StatDoctor app on iOS or Android, complete your profile and credentials once, and start receiving direct shift offers from hospitals that need your subspecialty.

Australia needs more ophthalmologists in the regions, and locum work is one of the fastest ways to get there. We will help you get paid properly for it.
fieldData.post-summary: Australia's ophthalmology workforce is heavily skewed toward capital cities, leaving regional hospitals with long wait lists and reliable demand for locum consultants. This 2026 guide covers pay rates, hotspots, registration pathways for FRANZCO holders and SIMGs, and how to make locum ophthalmology work as part of your career.
fieldData.author: 68e360c1b6fe0fae2425baeb
fieldData.name: Locum Ophthalmology Jobs in Australia: Pay Rates, Demand, and How to Get Started in 2026

fieldData.post-body:
If you trained overseas and are now practising in Australia, the term Section 19AB has probably come up more than once. It is the piece of legislation that sits behind the so called 10 year moratorium, and it dictates where international medical graduates can work and have their patients access Medicare rebates. Understanding how the rule works, and more importantly how scaling can reduce your time, is one of the most valuable things you can do for your locum career in 2026.

What Is Section 19AB?

Section 19AB of the Health Insurance Act 1973 is the Commonwealth law that restricts overseas trained doctors, foreign graduates of accredited medical schools, and former temporary resident graduates from accessing Medicare benefits unless they work in a Distribution Priority Area or District of Workforce Shortage. In practical terms, if your patients cannot bill Medicare, your billing capacity is limited and most private clinics will not be able to employ you. The restriction lasts for 10 years from the date you first obtained medical registration in Australia, which is why it is commonly called the 10 year moratorium.

The rule applies whether you came to Australia from the United Kingdom, India, Pakistan, the Philippines, South Africa, Malaysia, the United States, or anywhere else outside Australia or New Zealand. It also applies if you completed your medical degree in Australia but were a temporary resident at the time of enrolment.

Who Is Affected

You are subject to Section 19AB if you are an international medical graduate who completed your primary medical qualification overseas, a foreign graduate of an Australian accredited medical school enrolled as a temporary resident, or a permanent resident or citizen who is still within their 10 year moratorium period. The restriction applies to general practitioners working under DPA rules and non GP specialists working under DWS classifications.

Who Is Not Affected

If you completed your primary medical degree in Australia or New Zealand and were a citizen or permanent resident at the time, the moratorium does not apply to you at all. Australian trained doctors with full Medicare access can practise anywhere from day one. This is one of the reasons rural and regional employers compete so heavily for IMG locums, the workforce supply is shaped directly by Section 19AB.

How the 10 Year Moratorium Affects Locum Work

For locum doctors, Section 19AB shapes which jobs you can take. A locum GP role in inner Sydney that does not hold DPA status is generally off limits during your moratorium period. A locum role in a Modified Monash 4 town in regional Victoria, by contrast, sits squarely within the rules and often comes with daily rates of $2,000 to $2,500. The rule effectively pushes IMG locums toward the regions that need them most, which is also where the highest pay sits.

Hospital based locum work is treated differently. If you are working purely on a hospital salaried basis, billing through your employer rather than under your own provider number, Section 19AB does not directly restrict you. This is why many IMG registrars and consultants take hospital locum shifts during their moratorium years while building toward private practice or a Medicare provider number.

Scaling: How to Reduce Your Moratorium

The most useful part of Section 19AB for many IMGs is scaling. Scaling is the formal mechanism that reduces the length of your moratorium when you work in more remote parts of Australia. The scheme uses the Australian Statistical Geographical Classification Remoteness Area, known as RA, rather than the Modified Monash Model. The two systems overlap but they are not identical, and Services Australia applies your scaling credits using the RA value of the location where you bill Medicare.

The Scaling Table

Under the standard scaling structure, an IMG who works exclusively in a major city (RA1) for the full term will serve the entire 10 years. The reductions begin as soon as you move further out. Working in Inner Regional Australia (RA2) reduces your moratorium to 9 years. Outer Regional Australia (RA3) reduces it to 7 years. Remote Australia (RA4) brings it down to 6 years, and Very Remote Australia (RA5) reduces it to 5 years, the maximum reduction permitted under the legislation.

To put that in concrete terms, if you spend 4 years doing locum GP work in Port Hedland (RA4) and Broome (RA4) before moving to a metro practice, you can shave roughly 4 years off your remaining obligation, depending on the scaling formula and your billing volume in each month.

Eligibility for Scaling Credits

Scaling does not apply automatically just because you are physically working in a regional town. You need to hold a rebatable Medicare provider number for that location, and you need to be billing more than $5,000 in Medicare services per calendar month at that site. Once you cross that threshold, Services Australia automatically calculates and applies the credit. You do not need to submit a separate application.

This billing rule is a critical detail for locum doctors. Short three day stints will not always trigger the threshold, particularly if you are filling in for a low volume GP. Block bookings of two weeks or more, where you are running a normal patient load, almost always do.

Tracking Your Credits

You can view your accrued scaling credits and remaining moratorium period through your Health Professional Online Services account on Services Australia. Most IMG doctors find it useful to check this every six months, particularly if you have moved between RA classifications during the year. Discrepancies do happen, usually when billing has been incorrectly attributed to the wrong practice location, and they are easier to fix while the data is recent.

Section 19AB Exemptions

Beyond scaling, there are formal exemption pathways that can shorten or remove the moratorium entirely. Exemptions are assessed individually by the Department of Health, Disability and Ageing and they fall into a few main categories.

The After Hours Exemption allows you to provide unrestricted Medicare services after hours, even in non DPA areas, provided your normal weekday work continues to meet the moratorium rules. The Spousal Exemption can apply if your partner has a substantial professional or family connection that makes relocation impractical. There is also a category for doctors providing services in areas of acute workforce shortage that are not formally classified as DPA or DWS, though these are reviewed on a case by case basis.

Exemptions are not automatic. You need to apply through Services Australia with supporting documentation, and approval can take several weeks. They are most often relevant to GPs in private practice rather than hospital locums, but it is worth knowing they exist.

Permanent Residents Versus Temporary Residents

Your residency status changes how the moratorium ends. If you are a permanent resident or Australian citizen, the moratorium drops away the moment your 10 year period concludes (or the scaled period if you have earned credits). After that you can practise anywhere in Australia with full Medicare access.

If you remain a temporary resident, the situation is different. The moratorium does not end on its own at the 10 year mark. Temporary resident doctors continue to be restricted to DPA or DWS locations until they obtain permanent residency or citizenship. This is one of the strongest practical arguments for progressing your visa pathway during the moratorium period rather than waiting until after.

Recent Updates You Should Know About

The rules around Section 19AB have been updated several times over the past 18 months as part of the Federal Government's broader workforce reforms. Between mid 2025 and early 2026, the Department of Health, Disability and Ageing released revised guidance covering DPA boundaries, scaling thresholds, and the documentation required for exemption applications. These updates were aimed at smoothing the path for IMG locums into rural and regional areas where the workforce shortage is most acute.

The expansion of the Bulk Billing Practice Incentive Program in late 2025 sits alongside these changes. While BBPIP and Section 19AB are separate systems, an IMG GP working in a DPA location during the moratorium can stack the higher bulk billing incentives on top of their locum rate, which can materially change the economics of a regional placement.

Practical Strategy for IMG Locum Doctors

If you are early in your moratorium, the best strategy is usually to weight your first few years toward higher RA classifications. A 12 month run of regional and remote locum placements at the start of your moratorium period can compress your overall obligation significantly. Rates in those locations are also higher, so the financial trade off favours the rural choice.

If you are mid moratorium and unsure where you stand, start by checking your remaining time on Health Professional Online Services. From there, plan your next two to three placements with the RA classification of each location in mind. Even one block of work in an RA4 or RA5 town can move your end date forward by months.

If you are close to the end of your moratorium, focus on consolidating. Make sure your billing is being attributed correctly, your provider numbers are linked to the right practice locations, and your residency status is settled. The last thing you want is to discover an administrative gap in the final six months.

How StatDoctor Can Help

Working out which locum jobs count toward your scaling credits, and which ones simply tick the DPA box without moving the needle on your moratorium, is a complicated piece of work. StatDoctor takes that complexity and lays it out clearly. Every locum listing on the platform shows the location, the RA and MMM classifications, and the DPA status, so you can see at a glance whether a placement helps your scaling clock or not.

StatDoctor connects locum doctors directly with hospitals and GP clinics across Australia, with no agency middleman taking a slice of your rate. For IMG doctors working through the moratorium, that transparency matters. You can plan your career intentionally, choosing placements that build your scaling credits, your clinical experience, and your income at the same time. Download the StatDoctor app today and start browsing locum opportunities that fit where you are in your Section 19AB journey.
fieldData.post-summary: Section 19AB shapes where international medical graduates can work in Australia and for how long. This guide explains the 10 year moratorium, how scaling can shave up to 5 years off your time, and what every IMG locum doctor should know in 2026.
fieldData.author: 68e360c1b6fe0fae2425baeb
fieldData.name: Section 19AB and the 10 Year Moratorium: How IMG Locum Doctors in Australia Can Reduce Their Time

fieldData.post-body:
Palliative medicine is one of Australia's quietest workforce shortages. With an ageing population, post Royal Commission aged care reforms, and dementia rates climbing, demand for specialists who can lead end of life care is rising faster than the training pipeline. If you hold FAChPM, an FRACP with a palliative subspecialty, or you are an experienced GP with palliative scope, locum work in 2026 is genuinely available across hospital consult teams, community services, inpatient units, and aged care. This guide walks through what locum palliative medicine actually pays, where demand sits, and what you need before your first shift.

The State of the Palliative Medicine Workforce

Australia had 358 employed palliative medicine physicians in 2023, with 335 full time equivalents, according to the Australian Institute of Health and Welfare. That works out to 1.3 FTE per 100,000 people. The good news is that the workforce has been expanding, growing 5 per cent year on year between 2013 and 2023. The bad news is that demand has grown even faster, and the geographic spread has not improved.

Most palliative medicine physicians work in metropolitan centres, which leaves regional and rural health services systematically under resourced. The Australasian Chapter of Palliative Medicine has flagged that regional cancer networks, community palliative teams, and rural inpatient units routinely rely on locum cover or GPs with extended scope to keep services running.

The aged care sector is the other force pulling demand upward. Following the Royal Commission into Aged Care Quality and Safety, providers now have explicit obligations around palliative care planning. Industry workforce modelling estimates Australia needs around 110,000 additional direct care workers in aged care by 2036, plus thousands of additional clinical specialists, including palliative physicians who can support residential aged care facilities and home based services.

What Locum Palliative Medicine Pays in 2026

Pay varies more in palliative medicine than in some other specialties because the work spans hospital, community, and aged care settings, each with its own funding model.

Consultant Locum Rates

Hospital based palliative medicine consultants typically earn $1,800 to $2,800 per day for locum cover, with the upper end reached at regional hospitals that need short notice cover or extended on call. Some remote inpatient units in WA, the NT, and far north Queensland advertise $3,000 plus per day with flights and accommodation included.

For context, permanent staff specialist salaries in palliative medicine sit around $186,000 to $229,000 base in most state systems, with senior consultant packages reaching $290,000 to $400,000 once on call, allowances, and private practice rights are included.

Registrar and Advanced Trainee Rates

If you are a final year advanced trainee or a career medical officer with palliative experience, you can pick up registrar level locum work at $130 to $180 per hour, depending on the state and the facility. Inpatient palliative units sometimes need overnight or weekend cover at higher rates, particularly during winter respiratory peaks when aged care transfers spike.

Community and Sessional Rates

Community palliative care teams often pay $200 to $320 per hour for consultant sessions, especially for home visits or after hours phone advice. Aged care providers paying for outreach palliative consultations may negotiate session fees of $400 to $600, depending on travel and complexity. If you are a GP with extended palliative scope, the Medicare Benefits Schedule has dedicated case conferencing and home visit items that materially lift hourly billings on community visits.

Where the Demand Is in 2026

Regional and Rural Australia

Most active locum demand sits outside the capitals. Health services with consistent palliative locum advertising in 2026 include the Hunter New England, Mid North Coast, and Illawarra Shoalhaven networks in NSW, Sunshine Coast and Central Queensland in QLD, the Western Victoria and Loddon Mallee networks in regional Victoria, and the Tasmanian Health Service across all three regions. The Northern Territory has a small but consistent need across Royal Darwin and Alice Springs Hospitals, often with high pay and full travel support.

Hospital Consult Services

Most tertiary and large district hospitals run a palliative care consult service that responds to referrals from oncology, respiratory, geriatrics, ICU, and emergency. These services routinely need locum cover when consultants take leave or roles turn over. Examples of advertising employers in the past six months include Royal Brisbane and Women's Hospital, Sunshine Coast Hospital and Health Service, Caloundra Health Service, and Melbourne Health.

Community Palliative Teams

Community based palliative teams, often run jointly between hospitals and primary health networks, are a steady source of part time and locum sessional work. These roles suit consultants who want predictable hours and minimal on call.

Aged Care Outreach

Outreach into residential aged care facilities is one of the fastest growing slices of palliative work. Some Primary Health Networks now fund dedicated palliative aged care outreach. If you are willing to travel between facilities and document carefully, this is a high impact, financially viable model.

Who Can Take a Locum Palliative Role

Specialist Pathway

The standard pathway is FAChPM, the Fellowship of the Australasian Chapter of Palliative Medicine, awarded by the Royal Australasian College of Physicians. Training requires three years of advanced training, after Basic Physician Training in adult medicine, paediatrics, or after a fellowship in another recognised college such as the RACGP, ACEM, or ANZCA. The annual advanced training fee is around $3,646, with admission to fellowship around $1,344.

International medical graduates with specialist palliative qualifications can apply through AHPRA's specialist pathway. The expedited specialist pathway introduced in 2024 has shortened comparability assessments for graduates of certain UK, Irish, and New Zealand training programs, which has been particularly relevant for palliative medicine because of the close training overlap with the UK.

Non Specialist Pathways

You do not always need FAChPM to do palliative work. GPs with extended scope, particularly those who have completed the RACGP Advanced Skills Training in palliative care or hold the Advanced Diploma in Palliative Medicine, are widely accepted for community palliative care, aged care outreach, and rural inpatient cover. Senior advanced trainees can cover registrar locum gaps. Career medical officers with several years of palliative exposure are also commonly engaged for ward cover.

What You Need to Get Started

Before your first locum shift in palliative medicine, you should have your AHPRA registration current and at the appropriate level for the role. You will need medical indemnity insurance that explicitly covers your palliative scope, including Schedule 8 prescribing and end of life decision making. Most major insurers, including Avant, MIPS, and MIGA, treat palliative locum work as a defined practice category, so you should confirm the cover before accepting a role.

You will also need an ABN if you are working as a contractor, plus the standard hospital credentialing pack: police check within two years, working with children check for the relevant state, vaccination records covering Hep B serology, MMR, varicella, dTpa, COVID, and influenza, and references from your most recent supervisor.

For aged care work, you must hold a current Aged Care Worker Screening Check, which replaced the National Police Certificate for aged care in 2023. This is a separate process from the working with children check and is administered through state worker screening units.

Schedule 8 prescribing is non negotiable in palliative practice. Each state has its own authority. NSW requires authority through the Pharmaceutical Regulatory Unit, Victoria through the Drugs and Poisons Regulation, and so on. If you move between states, you will need separate authorities in each.

The Lifestyle and Skill Considerations

Palliative medicine is emotionally demanding work, but locum patterns suit it well. Hours are often more predictable than acute medicine. On call commitments vary widely between settings, with hospital based work typically heavier than community roles. Many palliative consultants build portfolio careers, mixing public locum work, private outpatient practice, hospice involvement, and teaching.

If you are a senior advanced trainee thinking about subspecialty direction, locum cover is a useful way to test different settings before committing. Hospital consult services give you the broadest case mix. Community work prioritises continuity. Aged care outreach rewards systems thinking and slow medicine. Each has a different rhythm, and the locum market lets you sample all three.

How StatDoctor Can Help

Locum palliative medicine work is fragmented across hospitals, community teams, and aged care providers, and most of it sits in regional Australia where agency markups bite hardest. StatDoctor connects you directly with hospitals and clinics advertising palliative cover, so the rate the facility pays is closer to the rate you actually receive. You can filter by specialty, location, and shift type, manage credentials in one place, and respond to genuine bookings without a recruiter in the middle.

If you are ready to find your next palliative locum shift, download the StatDoctor app and create a profile. The hospitals are already there, and the next regional service that needs your help is probably advertising right now.
fieldData.post-summary: Locum palliative medicine in Australia: $1,800 to $2,800 per day for consultants, ongoing shortages outside metro centres, and how to step into hospital, community, and aged care work in 2026.
