fieldData.name: Locum Palliative Medicine Jobs in Australia: Pay Rates, Demand, and How to Get Started in 2026

fieldData.post-body:
Payroll tax for medical practices has become one of the most contentious issues in Australian healthcare since 2023, and the rules have changed substantially again going into 2026. If you locum through agencies, contract directly with clinics, or run a practice that engages contractor doctors, the way you structure those arrangements can move tens of thousands of dollars in either direction. This guide walks through the current state of payroll tax for medical practices, the bulk billing exemptions that have rolled out across most jurisdictions, and what it means for you as a locum or as a clinic owner.

What Payroll Tax Is and Why Medical Practices Got Caught Up in It

Payroll tax is a state and territory tax on the wages a business pays its workers. Each state sets its own rate and threshold. In 2025-26, the rates and thresholds look roughly like this: New South Wales applies 5.45 per cent above an annual threshold of $1.2 million, Victoria charges 4.85 per cent above $900,000 (with regional rates lower), Queensland charges 4.75 per cent above $1.3 million, South Australia charges up to 4.95 per cent above $1.5 million, Western Australia charges 5.5 per cent above $1 million, and the ACT charges 6.85 per cent above $2 million. Tasmania and the Northern Territory have their own thresholds in the $1.25 million to $1.5 million range.

For most medical practices, that threshold sounds high enough that payroll tax should not apply. The catch is the "relevant contracts" provisions. Across the harmonised states, payments to a contractor can be treated as taxable wages if the contractor mainly supplies their labour and the arrangement falls within the legislative test. Following a string of cases in 2018 and 2019, including Thomas and Naaz, state revenue offices started applying these provisions to medical centres that engaged GPs and other doctors as contractors. Suddenly, a practice that thought it had no payroll tax exposure was looking at audit assessments that ran into hundreds of thousands of dollars.

The Bulk Billing Exemption Wave: State-by-State Payroll Tax Rules in 2026

Between late 2023 and 2025, every mainland state announced an amnesty, an exemption, or both, to take pressure off general practice. The detail matters because the rules are not uniform. If you locum across borders, you need to understand which protections apply where.

Queensland

Queensland is the most generous jurisdiction. Public Ruling PTAQ000.6.5 confirms that, from 1 December 2024, wages paid by a medical practice to a general practitioner are exempt from payroll tax, whether the GP is engaged as a contractor or an employee. There is no bulk billing threshold. The exemption also covers GP registrars. Specialists are not covered by this exemption, so non-GP locum work in Queensland still needs to be structured carefully.

Victoria

Victoria introduced an exemption from 1 July 2025 under the State Taxation Further Amendment Act 2024. The exemption applies to wages paid to contractor and employee GPs in relation to bulk billed consultations only. If your clinic mixes private billing and bulk billing, you must be able to identify the bulk billed component to claim the exemption. Victoria also operated a payroll tax amnesty that ran until 30 June 2024 for practices that had not previously paid payroll tax on contractor GPs.

New South Wales

NSW announced a permanent rebate from 4 September 2024 covering payroll tax on payments to contractor GPs for bulk billed consultations. Practices that meet the bulk billing threshold (80 per cent in metropolitan areas, 70 per cent in regional and rural locations) can also access broader rebates. There was a 12 month amnesty that closed in mid 2024, and Revenue NSW has resumed compliance activity for practices that did not register or take advantage of the rebates.

South Australia

South Australia legislated an exemption that took effect from 1 July 2024. Payments made to GPs in respect of bulk billed services are not subject to payroll tax. The structure is similar to Victoria, in that practices with mixed billing models need clean records of which consultations were bulk billed.

Western Australia

Western Australia takes a different approach because its payroll tax legislation does not include the standard relevant contracts test in the same form as the harmonised states. The WA Department of Finance applies a "totality of the relationship" test. In practice, this means that if a doctor genuinely operates as an independent contractor (with their own ABN, control over their hours, multiple practices, and proper service agreements), payroll tax exposure is lower. WA has not introduced a specific bulk billing exemption because the policy concerns that drove the changes in the eastern states have not played out in the same way locally.

Tasmania, ACT, and the Northern Territory

Tasmania has not announced a specific medical exemption, although the State Revenue Office has signalled it will take a measured approach. The ACT introduced a payroll tax exemption for GPs from 1 July 2025 covering bulk billed consultations, mirroring the Victorian and NSW approach. The Northern Territory has limited audit activity in this space, partly because its revenue office is smaller and the threshold is high relative to the size of most practices.

How Payroll Tax Changes Affect Locum Doctors

You might read all of that and conclude that payroll tax is a problem for clinic owners, not for you. That is partly true. The legal liability sits with the practice. In reality, the cost flows through to locum rates in three ways.

First, when a clinic faces a payroll tax bill of 4 to 6 per cent on contractor payments, the most direct way to manage it is to push that cost into the contractor split. A GP locum who used to negotiate a 65 per cent service fee may have found their effective split reduced once the practice started paying payroll tax. The bulk billing exemptions reverse this for genuine bulk billing work, but only for general practice, and only for consultations that meet the rules.

Second, hospitals and clinics have rewritten their service agreements to make sure contractor relationships are genuinely contractor relationships. That has been good for compliance but it has also pushed more administrative work onto locums. You are more likely now to be asked to provide your own indemnity, manage your own appointments through your own software, and produce evidence that you work for multiple practices.

Third, the changes have shifted the economics of working as a hospital VMO or a clinic owner versus locuming through an agency. If you specialise in something other than general practice, especially in Victoria, NSW, or SA, your service fees are still potentially in scope for payroll tax. Some specialists have responded by setting up their own Pty Ltd companies that engage them under arrangements designed to fall outside the relevant contracts provisions.

Practical Steps If You Locum or Run a Practice

If you locum, the most important thing is to make sure your contractor arrangement looks like a real contractor arrangement. That means using your own ABN, holding your own indemnity insurance through Avant, MIPS, or MIGA, working for more than one practice across the year, and having a service agreement that lets you control how you provide care. The states look at substance over form, but a properly drafted agreement still carries significant weight.

If you are a clinic owner, you should know whether you have registered for payroll tax, whether you are claiming any available rebate or exemption correctly, and whether your billing data can support the claim if audited. A common mistake is to claim the bulk billing exemption based on a whole of practice ratio when the legislation requires consultation level evidence. Several clinics have been pulled up on this in audits during late 2025.

If you cross borders, do not assume what is true in Brisbane is true in Melbourne. A locum GP doing a four week stint in Queensland is covered by the broadest exemption in the country. Move that same locum to Sydney and the rebate only applies to bulk billed consultations and only above the bulk billing threshold. Your invoicing and your service split should reflect that.

Why This Matters for the Locum Workforce

The payroll tax saga has accelerated something that was already happening. Practices want clarity, locums want fair rates, and both sides want less paperwork. The clinics that have come through the changes well are the ones that documented their contractor arrangements properly, paid attention to billing data, and kept their locums informed. The locums who have come through well are the ones who treated their work as a small business from day one, with good records, multiple clients, and proper insurance.

If you are still figuring out how to structure your locum work, talk to a medical accountant who deals with payroll tax regularly. The cost of advice is small compared with the cost of getting the structure wrong over a five year career.

How StatDoctor Can Help

StatDoctor was built to remove the friction between locum doctors and the hospitals and clinics that need them. Because we connect you directly with the facility, your service agreement is with the practice, not with a layered agency. That makes it easier to demonstrate a genuine contractor relationship and keeps more of the headline rate in your pocket. You set your own availability, accept the shifts that work for you, and keep clean records inside the app for tax time.

For practice owners, StatDoctor cuts the agency margin that has been quietly inflating locum costs while you also navigate payroll tax exposure. You see the doctor, you see the rate, and you have a clear paper trail for audit purposes.

Download the StatDoctor app today to see live shifts in your state, post your availability, or fill an urgent gap in your roster. Whether you are a locum looking for your next shift or a clinic looking for cover that does not blow your budget, StatDoctor gives you a cleaner, more transparent way to work.
fieldData.post-summary: Payroll tax rules for medical practices have shifted again in 2026, with bulk billing exemptions now live in Queensland, Victoria, NSW, SA and the ACT. Here is the state-by-state breakdown and what it means for locum doctors and the clinics that engage them.
fieldData.author: 68e360c1b6fe0fae2425baeb
fieldData.name: Payroll Tax for Medical Practices in Australia in 2026: What Locum Doctors and Clinics Need to Know

fieldData.post-body:
Intensive care medicine sits at the centre of modern hospital practice. Australia has 191 ICUs providing 2,378 baseline beds -- roughly 9.3 ICU beds per 100,000 population -- and the workforce that runs them is tightly regulated, highly trained, and consistently under pressure. For locum intensivists, the rates reflect that scarcity. Here is what the ICU locum market looks like in 2026.

The Specialty in Brief

The College of Intensive Care Medicine of Australia and New Zealand (CICM) is the training body for intensive care medicine. FCICM (Fellowship of CICM) is the specialist qualification required to work as a consultant intensivist in Australia. The training pathway is one of the longest in medicine -- six years minimum post-internship, including rotations in ICU, anaesthetics, internal medicine, and paediatrics for those pursuing paediatric ICU.

Australia's 191 ICUs are split roughly 62 per cent public (119) and 38 per cent private (72), with tertiary ICUs concentrated in major metropolitan centres. Demand has grown consistently since COVID, with Medrecruit noting that workforce shortages in regional areas and a renewed focus on critical care infrastructure have kept consultant demand high across both public and private systems.

‍

Pay Rates by Seniority

Consultant Level (FCICM)

According to Medipeople's 2026 agency data, locum ICU consultants earn $2,000 to $3,500 per day. Medrecruit notes consultants can earn up to $3,000 per day, with premiums during peak periods such as winter surge or holidays. Remote and regional placements push rates higher -- current job listings show consultant intensivist roles in remote Australia advertised between $460,000 and $670,000 per year.

Permanent ICU consultants earn between $250,000 and $500,000 per year nationally, with senior roles and subspecialty qualifications (ECMO, trauma, cardiac ICU) at the higher end. Locum work consistently exceeds the permanent annualised equivalent.

Registrar Level

Medipeople lists ICU registrar locum rates at $1,200 to $1,900 per day -- among the highest registrar rates in any specialty. Registrars pursuing dual training with anaesthetics or emergency medicine are particularly sought after because they can flex across departments.

Medlo's 2026 dataset, which tracks hospital shift rates, shows ICU resident medical officers averaging $134 per hour ($1,072 per day) across 338 sampled shifts. These rates sit below the registrar and consultant agency rates above because they reflect junior RMO-level work rather than credentialed registrar shifts.

‍

‍
fieldData.post-summary: Locum ICU consultants in Australia earn $2,000-$3,500 per day. Here is what you need to know about demand, pay, CICM training, and what to expect.
fieldData.author: 68e360c1b6fe0fae2425baeb
fieldData.name: Locum Intensive Care Medicine Jobs in Australia: Pay Rates, Demand, and How to Get Started

fieldData.post-body:
Credentialing is the process that decides whether a hospital will let you work there. It verifies your qualifications, checks your clinical experience, reviews your insurance, and defines exactly what procedures you are approved to perform. For locum doctors, credentialing determines whether you can start a placement on time or spend weeks chasing paperwork. Here is what you need to know to get through the process cleanly.

What Credentialing Actually Is

The Australian Commission on Safety and Quality in Health Care defines credentialing as a process used to verify the qualifications and experience of a clinician to determine their ability to provide safe, high quality health care services. It has two components: credentialing (the verification of your qualifications, training, registration, insurance, and professional history) and scope of clinical practice (the specific procedures and responsibilities you are authorised to perform at a particular facility). A hospital does not just check that you are registered -- it decides exactly what you are allowed to do there.

The Core Documents You Will Need

Based on the Commission's published credentialing checklist, expect to provide: current AHPRA registration certificate (including any conditions); certified copy of your primary medical degree (MBBS or MD); post-graduate qualifications (FRACP, FRACGP, FACEM, FRANZCOG, FRACS); medical indemnity insurance certificate of currency from Avant, MDA National, or MIPS; photo identification for 100-point identity check; current CV; clinical references (typically three, including one from your current or most recent supervisor within the past 12 months); national police check current within three years; Working with Children Check for paediatric roles; CPD evidence demonstrating 50 hours annually; vaccination records (Hepatitis B, MMR, varicella, pertussis, COVID-19); and for procedural specialists, scope of practice documentation including your logbook and procedure list.

How State-Specific Credentialing Works

Credentialing is facility-specific, but most states run it through local health district structures with common elements.

NSW

NSW Health operates a formal Register of Medical Locum Agencies that have passed third-party JAS-ANZ accredited audits. If you go through a registered agency, pre-placement checks are handled for you. If you apply directly, you must complete Document 1 (pre-placement checks covering registration, visa, ID, vaccinations, police check, WWCC), Document 2.1 (Clinical Skills Self-Assessment), Document 2.2 (Referee Check for Clinical Skills), Documents 2.3 to 2.5 (three Referee Checks for Professional Behaviour), and Document 3 (Approval for Secondary Employment if applicable).

Victoria

‍

Under the Victorian Department of Health's credentialing policy, senior medical practitioners cannot work in Victorian health services without current and approved credentials and a defined scope of clinical practice. Each health service must operate a formal credentialing and scope of clinical practice committee. Locum consultants must have their scope of clinical practice defined by the health service according to service capability.

Queensland WA SA NT TAS ACT

Each jurisdiction runs its own credentialing framework, but all align with the Commission's national credentialing standard. Expect the same core document set with jurisdiction-specific forms and vaccination requirements.

Scope of Clinical Practice: The Part Most Locums Underestimate

Registration alone does not authorise you to work in a specific hospital. Scope of clinical practice is facility-specific and is based on three things: your credentials, the hospital's service capability, and the agreed boundaries of what you are allowed to do there. For procedural specialists -- surgeons, anaesthetists, O&G, ED physicians performing procedural sedation -- expect a detailed assessment. A general surgeon credentialed for laparoscopic colorectal surgery at a tertiary hospital may only be credentialed for general open emergency work at a smaller regional facility.

Before accepting a placement, clarify which procedures you are specifically credentialed to perform, what supervision or backup is available, whether there are any equipment or staffing limitations that affect your scope, and your on-call expectations.

Mutual Recognition: Working Across States

If you hold full registration in one Australian state or territory, you can work in all others under the Trans-Tasman Mutual Recognition Agreement and the national medical registration framework administered by AHPRA. Mutual recognition applications can take up to two weeks to process, so submit as soon as you have confirmed a locum position. Mutual recognition covers your AHPRA registration but not hospital credentialing -- you still need to complete credentialing at each new facility.

Medicare Provider Numbers

‍

Separate from credentialing but often required: you need a new Medicare provider number for each new location where you practise and bill Medicare, even for short locum posts. Apply through HPOS (Health Professional Online Services) with your PRODA login.

Tips for Getting Credentialed Faster

Start early -- credentialing typically takes four to six weeks for a new site. Keep a master file of certified copies of your degree, fellowship certificates, AHPRA registration, indemnity insurance, police check, and vaccination records in a digital folder. Brief your referees when to expect reference requests (unresponsive referees are the number one cause of credentialing delays). Maintain CPD consistently -- do not wait until credentialing time to catch up. Respond quickly to queries -- credentialing committees meet on set schedules (often monthly) and missing a committee meeting can push your start date back by weeks.

The Bottom Line

Credentialing is the paperwork gate between you and your next locum placement. It is not optional, and the standards are national. Get your document set in order once, keep it updated, and respond to each new facility's process promptly. If you are ready to find locum shifts across Australia, download the StatDoctor app and connect directly with hospitals.

‍
fieldData.post-summary: What you need to get credentialed at Australian hospitals as a locum doctor: documents, timelines, references, scope of practice, and state-specific requirements.
fieldData.author: 68e360c1b6fe0fae2425baeb
fieldData.name: Hospital Credentialing for Locum Doctors in Australia: The Complete Checklist

fieldData.post-body:
Your invoice is the only document between you and getting paid. Get it right and payments arrive on time. Get it wrong and you are chasing hospital accounts departments for weeks. Locum doctors are sole traders or company directors invoicing for professional services -- this means the ATO has specific rules about what your invoice must contain, what GST status applies, and how records must be kept. Here is exactly how to invoice correctly.

The Two Types of Invoice

Under Australian law, the type of invoice you issue depends on your GST registration status. A tax invoice applies if you are registered for GST -- which you must be if your annual locum income exceeds $75,000. You must issue a tax invoice for every taxable sale over $82.50 including GST. The words "tax invoice" must appear on the document. A regular invoice applies if you are not registered for GST. You must NOT use the words "tax invoice" on these documents. Most full-time locum doctors use tax invoices because GST registration is effectively compulsory once you pass the threshold.

What Your Tax Invoice Must Include

According to business.gov.au and the ATO, every tax invoice must contain: the words "tax invoice", your business name, your Australian Business Number (ABN), the date the invoice is issued, a short description of what you sold including quantity and price, and the amount of GST payable.

For invoices over $1,000, you must also include the buyer's identity or ABN. In practice, a locum invoice will clearly identify the hospital as the buyer, describe the services (for example, "ED consultant locum shifts, Hospital X, 12-16 April 2026"), and show the rate, hours, total before GST, GST amount, and total including GST.

What to Add Beyond the Minimum

The ATO minimum is the floor, not the ceiling. Include these extras to get paid faster: a unique invoice number with sequential numbering (INV-001, INV-002) for tracking; your contact details (postal address, email, phone) so accounts payable can resolve queries; clear payment terms such as net 14 or net 30 days plus your BSB and account number; a purchase order (PO) number if the hospital provided one; and itemised shift detail (date, hours, shift type, rate for each shift).

GST and Locum Work: What You Are Actually Supplying

This is where locum doctors regularly get tripped up. The ATO is specific: when you invoice a hospital or clinic for your services, the supply is generally taxable -- not GST-free. The GST exemption for medical services applies to supplies made to patients -- not to supplies made to third parties such as hospitals or clinics. The ATO's Health Industry Partnership issues register makes this clear.

Practically, this means your locum invoice to a hospital will include 10 per cent GST on your fees. The hospital can claim the input tax credit because it is registered for GST. You remit the GST collected to the ATO when you lodge your quarterly BAS. The exceptions are narrow -- when in doubt, confirm with your accountant.

No ABN No Payment

Under the ATO's no-ABN withholding rules, if you supply services to another business without quoting your ABN on the invoice, the hospital must withhold 47 per cent of the payment and remit it to the ATO. In practice, hospitals will simply refuse to pay invoices without a valid ABN rather than deal with the withholding obligation. Register your ABN through the Australian Business Register before your first locum shift.

Common Mistakes That Delay Payment

Missing ABN -- the no-ABN withholding rules mean hospitals cannot pay you without it. Wrong GST status -- applying "GST-free" to hospital locum work triggers BAS reconciliation issues. Vague service descriptions -- "medical services rendered" is not enough. No purchase order reference -- large hospital accounts teams handle hundreds of invoices weekly. Inconsistent business name -- your ABN, bank account name, and invoice header must all match. Wrong BSB or account number -- hospitals will not chase you to correct it. Using "tax invoice" when not GST-registered -- legally prohibited. Incorrect GST calculation -- 10 per cent of the subtotal, not 11 per cent of the total.

Record-Keeping Obligations

The ATO requires you to keep copies of all invoices issued and received for at least five years. Most locum doctors use Xero, QuickBooks, or MYOB for automated invoice generation and record-keeping. A basic spreadsheet tracker also works if you are meticulous. Your invoices and payment records feed

directly into your quarterly BAS lodgements and your annual tax return.

The Bottom Line

Invoicing is administrative housekeeping, but it is the housekeeping that determines when and how reliably you get paid. Follow the ATO's rules, include everything the business.gov.au guidelines require, add sensible extras like PO numbers and clear payment terms, and you will avoid the common delays that plague new locum doctors. If you are ready to find your next locum shift, download the StatDoctor app and connect directly with hospitals across Australia.

‍
fieldData.post-summary: A guide to invoicing correctly as a locum doctor in Australia. Tax invoice requirements, GST rules, payment terms, and common mistakes that delay payment.
fieldData.author: 68e360c1b6fe0fae2425baeb
fieldData.name: How to Invoice as a Locum Doctor in Australia: Tax Invoices, GST, and Common Mistakes

fieldData.post-body:
Obstetrics and gynaecology is one of the hardest specialties to cover in rural and regional Australia -- and one of the most financially rewarding for locum doctors willing to travel. Maternity units in regional hospitals routinely run short of consultants, registrars are among the most sought-after in the country, and the pay reflects that scarcity. Here is what the O&G locum market actually looks like in 2026.

The Specialty in Brief

The Royal Australian and New Zealand College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists (RANZCOG) is the training body for O&G in Australia. According to the Department of Health's Future Health Workforce data, there were roughly 1,900 accredited O&G specialists with current medical registration in Australia, the majority (94 per cent) working as clinicians.

RANZCOG recognises five subspecialties beyond general O&G training: maternal-fetal medicine, gynaecological oncology, reproductive endocrinology and infertility, urogynaecology, and obstetrical and gynaecological ultrasound. For most locum work, general FRANZCOG is the relevant credential. Sixty-three per cent of O&G specialist clinical FTE is spent in the private sector nationally, but locum demand is strongest in public hospital maternity units, particularly outside major cities.

Pay Rates by Seniority

Consultant Level

FRANZCOG consultant locums are among the highest-paid specialists in Australia. According to current agency listings, rates typically fall between $2,000 and $3,500 per day. Medrecruit lists consultant O&G opportunities up to $3,500 per day. CC Medical's 2025 report identifies O&G as one of the top-earning locum specialties, with the Northern Territory, Far North Queensland, and Tasmania highlighted as the highest-paying regions. On-call and weekend shifts attract premium rates. Permanent O&G specialists earn between $250,000 and $450,000 annually, but locum work at consultant level can significantly exceed this on an annualised basis.

Registrar Level

O&G registrars are consistently described as one of the most hard-to-find registrars by Australian recruiters. Registrar locum rates typically fall between $150 and $220 per hour, or roughly $1,200 to $1,760 per day. Permanent registrar roles average around $132,000 per year.

‍

RMO/HMO Level

Junior doctors rotating through O&G can expect $80 to $150 per hour for locum work, with night shifts and weekends attracting higher rates.

Where the Demand Is

The O&G workforce is highly concentrated in major cities (MMM1 under the Modified Monash Model). Specific drivers of current locum demand include: rural maternity unit cover (many regional hospitals cannot recruit permanent O&G consultants and rely on locum coverage to keep services running); retirement of senior specialists approaching retirement age (amplifying workforce pressures according to RANZCOG); growing service complexity (rising maternal age, increasing comorbidities, more complex pregnancies); and private practice flexibility (consultants supplementing private practice with on-call public work). The Northern Territory, Far North Queensland, and Tasmania are perennial hotspots.

Qualifications and Pathways

To work as a locum O&G consultant in Australia, you need FRANZCOG or an equivalent specialist qualification recognised by the Medical Board of Australia. International specialist medical graduates apply through RANZCOG under the Specialist Pathway. UK-trained O&G specialists with CCT and full MRCOG can apply through the Expedited Specialist Pathway. Specialists from other countries follow the standard Specialist Pathway, involving RANZCOG assessment of qualifications, training, and experience, often with a supervised practice period.

For registrar-level locum work, you need current AHPRA registration with no restrictions and demonstrable O&G experience. Most registrar locum roles expect you to be on or have completed a RANZCOG-accredited training program, or equivalent international experience.

What to Expect on an O&G Locum Placement

On-call intensity -- obstetric cover is 24/7. Expect frequent after-hours calls, especially in smaller centres. Theatre and delivery load -- a typical rural consultant placement includes elective gynaecology lists, obstetric ward rounds, emergency caesareans, and outpatient clinics. Credentialing -- hospitals require detailed scope-of-practice documentation including your RANZCOG logbook and recent references. Allow four to six weeks for credentialing at a new site. Support services -- confirm on-site anaesthetic cover, paediatric backup, and theatre nursing before accepting a rural placement.

The Bottom Line

O&G is one of the highest-paying and most in-demand locum specialties in Australia. The combination of workforce concentration in capital cities, persistent rural shortages, and the intensity of obstetric on-call work means experienced O&G locums can command premium rates and choose placements that suit their lifestyle. If you are ready to find O&G locum shifts across Australia, download the StatDoctor app and connect directly with hospitals -- no agency fees, no middlemen.
fieldData.post-summary: Locum O&G consultants in Australia earn $2,000-$3,500 per day. Here is what you need to know about subspecialties, rural demand, and RANZCOG pathways.
fieldData.author: 68e360c1b6fe0fae2425baeb
fieldData.name: Locum Obstetrics and Gynaecology Jobs in Australia: Pay Rates, Subspecialties, and What You Need

fieldData.post-body:
India is the single largest source of international medical graduates practising in Australia. According to a 2022 study published in the Journal of Migration and Health, 21.3 per cent of IMGs in Australia trained in India -- more than any other country, including the United Kingdom (19.1 per cent) and South Africa (8.2 per cent). If you are an Indian-trained doctor considering the move, the pathway is well-established but demanding. Here is exactly what it involves.

‍

Is Your Indian MBBS Recognised in Australia?

Not directly. An Indian MBBS degree -- whether from an NMC (National Medical Commission, formerly MCI) recognised institution -- is not automatically accepted for medical registration in Australia. This applies to medical degrees from most countries outside Australia, New Zealand, the UK, Ireland, the USA, and Canada. Instead, you must demonstrate equivalence to Australian medical standards by passing the Australian Medical Council (AMC) examinations.

The AMC Standard Pathway

The Standard Pathway is the main route for Indian doctors. It consists of four steps:

Step 1: AMC Portfolio Submission ($642)

Submit your qualifications to the AMC for verification. You provide certified copies of your MBBS degree, transcripts, and internship completion certificate alongside identity verification. Processing takes approximately four to eight weeks.

Step 2: AMC MCQ Examination ($2,920)

The AMC MCQ is a Computer Adaptive Test with 150 multiple-choice questions over 3.5 hours. It is available at Pearson VUE test centres in Delhi, Mumbai, Bengaluru, Chennai, Hyderabad, and Kolkata. The first-attempt pass rate is approximately 50 per cent. A pass is valid for three years.

Step 3: AMC Clinical OSCE ($3,000-$3,400)

The Clinical OSCE has a first-attempt pass rate of just 21 to 24 per cent. It consists of 16 assessed stations plus four rest stations. The pass mark is nine out of 14 scored stations. The exam is held in Melbourne (in-person: $3,000) or online ($3,400).

Step 4: AHPRA Registration ($1,058/year)

Once you pass both exams, you apply to AHPRA for registration. Most Indian MBBS graduates initially receive provisional registration and complete 12 months of supervised practice before progressing to general registration.

The Specialist Pathway

If you hold an Indian postgraduate qualification (MD, MS, or DNB), you may be eligible for the Specialist Pathway via assessment by the relevant Australian specialist college. This can be faster than the Standard Pathway if you receive favourable assessment.

Visa Options

Medical practitioners are on Australia's skilled occupation list. Key visa options include Subclass 482 (employer-sponsored, up to 4 years), Subclass 189 (skilled independent, permanent), Subclass 190 (state-nominated, permanent), and Subclass 491 (regional, 5-year provisional with PR pathway). Regional work fast-tracks permanent residency.

Costs and Timeline

The minimum cost assuming first-attempt passes is approximately $10,000 to $13,000. The typical timeline from starting the process to practising independently is 15 to 27 months. Multiple exam attempts add significantly to both cost and time.

What Locum Work Looks Like After Registration

Once you hold general registration, you can work as a locum across Australia. Starting rates range from $140 per hour (RMO level) to $229 per hour (consultant level), with rural placements paying significantly more. You will need an ABN, GST registration, and professional indemnity insurance.

The Bottom Line

The pathway from India to practising medicine in Australia is demanding but absolutely achievable. India's position as the largest IMG source country means the pathway is well-trodden, with strong support networks and hospitals familiar with Indian training standards. Download the StatDoctor app and connect directly with hospitals across Australia.
fieldData.post-summary: Indian doctors are the largest group of IMGs in Australia. Here is the complete pathway: AMC exams, AHPRA registration, visa options, costs, and timeline.
fieldData.author: 68e360c1b6fe0fae2425baeb
fieldData.name: Moving from India to Australia as a Doctor: AMC Exams, Registration, and Locum Work

fieldData.post-body:
Working as a locum doctor in rural Australia already pays well -- rates are consistently higher than metro, and most placements include flights, accommodation, and a vehicle. But on top of the locum rate itself, there are government-funded incentive programs specifically designed to attract and retain doctors in regional, rural, and remote areas. Many locum doctors do not know these programs exist or assume they are only for permanent staff. Here is a breakdown of every major incentive available in 2026.

Understanding the Modified Monash Model

Before diving into the programs, you need to understand the Modified Monash Model (MMM). This is the classification system the Australian Government uses to define how rural a location is, and it determines your eligibility for most incentives. MM 1 covers metropolitan areas (Sydney, Melbourne, Brisbane, Perth, Adelaide). MM 2 covers regional centres. MM 3 covers large rural towns. MM 4 covers medium rural towns. MM 5 covers small rural towns. MM 6 covers remote communities. MM 7 covers very remote communities. Most incentive programs kick in at MM 3 and increase with remoteness. You can check any location's classification using the Health Workforce Locator on the Department of Health website.

Workforce Incentive Program (WIP) -- Doctor Stream

The WIP Doctor Stream is the primary government incentive for GPs working in rural areas. It has two payment components. The Central Payment System (CPS) is automatic and linked to your Medicare billing data. If you are billing Medicare in MM 3-7 locations, the system assesses your claims and pays accordingly. Payments range from $3,600 to $60,000 per year depending on your clinical activity, the remoteness of your location, your length of participation, and your vocational registration status (FRACGP or FACRRM). The Flexible Payment System (FPS) covers work not captured by Medicare claims and is managed by your state's rural workforce agency. The system heavily favours more remote regions. MM 7 work attracts the highest payments. You must register your bank details in your Health Professional Online Services (HPOS) account to receive payments.

WIP -- Rural Advanced Skills Stream

This stream provides additional payments for GPs who deliver emergency medicine or advanced procedural services in rural areas. Stream 1 (Emergency Medicine) pays up to $10,500 per year for doctors providing emergency care in MM 3-7 locations. Stream 2 (Advanced Skills) pays up to $10,500 per year for doctors holding recognised advanced skills qualifications. You can claim both streams simultaneously, potentially adding up to $21,000 per year on top of your locum income. The Rural Procedural Grants Program (RPGP) offers additional support. Emergency services GPs can claim up to $6,000 per financial year for training costs, while procedural GPs can claim up to $20,000. Locums must complete a minimum of 28 days of work in MM 3-7 locations per financial year to qualify.

Rural Locum Assistance Program (Rural LAP)

The Rural LAP is specifically designed for locum placements. The program is free to both the hosting practice and the locum. Rural LAP covers all travel and accommodation costs, a $150 per working day incentive payment on top of your locum wage, and a $100 per day travel and meal allowance. The hosting organisation only pays your base locum wage. Placements run up to 14 working days for MM 2-7 locations and up to 28 working days for remote placements (MM 6-7). A two-week rural locum placement through Rural LAP means an extra $2,100 in incentive payments plus $1,400 in travel and meal allowances -- $3,500 on top of your locum rate, with flights and accommodation covered separately.

Bulk Billing Incentives

If you are a GP doing locum work that involves Medicare billing, the expanded bulk billing incentives that took effect from November 2025 are worth understanding. GPs now receive a bulk billing incentive for all Medicare-eligible patients. For a standard level B consultation, the incentive ranges from $21.85 in metropolitan areas to $42.05 in very remote areas -- on top of the standard Medicare rebate. Additionally, practices that bulk bill all patients receive an extra 12.5 per cent quarterly payment through the Bulk Billing Practice Incentive Program, split 50/50 between the GP and the practice.

Other Programs Worth Knowing

The HELP for Rural Doctors program reduces your HECS-HELP debt if you work in rural or remote areas -- effectively increasing take-home pay. If you are an international medical graduate, working in a Distribution Priority Area or District of Workforce Shortage -- which most rural areas are -- can reduce your moratorium period. The more remote the location, the greater the reduction.

Stacking the Incentives

The real value comes from combining multiple programs. A locum GP working in an MM 5-7 location could potentially access: higher locum rates ($2,000-$4,000+ per day versus $1,200-$1,800 metro), WIP Doctor Stream (up to $60,000/year), WIP Rural Advanced Skills (up to $21,000/year), Rural LAP incentive ($150/day plus $100/day allowance), enhanced bulk billing incentives (up to $42.05 per consultation), RPGP training grants (up to $20,000/year for procedural GPs), HELP debt reduction, and flights, accommodation, and vehicle provided. Not all of these will apply to every locum placement, but the financial package for rural locum work extends well beyond the headline daily rate.

The Bottom Line

The Australian Government invests heavily in attracting doctors to rural areas, and the incentive landscape is more generous than most locums realise. If you are already earning premium rates for rural locum work, these programs add thousands more per year in direct payments, training grants, and debt reduction. If you are ready to explore rural locum opportunities across Australia, download the StatDoctor app and connect directly with hospitals -- no agency fees, no middlemen.
fieldData.post-summary: Rural locum doctors in Australia can access government incentives worth thousands per year. Here is every program, payment, and bonus available in 2026.
fieldData.author: 68e360c1b6fe0fae2425baeb
fieldData.name: Rural Health Incentives for Locum Doctors in Australia: Every Payment You Can Access

fieldData.post-body:
Surgery is one of the most diverse and highest-paying fields for locum work in Australia. Whether you are a general surgery registrar picking up extra shifts or a consultant orthopaedic surgeon covering a rural hospital for a week, there is consistent demand across sub-specialties and locations. Here is what the market looks like and what you need to work as a locum surgeon.

The Surgical Sub-Specialties

The Royal Australasian College of Surgeons (RACS) recognises nine surgical specialties, and locum demand exists across all of them:

General Surgery -- the broadest and most in-demand, covering emergency and elective abdominal, breast, endocrine, and trauma surgery. Orthopaedic Surgery -- high demand in both metro and rural settings, driven by elective joint replacements and trauma. Urology -- strong demand particularly in regional hospitals without permanent urologists. Plastics and Reconstructive Surgery -- locum cover needed for burns units, trauma, and elective lists. Cardiothoracic Surgery -- niche but well-paying, primarily in tertiary centres. Neurosurgery -- limited locum availability due to small workforce and highly specialised infrastructure. ENT (Otolaryngology Head and Neck Surgery) -- consistent demand especially for paediatric and regional ENT cover. Vascular Surgery -- smaller workforce, strong demand in regional areas. Paediatric Surgery -- specialised, with locum cover mainly in children's hospitals.

General surgery and orthopaedics dominate the locum market by volume. These two sub-specialties account for the bulk of both registrar and consultant locum shifts available at any given time.

Pay Rates by Seniority

Based on Medlo's 2026 dataset covering over 49,000 locum shifts and agency listing data:

Registrar Level

Surgery registrars average $163 per hour ($1,304 per day) based on 2,989 sampled shifts. That places surgery in the mid-range for registrar specialties -- above paediatrics ($161/hr) but below emergency medicine ($196/hr) and O&G ($176/hr). Night shifts attract a significant premium. The average registrar night rate is $192 per hour versus $166 for day shifts across all specialties.

Consultant Level

Consultant surgeons (FRACS) average $177 per hour ($1,416 per day) in the Medlo hospital shift dataset, though this figure understates the full picture. Many consultant locum placements are structured as daily rates negotiated directly or through agencies, particularly for operating lists. According to current agency listings, consultant locum surgeon rates typically fall between $2,000 and $3,500 per day, with rural and remote placements reaching $4,000 or more.

RMO/HMO Level

Junior surgical RMOs average $133 per hour ($1,064 per day) -- lower than emergency medicine ($151/hr) and anaesthetics ($151/hr) at the same seniority level, but still competitive compared to non-procedural specialties.

Where the Demand Is
fieldData.post-summary: Locum surgery jobs in Australia pay $1,200-$3,500+ per day depending on seniority and sub-specialty. Here is what you need to know about demand, pay, and FRACS requirements.
fieldData.author: 68e360c1b6fe0fae2425baeb
fieldData.name: Locum Surgery Jobs in Australia: Sub-Specialties, Pay Rates, and How to Get Started

fieldData.post-body:
Urology is one of the highest-paying specialties in Australian locum medicine, with daily rates that regularly sit between $2,500 and $4,000 and top-tier remote placements pushing higher. Demand is structural rather than cyclical. An ageing population, a small training pipeline, and a concentration of urologists in metro centres have combined to create persistent gaps in regional hospitals and high-volume metro theatre lists. If you hold FRACS with urology specialty recognition, or you are a senior overseas-trained urologist moving through the Specialist Pathway, locum urology jobs in 2026 offer some of the strongest earnings and flexibility available in surgical medicine.

Why Locum Urology Demand Stays High

The maths is not complicated. Australia has roughly one practising urologist per 20,000 people, and the distribution is heavily skewed toward capital cities. In Queensland, for example, 81.7 percent of urologists practise in major metropolitan areas and only 18.3 percent are based in regional centres. Tasmania, the Northern Territory, and regional WA regularly advertise urology positions that remain vacant for months.

Three demographic and workforce pressures are making that gap wider:

  • By 2026, more than 22 percent of Australians will be aged over 65, up from about 16 percent in 2020. Conditions like benign prostatic hyperplasia, prostate cancer, urinary incontinence, and kidney stones become more prevalent with age, and each of those generates ongoing demand for urological assessment, cystoscopy, TURP, and stone work.
  • Around 42 percent of the 2023 urology workforce indicated an intention to retire within a decade, according to specialty workforce data. That creates a steady exit that new trainees cannot fully replace.
  • USANZ appoints only around 20 SET trainees nationally each year through the selection process run jointly with RACS. The training program runs six years after prevocational work, meaning the pipeline simply cannot expand quickly.

The result is straightforward. When a regional hospital loses its sole staff urologist, the only option for maintaining an elective list, a cystoscopy clinic, or a stone service is to fly in a locum. For locum urologists with the flexibility to travel, this is an opportunity.

What Locum Urologists Actually Earn

Locum urology pay sits at the top of the specialty ladder, alongside anaesthetics and emergency medicine. Based on 2026 agency listings and direct platform data:

  • Metro locum urology day rates typically run from $2,500 to $3,500 per day for consultant-level work.
  • Rural and regional placements regularly pay $3,500 to $4,000 per day, with travel, accommodation, and car hire included.
  • Remote and hard to fill positions in the Pilbara, Far North Queensland, and the Top End can exceed $4,000 per day, and some urgent cover bookings attract premium on-call loadings on top of the base rate.
  • Staff specialist urologists in the public system earn an average base of around $417,524 per year once allowances, on-call, and procedural supplements are included. Senior consultants in private practice can bill well above that, but most private income is fee for service rather than a fixed locum day rate.

A simple comparison makes the locum premium clear. A locum urologist working four days a week at $3,500 per day over 46 clinical weeks grosses $644,000. That is before you factor in on-call, travel allowances, and fees for after-hours procedural work. The trade off, as with any contractor arrangement, is that you manage your own leave, super, tax, and indemnity.

Metro vs Regional Rates

Metro lists tend to be well organised, with efficient theatre turnover, nursing teams that know the procedural flow, and reliable instrument sets. You will often do more TURPs, cystoscopies, and stone work per session in metro settings. Regional lists are less predictable. You may see a wider case mix, manage acute retention admissions overnight, and cover general urology calls that would normally be subspecialised in the city. The financial premium reflects this scope.

Who Can Work as a Locum Urologist in Australia?

There are two realistic pathways into locum urology in 2026.

FRACS (Urol) and Specialist Registration

Australian trained urologists hold Fellowship of the Royal Australasian College of Surgeons in Urology after completing the USANZ SET program. With specialist registration from AHPRA, you can take locum work in public and private hospitals across every state and territory, and you do not need additional assessment beyond individual hospital credentialing.

Specialist Pathway for Overseas Trained Urologists

If you trained overseas, you apply through the Medical Board of Australia Specialist Pathway, which USANZ assesses on behalf of AHPRA. There are two relevant streams. The first is the Expedited Specialist Pathway, which was expanded in 2024 and 2025 for comparably trained specialists from approved jurisdictions such as the United Kingdom, Ireland, New Zealand, and selected United States programs. The second is the standard substantial equivalence or partially comparable assessment, which may require a period of supervised practice and a performance assessment before unconditional specialist registration is granted.

During a period of supervised practice, you can often work in a urology registrar or service registrar capacity while the assessment progresses. Locum work at consultant rates generally requires full specialist registration with no conditions that limit your scope.

Where the Best Locum Urology Jobs Are in 2026

Regional New South Wales and Queensland

Centres like Tamworth, Dubbo, Orange, Wagga Wagga, Bundaberg, Mackay, Rockhampton, and Townsville regularly cycle through locum urology cover. These hospitals usually have one or two staff urologists and rely on visiting or locum urologists to cover elective lists during leave, parental leave, or between permanent appointments.

Tasmania and the Northern Territory

Both jurisdictions have small permanent urology footprints and a long running dependence on locum and visiting urologists. Royal Hobart Hospital, Launceston General Hospital, and Royal Darwin Hospital all advertise recurring locum blocks. Rates here are among the highest in the country, driven by flight time and relative remoteness.

Rural Western Australia and Regional South Australia

The Kimberley, Pilbara, and Goldfields regions in WA, and centres like Port Augusta, Whyalla, and Mount Gambier in SA, face chronic urology workforce pressure. Placements typically include flights, accommodation, and a car, with daily rates at the top of the national range.

Private Hospital Cover

Private urology locum work is usually coordinated through individual urologists covering each other rather than traditional agency placements. If you build a reputation with a metro private group, you can pick up operating lists and consulting sessions on a structured schedule. This is a quieter market than public locum work, but it can be a reliable top up for a semi retired consultant or a urologist winding down toward full retirement.

Getting Set Up as a Locum Urologist

  1. AHPRA specialist registration as a urologist, with no conditions or undertakings that would limit your scope of practice.
  2. FRACS or equivalent qualifications, plus current USANZ membership if you want access to specialty updates, MOPS, and networking.
  3. An ABN if you are working as a contractor, which is the default arrangement for almost all locum urology work.
  4. Medical indemnity insurance that covers elective and emergency urology at the sites you will work, including cystoscopy, TURP, laparoscopic and open upper tract surgery, and any robotic work you perform. Check carefully that rural and locum work is within your declared scope.
  5. Up to date CPD through the RACS MOPS framework, which is the accepted CPD program for urologists.
  6. Standard credentialing documents: current CV, national police check, Working with Children Check where required, evidence of vaccinations, two recent referees, logbook summaries, and recent procedural audit data.
  7. A current driver licence is a practical must for regional placements, and photo ID is useful for short notice interstate travel.

The Lifestyle Trade Off

Locum urology pays well because it solves a real problem for hospitals. That means you are often parachuted in with little handover, a half known theatre team, and a list of patients you have not met. You need to be comfortable making procedural decisions quickly, managing post operative complications without your usual network, and understanding unfamiliar governance processes.

Rural work compounds this. A solo urologist in a regional hospital may be the only person in town qualified to manage an obstructed infected kidney at 2 am. If that prospect energises rather than worries you, locum urology can be one of the most intellectually and financially rewarding ways to practise. If it does not suit your life stage, metro private and public cover lists offer a lower intensity alternative.

Many urologists use locum work as a bridge, a way to earn strongly while deciding between fellowships, between permanent appointments, or across a move interstate. Others make it a permanent model and build a lifestyle around four day on, three day off placements with long holidays at home.

How StatDoctor Can Help

StatDoctor connects locum doctors, including urologists, directly with hospitals and practices across Australia without traditional agency fees sitting between you and the work. That means you see the full rate on offer, you deal directly with the hiring facility, and you keep control of your availability and scope. Hospitals benefit from filling urology gaps faster and at a lower total cost, which in turn means they can afford to pay a stronger rate to the locum.

If you are a urologist looking for your next locum block, or a hospital trying to cover an elective list or maintain a stone service, download the StatDoctor app to see current urology opportunities and connect directly with facilities that need cover right now.
fieldData.post-summary: Locum urology jobs in Australia pay $2,500 to $4,000 plus per day. An ageing population, a tight USANZ SET training pipeline, and chronic regional shortages keep demand strong. Here is what urologists need to know about rates, pathways, and where to find work in 2026.
fieldData.author: 68e360c1b6fe0fae2425baeb
fieldData.name: Locum Urology Jobs in Australia: Pay Rates, Demand, and How to Get Started in 2026

fieldData.post-body:
Australia's largest reform to GP funding in a generation went live on 1 November 2025, and the first payments hit practice bank accounts in January 2026. The Bulk Billing Practice Incentive Program (BBPIP) pays an extra 12.5 percent on top of every Medicare benefit billed for an eligible bulk billed service, with the cash split evenly between the practice and the doctor who delivered the care. If you locum in general practice, this changes the maths on every shift you take, and there are a handful of rules you need to follow to make sure you actually get paid.

What the BBPIP actually is

The BBPIP sits alongside the existing Bulk Billing Incentive items (the per-consultation top-ups that GPs have been claiming for years). It is a separate quarterly payment from Services Australia, designed to reward practices that commit to bulk billing every Medicare-eligible patient, not just concession card holders, children under 16, and DVA cardholders.

Here is the headline structure:

  • The incentive is calculated at 12.5 percent of the MBS benefits paid for all eligible bulk billed services delivered during the quarter.
  • The payment is split 50/50 between the practice and the provider who rendered the service.
  • Payments are made quarterly, in arrears.
  • The first BBPIP cycle ran for two months (November and December 2025) and paid out in January 2026. The standard quarterly cycle then resumed.

The program is the practical mechanism for the federal government's commitment to lift bulk billing rates back up toward 90 percent by 2030, and it is paired with the expanded Bulk Billing Incentive items that, from 1 November 2025, became available for every Medicare-eligible patient at participating practices.

Why this matters for locum GPs

If you do any locum work in general practice, the BBPIP can add several thousand dollars per quarter to your billings, but only if the practice you are working at is registered, and only if you are correctly linked to that practice in the Organisation Register.

Think of it as a hidden line item that should appear on top of your usual percentage of billings. If your contract pays you 65 percent of billings, the BBPIP component is paid separately and should flow to you on top, with the practice retaining its 50 percent share. Any locum agreement that quietly absorbs the BBPIP into the practice cut is leaving real money on the table, so the contract conversation is worth having before you sign on.

The other significant change for locums is the patient mix. Practices that opt in must bulk bill every Medicare-eligible patient that you see during your shifts. Mixed billing is incompatible with BBPIP. If a practice is participating, you are bulk billing for the day, full stop.

Who is eligible to participate

The eligibility rules cover both the practice and the individual provider, and both layers need to be in place for the payments to flow.

Practice level requirements

For a practice to claim BBPIP it must:

  • Be registered for MyMedicare (the patient registration system that came in with the 2023 Strengthening Medicare reforms).
  • Have added BBPIP as a program in the Organisation Register on Health Professional Online Services (HPOS).
  • Bulk bill every Medicare-eligible patient for in-scope MBS items during the relevant quarter.
  • Maintain accurate provider links so that every locum and registrar working there is correctly attached.

A practice can opt in at the start of any quarter, but it has to commit to bulk billing across the whole period. If it bills any in-scope item privately during the quarter, the practice loses the BBPIP entitlement for that quarter.

Provider level requirements

You qualify as an eligible provider if you are:

  • A vocationally registered (VR) general practitioner.
  • A non-vocationally registered (non-VR) GP.
  • A GP registrar on an approved training pathway.

You also need a current Medicare Provider Number that is specific to the participating practice location, and you need to be linked to that practice in the Organisation Register before services are claimed under the BBPIP. If your provider number was set up for a previous location, you will need to apply for a new one each time you start at a new BBPIP-registered practice. Services Australia typically turns these around within two weeks if you submit clean paperwork, but the lead time is something to factor into the start date you negotiate.

How the money actually flows

The 12.5 percent is calculated on the MBS benefit paid, not on the patient's out of pocket fee (there isn't one, because the service was bulk billed). So if you bill a Level B Item 23 consultation, the MBS benefit at the standard rate generates a BBPIP component on top.

A worked example helps here. Imagine you do a busy 10 hour Saturday locum shift in a metropolitan practice and bill 60 Level B consultations and 10 Level C consultations across the day. Roughly speaking, that's somewhere around $3,000 in MBS benefits for the consults alone, before any chronic disease items, mental health items, or care plan claims. The BBPIP component on that day is approximately 12.5 percent of $3,000, which is $375. Of that, your share is around $187.50, paid in the next quarterly cycle.

Stretch that across a full quarter of locum days and the contribution becomes meaningful. For a locum working two or three days a week in a participating practice, the BBPIP can comfortably add $1,500 to $3,000 per quarter on top of your usual takings.

How geography changes the numbers

The BBPIP works in tandem with the existing Bulk Billing Incentive items, which are scaled by Modified Monash Model (MMM) classification. As you move further from the capital cities, both the per-consult bulk billing incentive and the BBPIP tier multiplier go up. The practical result is that rural and remote locum days are now significantly more lucrative under bulk billing than they used to be.

The current MMM scaling for the Bulk Billing Practice Incentive component is roughly:

  • MMM2 (regional centres): 150 percent of the MMM1 baseline
  • MMM3 to MMM4 (large and medium rural towns): 160 percent
  • MMM5 (small rural towns): 170 percent
  • MMM6 (remote communities): 180 percent
  • MMM7 (very remote communities): 190 percent

When you stack that against the headline per-consult Bulk Billing Incentive, which lifts from around $20.65 in metropolitan areas to around $39.65 in MMM7, a single bulk billed standard consult in a very remote town can now generate close to double the incentive payments that the same consult would attract in inner Sydney or inner Melbourne. For locums willing to take rural work, the BBPIP has materially improved the case.

What to check before you sign your next locum contract

The BBPIP is straightforward in theory, but the way it interacts with locum contracts and provider numbers is where the friction shows up. Before you start a shift at a new practice, walk through this short checklist.

Confirm the practice is registered

Ask the practice manager to confirm that the practice is registered for MyMedicare and has BBPIP added in the Organisation Register. If you are agreeing to bulk bill every patient on the assumption that you will share in the BBPIP, you want that assumption verified in writing before the shift starts.

Get your provider number sorted early

If this is a new location for you, lodge your Medicare Provider Number application as soon as you have a confirmed start date. You cannot retrospectively claim BBPIP payments for services billed under the wrong provider number, so a delay here is real lost income.

Read the BBPIP clause in your contract

Locum agreements vary widely in how they handle the BBPIP component. Some treat it as standard billings and apply the usual percentage split. Others recognise that the BBPIP is already split 50/50 by the program rules and pay it through directly to the doctor. A small number of contracts try to retain the full amount within the practice. Make sure the agreement is explicit, and if it is silent, ask the question.

Understand the bulk billing requirement

You cannot privately bill a patient on a BBPIP day. If a patient walks in expecting to pay a gap and you are at a participating practice, the practice loses BBPIP eligibility for the entire quarter if you take a private fee. Most participating practices will have clear front desk processes for this, but it is worth confirming so you are not put in an awkward spot mid-consult.

Track your billings

Quarterly payments arrive in arrears, so reconciliation is on you. A short spreadsheet noting your bulk billed services per practice per day will let you cross-check the BBPIP component you are paid against what you delivered. Most practices use practice management software that can generate this report, so request it as part of your handover.

How the BBPIP changes the locum market

The combined effect of the expanded bulk billing incentive items and the BBPIP is a meaningful shift in the economics of GP locum work. Practices that historically ran a mixed billing model are reassessing the maths, particularly in regional and rural locations where the MMM scaling makes full bulk billing almost as profitable as a small gap fee. Many of these practices are now actively looking for locums who are comfortable with a bulk billing model, and rates for short term cover are firming up in those areas.

Two trends are worth watching. Expect more practices to opt in as the cash flow from the first January 2026 payment becomes visible across the sector, and expect locum demand to tilt toward MMM2 to MMM7 locations where the combined incentive structure is most generous. For non-VR GPs and registrars in particular, the BBPIP is a useful door into well paying locum work, because the same payment structure applies regardless of vocational registration status.

How StatDoctor Can Help

StatDoctor is built for locum doctors who want to spend less time chasing agencies and more time choosing the work that actually suits them. Every GP locum shift listed on the platform is direct from the practice or hospital, with the rate, location, and shift details visible upfront. No middleman, no vague descriptions, and no surprise BBPIP clauses buried in agency paperwork.

If you want to start picking up locum work at practices that are participating in the Bulk Billing Practice Incentive Program, download the StatDoctor app for iOS or Android, complete your profile, and you will see eligible shifts in your area within minutes. Practice managers can list a shift, specify the bulk billing arrangement, and have a credentialled locum confirmed without going through a recruiter.

Visit statdoctor.app to download the app or list a shift, and join the locums and practices getting paid faster, fairer, and direct.
fieldData.post-summary: The BBPIP launched on 1 November 2025 and paid out for the first time in January 2026, adding 12.5 percent on top of Medicare benefits for every bulk billed service at participating practices. Here is what locum GPs need to know about eligibility, provider numbers, contract clauses, and how the Modified Monash Model tiers change the maths from metro to remote.
fieldData.author: 68e360c1b6fe0fae2425baeb
fieldData.name: The Bulk Billing Practice Incentive Program (BBPIP): What Locum GPs in Australia Need to Know in 2026

fieldData.post-body:
Locum dermatology jobs in Australia have never been more in demand, and for good reason. With fewer than 40 dermatologists practising across the entire rural and regional parts of the country, a workforce where half the practitioners are aged 60 or older, and a skin cancer burden that sees two in three Australians diagnosed by the age of 70, the gap between patient need and available specialists is growing every year. If you hold Fellowship of the Australasian College of Dermatologists (FACD) and are looking for flexible, well-paid work, 2026 may be the best year yet to explore what locum dermatology can offer you.

The State of Dermatology in Australia: Why Demand Is So High

Australia has one of the highest rates of skin cancer in the world, driven by high UV exposure, an outdoor-heavy culture, and an ageing population with cumulative sun exposure over decades. Melanoma remains one of the most commonly diagnosed cancers in the country, and demand for dermatological assessment, procedural work, and ongoing patient management continues to outstrip supply.

The Australasian College of Dermatologists (ACD) currently trains just 20 to 25 new dermatologists each year, a figure that has remained relatively static while the population ages and skin cancer rates climb. The total registered workforce sits at roughly 620 dermatologists nationally, of whom approximately 94 per cent are concentrated in metropolitan areas. Fewer than 40 dermatologists practice in rural, regional, and remote regions combined, leaving enormous coverage gaps in communities where skin cancer rates are often highest.

Compounding this, around half the existing workforce is aged 60 years or above, meaning a substantial wave of retirements is expected in the coming decade. The ACD has been vocal about this looming crisis, noting that without structural changes to training capacity and distribution, demand will far outpace supply. For locum dermatologists willing to travel, this shortage translates directly into consistent work, premium pay, and genuine clinical impact.

Locum Dermatology Pay Rates in Australia in 2026

Dermatology is among the higher-paying medical specialties in the locum market. As a consultant-level specialist working as a locum, you can generally expect rates in the range of $2,000 to $3,500 per day, depending on the nature of the role, the location, and the urgency of the booking. Some roles, particularly in remote areas or when filled on short notice, can exceed this range significantly.

Metropolitan Rates

In Sydney, Melbourne, Brisbane, and Perth, locum dermatology roles at public hospitals typically sit in the $2,000 to $2,500 per day range. Private clinic work can vary widely depending on the billing arrangement, with some billings-based models offering 65 to 70 per cent of fee income collected, which at full clinic capacity can exceed the hospital day rate. The trade-off is usually a more admin-intensive environment and less predictable volume.

Rural and Remote Rates

The most financially rewarding locum dermatology roles tend to be in rural, regional, and remote Australia. Healthcare facilities in these areas, which often have no resident dermatologist at all, regularly offer rates 30 to 50 per cent above metropolitan equivalents. Day rates of $3,000 to $4,500 are not uncommon in more remote settings, and accommodation, travel, and meals are typically covered on top of your daily fee. Queensland Health, WA Health, and NT Health all run specialist visiting medical officer programs that include structured locum dermatology placements, many of which are booked months in advance.

What Work Does a Locum Dermatologist Actually Do?

The scope of a locum dermatology role varies depending on the setting. In a hospital outpatient department, you will typically see a mix of general dermatology referrals, procedural work such as biopsies and excisions, inpatient consults from wards managing complex skin conditions, and follow-up care. In rural visiting roles, the caseload is often heavily skewed toward skin cancer assessment, with high volumes of patients who have had limited previous access to specialist review.

Teledermatology has grown considerably over the past few years, and a number of services now offer hybrid models where a dermatologist reviews cases remotely and then travels to the region periodically for procedural follow-up. If you prefer a more flexible schedule without constant travel, this type of arrangement is increasingly available and represents a meaningful way to extend your reach beyond where you physically sit.

Registration and Credentialing Requirements

To work as a locum dermatologist in Australia, you will need general registration with the Australian Health Practitioner Regulation Agency (AHPRA) under the Medical Board of Australia, with specialist registration in dermatology. Specialist registration is granted on the basis of recognised fellowship, which for dermatology means either the FACD (Fellowship of the Australasian College of Dermatologists) or an equivalent qualification assessed through the AMC or a specialist pathway for international medical graduates.

AHPRA Specialist Registration

If you trained in Australia and hold FACD, your pathway to specialist registration is straightforward. You apply through AHPRA, provide evidence of your fellowship, and undergo the standard identity and criminal history checks. If you trained overseas and hold a recognised equivalent qualification, you may be eligible for the Competent Authority Pathway or the Specialist Pathway, both of which are managed through AHPRA. The Expedited Specialist Pathway, designed for high-demand specialties, may also be available to eligible international dermatologists.

Hospital Credentialing

Each hospital you work at as a locum will also require you to complete its own credentialing process. This involves submitting your AHPRA registration certificate, fellowship certificate, indemnity insurance details, CV, references, immunisation records, and a completed credentialing application. The timeline varies from a few days for facilities with streamlined processes to several weeks for larger public health networks. If you work through a platform like StatDoctor, your credentialing documents can be stored centrally and submitted to multiple facilities without having to compile the same paperwork repeatedly.

Medical Indemnity

You will need current medical indemnity insurance that covers your dermatology locum work. The two main providers in Australia are MDA National and Avant. Ensure your policy covers the procedures you perform, including excisions and biopsies, and confirm whether your coverage extends to rural and remote placements if you plan to take those roles.

Where Are Locum Dermatology Jobs in Australia?

Locum dermatology roles are available in every state and territory, though the nature and volume of work differs considerably by location.

In Queensland, the skin cancer burden is among the highest nationally, and demand for visiting dermatologists in regional centres such as Cairns, Rockhampton, Townsville, and Mackay is significant. The Queensland visiting specialist program places dermatologists in rural facilities on a rotational basis, and the state has invested in teledermoscopy infrastructure to support remote consultations.

In New South Wales, large regional hospitals including Orange, Tamworth, Wagga Wagga, and Dubbo regularly seek locum dermatology cover. Western Sydney and Western NSW have particular shortfalls due to population growth and limited access to private dermatology services.

In Western Australia, the sheer geographic spread of the state creates significant challenges for rural skin cancer management. Kalgoorlie, Karratha, Port Hedland, and the Kimberley region all have documented shortfalls in dermatology cover, with roles often attracting premium rates due to remoteness.

The Northern Territory presents some of the most acute need, with Darwin Hospital and the regional health services in Katherine, Alice Springs, and Tennant Creek all relying on visiting specialists. Work here is clinically rewarding but does require comfort with remote practice conditions and, at times, complex social presentations.

Victoria, South Australia, and Tasmania also have locum dermatology opportunities, particularly in regional centres and hospital outpatient departments where permanent positions have been difficult to fill.

Getting Started as a Locum Dermatologist

If you have AHPRA specialist registration and want to begin taking locum dermatology shifts, the practical steps are relatively straightforward. You will need an Australian Business Number (ABN) if you plan to invoice as a contractor, which is the standard arrangement for locum specialist work. You will also need to set aside funds for GST if your annual locum income is likely to exceed $75,000, as you will be required to register for GST and lodge a Business Activity Statement (BAS) quarterly. Consulting a medical-specialist accountant early is worthwhile, particularly as your income grows.

In terms of scheduling, many dermatology locum roles are booked weeks to months in advance, particularly in rural settings where facilities need to plan outreach clinics around your availability. Building a profile that makes it easy for facilities to book you, see your credentials, and communicate directly without going through an agency is one of the best ways to get consistent work without losing a significant portion of your rate to intermediaries.

How StatDoctor Can Help

StatDoctor is an Australian platform built specifically for doctors who want to find and book locum shifts without the overhead of traditional agencies. Instead of paying a recruitment agency 20 to 30 per cent of your rate, StatDoctor connects you directly with hospitals and clinics, which means more money reaches you and facilities pay less to fill their gaps.

If you are a dermatologist looking to expand into locum work in 2026, whether that means one day a fortnight in a regional clinic or full-time visiting specialist rotations across Queensland and WA, StatDoctor can help you find the right roles for your schedule and preferences. You can store your credentialing documents in one place, receive booking requests directly from facilities, and manage your schedule with full transparency over rates and conditions.

Download the StatDoctor app and create your profile today. With locum dermatology demand at a historic high and the specialist shortage showing no signs of easing, the opportunities available to you this year are genuinely exceptional.
fieldData.post-summary: Australia faces a critical shortage of dermatologists, with 94 per cent of the workforce concentrated in metropolitan areas and fewer than 40 practising in rural and regional communities. For FACD-qualified doctors, this shortage creates exceptional opportunities for locum work, with day rates ranging from $2,000 to $4,500 depending on location, and demand for visiting specialists across Queensland, NSW, WA, and the Northern Territory at a historic high.
