fieldData.post-body:
Australia has a critical shortage of neurologists and the gap between supply and demand is expected to persist well into the 2030s. For doctors with a neurology fellowship or registrar training, this creates an unusually strong locum market with high rates, diverse clinical environments, and the ability to choose where and when you work. This guide covers locum neurology jobs in Australia for 2026, including pay rates by level, what qualifications you need, and how to find work across the states.

The Demand for Locum Neurology Jobs in Australia

The Australian and New Zealand Association of Neurologists (ANZAN) Workforce Committee has modelled neurologist supply against demand through to 2034. In 2021, there were 422 registered neurologists across Australia. Even with projected growth to approximately 739 neurologists by 2034, the committee's modelling shows persistent deficits in both initial and review encounters, meaning demand will continue to outpace supply for the foreseeable future.

This is not just a rural problem. Urban hospitals, particularly in outer metropolitan areas, also struggle to staff neurology departments consistently. Consultant leave, training positions, and the growth of conditions like stroke, epilepsy, multiple sclerosis, and dementia are all driving demand upward at a faster rate than the workforce is expanding.

Regional Australia Carries the Greatest Burden

Regional Australia is particularly underserved. Research published in the Journal of Neurology, Neurosurgery and Psychiatry found that regional areas contain 31 per cent of Australia's population but are served by only 4.1 per cent of its neurologists. For patients outside of major metropolitan centres, accessing neurology care can mean waiting months for a referral or travelling several hours each way.

This creates exactly the kind of opportunity that locum work is designed to fill. Regional hospitals across Queensland, Western Australia, South Australia, and New South Wales regularly advertise for locum neurology cover, and the rates in these settings tend to be higher than city-based positions to reflect the additional travel and accommodation requirements.

Locum Neurology Pay Rates in Australia

Neurology is a well-compensated specialty in the locum market, reflecting both the years of training required and the acute shortage of available practitioners. Rates vary by level, state, and whether you are covering public hospital shifts or private outpatient sessions.

Consultant Neurologist Rates

For a consultant neurologist (Fellow of the Royal Australasian College of Physicians with a neurology subspecialty, or equivalent recognition), locum rates in the public sector typically range from $250 to $400 per hour depending on the location and acuity of the role. Day rates for hospital cover, including on-call components, generally sit between $2,000 and $4,000 per day.

Private outpatient sessions can pay differently. If you are billing through Medicare under your own provider number, your income depends on billings rather than a fixed rate. A busy outpatient session with 10 to 15 patients can generate $1,500 to $3,000 or more, depending on the complexity of consultations and the mix of initial versus review appointments.

Salary data from ERI for 2026 puts the average full-time equivalent for a neurologist in Australia at approximately $370,000 per year. In Sydney, the average rises to around $393,000, and in Melbourne to approximately $415,000. Locum rates on an annualised basis tend to exceed these figures when you account for the premium built into casual and short-term engagements.

Registrar and Advanced Trainee Rates

If you are a neurology registrar or advanced trainee completing your FRACP training, locum rates at the registrar level typically range from $100 to $180 per hour for hospital-based work. Some states will allow registrars to take locum positions between training terms or during annual leave, while others require the employing hospital to confirm that the work is consistent with your training requirements. Always check this with your training supervisor and college before committing to locum shifts as a registrar.

State-by-State Rates and Super Inclusions

As with other specialties, whether the quoted rate is super-inclusive or super-exclusive varies by state. In Victoria and Queensland, quoted rates typically include the 12 per cent superannuation contribution. In New South Wales, super is more commonly paid on top of the quoted rate. A $300 per hour Victorian offer and a $300 per hour NSW offer are not equivalent. The NSW offer is worth approximately $36 per hour more in pre-tax terms. Always confirm which basis the rate is quoted on before you accept.

What Qualifications You Need for Locum Neurology Work

The requirements differ depending on whether you trained in Australia or overseas, and whether you are seeking work as a consultant or a registrar.

Australian-Trained Neurologists

To work as a consultant neurologist in Australia, you need full registration with AHPRA, completion of your FRACP and the advanced training program in neurology administered through the Royal Australasian College of Physicians (RACP). You will also need a valid Medicare provider number, a current medical indemnity policy that covers locum work, and hospital credentialing at the facilities where you intend to work.

Hospital credentialing is the most common cause of delays when starting locum work. Most hospitals require certified copies of your fellowship certificate, proof of indemnity, your AHPRA registration certificate, a current curriculum vitae, and referee contacts from recent clinical supervisors. Starting this process at least four to six weeks before your first shift will prevent gaps in your work schedule.

International Medical Graduates

If you trained as a neurologist overseas, the pathway to working in Australia depends on your country of training and specialty recognition. The Expedited Specialist Pathway allows certain internationally-trained specialists to apply for specialist registration through AHPRA without sitting the AMC examinations, provided their training is assessed as comparable to Australian fellowship standards. The Medical Board of Australia assesses applications through the relevant specialist college, which for neurology is the RACP.

If your qualifications are not assessed as comparable, you may need to complete additional training or supervised practice under area of need provisions. Some overseas-trained neurologists begin work in Australia through limited or area of need registration, particularly in rural or regional hospitals that have difficulty filling positions, and then convert to general registration after completing the required supervised period.

The AHPRA website and the RACP's international medical graduate section have the most current information on required assessments, timelines, and costs. Processing times vary but the AMC assessment pathway for specialists typically takes six to eighteen months from application to registration.

Types of Locum Neurology Work Available

Locum neurology in Australia covers a range of clinical settings and commitment levels. Understanding what each type involves helps you match opportunities to your skills and availability.

Inpatient and Ward Cover

Public hospital neurology departments need locum cover for consultant and registrar leave, parental leave, and gaps between permanent appointments. Inpatient work involves reviewing ward patients, seeing stroke and seizure presentations through emergency, and supporting the broader medical team with neurological consults. On-call components, including after-hours phone consultations and emergency callouts, are typically included in the daily rate.

Outpatient Clinics

Many neurology positions are outpatient-focused, covering clinics for epilepsy, multiple sclerosis, movement disorders, headache, and general neurology. Outpatient locum shifts are often easier to plan around because they run to a fixed schedule and do not typically involve after-hours commitments. These positions are available in both metropolitan and regional settings, and some clinics now offer hybrid arrangements where you see patients on site for part of the session and complete documentation or telehealth reviews remotely.

Telehealth Neurology

The growth of telehealth since 2020 has opened up a significant new category of locum work for neurologists. Regional health services in Western Australia, Queensland, and the Northern Territory increasingly run telehealth neurology clinics to provide specialist access to patients who cannot travel. Many of these clinics operate on a sessional or casual basis and can be done from your home city. If you hold a Medicare provider number and appropriate credentialing with the health service, telehealth locum neurology work can complement your existing schedule without requiring interstate travel.

How to Find Locum Neurology Jobs in Australia

There are several ways to find locum neurology work in Australia. Traditional medical recruitment agencies maintain databases of hospitals seeking locum cover and can match you to positions based on your availability and location preferences. The main limitation with agencies is the margin they charge, which typically reduces the effective hourly rate you receive compared to working directly with the hospital.

Some neurologists build relationships directly with hospital medical staffing offices and accept work on an ad hoc basis as vacancies arise. This approach works well once you are established in a particular state or have existing connections in the public hospital system.

StatDoctor connects locum doctors directly with hospitals and clinics without the traditional agency intermediary. Because the platform removes the agency margin, hospitals can offer more competitive rates and doctors receive a higher proportion of what the hospital actually pays. For neurology specifically, where day rates are substantial, the difference between an agency-intermediated rate and a direct rate can be considerable over a series of shifts.

How StatDoctor Can Help

StatDoctor is built for exactly this kind of work. If you are a neurologist or neurology registrar looking for locum shifts in Australia, the platform lets you set your availability, specialty, and preferred locations, and then connects you directly with hospitals and clinics that need cover. There is no agency in the middle taking a cut, which means the rates on offer tend to be higher than what you would see through a traditional agency.

Hospitals on the platform can see your verified credentials, making the credentialing process faster. You can manage your indemnity documentation, registration certificates, and availability all through the app, and hospitals can contact you directly when a shift matches your profile.

If you are ready to start taking locum neurology work or want to explore what is currently available in your state, download the StatDoctor app and set up your profile today. The app is free for doctors to use and available on iOS and Android.
fieldData.post-summary: Australia has a critical shortage of neurologists, with demand far outpacing supply through to 2034. If you hold a neurology fellowship or are training as a neurology registrar, locum opportunities are plentiful and the pay rates are among the strongest in specialist medicine.
fieldData.author: 68e360c1b6fe0fae2425baeb
fieldData.name: Locum Neurology Jobs in Australia: Pay Rates, Demand, and How to Get Started in 2026

fieldData.post-body:
ENT surgery, formally known as otolaryngology, head and neck surgery, is one of the smallest surgical specialties in Australia by trainee numbers, yet demand for ENT services continues to outpace supply in both metropolitan and regional hospitals. If you are a qualified ENT surgeon or a senior registrar looking at locum work, the combination of long public waitlists, a concentrated consultant workforce, and growing private demand makes this a strong market in 2026. This guide walks you through what locum ENT work in Australia actually pays, where the demand sits, and how you can get started without losing weeks to paperwork.

Why ENT Locum Demand Is So Strong in 2026

ENT has historically been one of the most undersupplied surgical specialties outside of capital cities. The Royal Australasian College of Surgeons reports that fewer than 600 active otolaryngology fellows practise in Australia, and only a small fraction of them work outside major metropolitan areas. The result is long public waitlists for tonsillectomies, grommets, septoplasties and head and neck procedures, alongside chronic understaffing in regional hospitals that rely on visiting consultants.

For locums, this translates into consistent work across three streams. Public hospitals need short notice cover for leave, recruitment gaps and elective surgery blitz programs. Regional and rural hospitals offer fly in fly out consultant rosters to maintain theatre access. Private day surgeries and ENT clinics bring in locum fellows to cover planned leave and to run high volume lists. If you hold specialist registration, you can usually choose between all three.

The Numbers Driving the Shortage

A few data points are worth keeping in mind as you plan. Public elective surgery waitlists across most states remain well above pre pandemic levels, with ENT routinely appearing in the top three specialties for overdue category 2 and category 3 patients. Australia will need around 13,000 additional doctors by the end of 2026 to meet population demand, and surgical specialties with small training pipelines, including ENT, are expected to carry an outsized share of that gap. Around 27 percent of Australians live in regional or remote areas, but fewer than 16 percent of surgeons practise outside the major cities. For ENT specifically, that rural proportion is even smaller.

What Locum ENT Doctors Actually Earn

Locum pay for ENT varies by seniority, location, scope of practice and whether you are engaged through a hospital directly or via an agency. As a general guide for 2026, these are the ranges you can reasonably expect once you have your registration and credentialing sorted.

Registrar and Senior Registrar Rates

If you are an accredited or unaccredited ENT registrar doing locum shifts, metropolitan hospitals typically pay between $140 and $200 per hour for standard weekday cover. Weekend, public holiday and on call shifts usually attract loadings of 50 to 100 percent. Regional hospitals offering short term block contracts often sit at the upper end, with $180 to $220 per hour plus travel and accommodation provided. For a standard Monday to Friday week, senior registrars regularly clear $8,000 to $10,000 gross before tax.

Consultant and Fellow Rates

Once you hold FRACS (OHNS) or an equivalent specialist qualification, the jump in rates is significant. Metropolitan consultant locums generally sit between $250 and $350 per hour, with outlying roles paying $400 or more for night and weekend cover. In rural and remote settings, day rates of $2,500 to $3,500 are common, and you can expect flights, ground transport, accommodation and meals on top. A one week regional block, including a theatre list, outpatient clinics and on call, will commonly bill out at $15,000 to $22,000 gross.

Private Sector and Clinic Work

Locum cover in private ENT practice tends to be structured on a percentage of billings rather than an hourly rate. Splits of 60 to 70 percent to the locum are typical, with the clinic retaining the remainder for rooms, admin and instruments. A busy private list with MBS item numbers for septoplasty, endoscopic sinus surgery and middle ear procedures can be highly lucrative, particularly if you bring your own theatre efficiency and can absorb a full day of cases.

Where the ENT Locum Jobs Are

Geography matters. The highest day rates are almost always in hospitals that struggle to retain a permanent consultant. If you are open to travel, the following regions consistently have ENT locum vacancies in 2026.

Regional New South Wales and Victoria

Hospitals across the Hunter New England, Mid North Coast, Murrumbidgee and Western NSW networks frequently run ENT locum rosters. In Victoria, Barwon Health, Goulburn Valley Health, Bendigo Health and Albury Wodonga Health are regular users of visiting ENT specialists. Expect one to two week blocks, often with a theatre day, outpatient clinic and weekend on call.

Queensland Regional Centres

Queensland has some of the strongest regional demand for ENT services in the country. Townsville, Cairns, Mackay, Rockhampton, Bundaberg and Toowoomba all run ENT services that depend on a mix of resident and locum consultants. Day rates in these centres are among the highest in Australia, particularly in the Far North and the Central West.

Western Australia and the Northern Territory

Perth metropolitan hospitals occasionally take short term ENT locums, but the real volume is in the Kimberley, Pilbara and Goldfields regions, and across the Top End. Royal Darwin Hospital, Alice Springs Hospital and the Kimberley regional services regularly engage fly in fly out ENT consultants, with a strong case load in paediatric middle ear disease, which remains a significant public health issue in remote Aboriginal communities.

Tasmania and South Australia

Tasmania runs a small permanent ENT workforce concentrated in Hobart and Launceston, meaning leave cover is frequently advertised. South Australia has several regional roles in the Riverland, Limestone Coast and Flinders and Upper North networks. These are typically week long blocks paid at full regional rates with travel and accommodation included.

Registration and Credentialing: What You Need

Before you can accept a locum role, you need the right registration with AHPRA and completed credentialing at each hospital. For ENT, the specific requirements are tight because of the invasive nature of the work.

AHPRA Registration

You will need specialist registration in otolaryngology, head and neck surgery if you want to work at consultant level. Australian trained FRACS (OHNS) holders qualify automatically. International medical graduates usually go through the RACS specialist assessment or the new expedited specialist pathway, which has reduced assessment times significantly for applicants from the UK, Ireland and New Zealand. Registrars need general registration plus an approved training position or an area of need endorsement.

Hospital Credentialing

Each hospital runs its own credentialing process, but the core documents are consistent. You will need current AHPRA registration, medical indemnity insurance covering the scope of practice, a CV, references, vaccination and serology evidence, police check, working with children check where relevant, and a completed scope of clinical practice form. Most hospitals will also ask for evidence of recent CPD, including the RACS continuing professional development program.

Credentialing can take anywhere from two to eight weeks, depending on the hospital and how organised your documents are. Setting up a single, up to date credentialing pack that you can submit quickly is one of the highest value things you can do early in your locum career.

Practical Tips for Maximising Your ENT Locum Income

A few practical strategies separate locums who earn well from those who leave money on the table.

First, build a relationship with two or three hospitals directly rather than relying solely on agencies. Agencies take a margin that can reach 20 to 30 percent of the billed rate, and direct engagement often means better pay, faster credentialing renewals and a more predictable roster.

Second, batch your work into blocks of one to two weeks rather than chasing single day shifts. Travel and credentialing overhead is the same regardless of length, so longer blocks deliver a much better net hourly rate.

Third, think carefully about your business structure. Most ENT locums earning above $200,000 a year benefit from operating through a Pty Ltd company rather than as a sole trader, particularly once you factor in income splitting, superannuation contributions and retained earnings. Talk to an accountant who specialises in medical professionals before you pick a structure.

Finally, keep your CPD, indemnity and medical registration renewals on a single calendar. A lapsed indemnity certificate or expired CPR certificate can cost you a week of work if a hospital catches it during onboarding.

How StatDoctor Can Help

StatDoctor is built for exactly this kind of work. Instead of routing you through an agency, StatDoctor connects you directly with hospitals and clinics that need ENT cover. You set your rate, choose the shifts and blocks that suit your life, and keep the full value of your billings without an agency margin eating into your income.

Hospitals benefit too, because they can post vacancies, review verified profiles and confirm coverage in minutes rather than weeks. For you as a locum, that means faster offers, more consistent work and transparent pay.

If you are ready to take more control of your ENT locum career in 2026, download the StatDoctor app and create your profile. Upload your credentialing pack once, set your availability, and start receiving matched shifts from hospitals that actually need your skills. Whether you want a single weekend in regional Queensland or a recurring weekly list in metropolitan Sydney, StatDoctor puts you directly in front of the people making hiring decisions.
fieldData.post-summary: ENT is one of Australia's most undersupplied surgical specialties, which means strong demand and premium pay for locums. Here is what to expect from ENT locum rates, where the jobs are, and how to get started in 2026.

fieldData.name: Locum ENT Jobs in Australia: Pay Rates, Demand, and How to Get Started

fieldData.post-body:
Cardiology is one of the highest paying and most sought after medical specialties in Australia, and the demand for locum cardiologists continues to grow in 2026. With an ageing population, rising rates of cardiovascular disease, and a significant portion of the existing cardiology workforce approaching retirement, hospitals across the country are actively seeking locum specialists to fill gaps. If you hold specialist registration in cardiology or are considering a move into locum work, here is everything you need to know about pay rates, demand hotspots, and how to get started.

Why Cardiology Locum Work Is Booming in 2026

Cardiovascular disease remains the leading cause of death in Australia, accounting for around 25% of all deaths annually. The demand for cardiologists has been rising steadily, and in 2026, that trend shows no sign of slowing down. Several factors are driving the need for locum cardiologists across the country.

An Ageing Workforce

Approximately 60% of practising cardiologists in Australia are over the age of 55. As these specialists begin to retire over the coming decade, the gap between patient demand and available clinicians is widening. Hospitals, particularly those in regional and remote areas, are turning to locum placements to maintain continuity of cardiac care.

Growing Patient Numbers

Australia's population is both growing and ageing. The proportion of Australians aged 65 and over is increasing year on year, and this demographic carries the highest burden of cardiovascular conditions including ischaemic heart disease, heart failure, and atrial fibrillation. More patients means more demand for specialist cardiology services in both public hospitals and private practice settings.

Skills in Demand Recognition

Cardiology is listed on Australia's Core Skills in Demand List, which signals a recognised national shortage. This classification has practical implications for international medical graduates (IMGs), as it can unlock faster visa processing and more streamlined pathways to specialist registration through AHPRA.

How Much Do Locum Cardiologists Earn in Australia?

Locum cardiology is one of the higher paying specialty locum markets in Australia. The rates vary depending on location, the type of cardiology work involved, and whether the placement is in a metropolitan or regional setting.

Daily Locum Rates

As a general guide, locum cardiologists in Australia can expect the following daily rates in 2026. Metropolitan hospitals typically offer between $2,000 and $2,800 per day. Regional and outer metropolitan placements tend to range from $2,500 to $3,200 per day. Remote and rural hospitals, where demand is greatest, can offer $3,000 to $3,500 or more per day, often with accommodation and flights included.

For context, a recent locum cardiology position advertised in Cairns, North Queensland, was offering $1,800 per day with accommodation and return flights included. Other regional placements have been advertised at $2,300 per day with full travel benefits. Interventional cardiologists, given the procedural nature of their work, can command rates at the higher end of the spectrum.

Annual Earning Potential

If you work consistently as a locum cardiologist, annual earnings can range from $350,000 to over $550,000 depending on how many weeks you work and where you take placements. Some cardiologists who focus on high demand remote placements and work 40 or more weeks per year report earnings above $600,000 before tax.

What Is Typically Included

Many locum cardiology placements, especially those in regional and remote areas, include accommodation, flights or travel reimbursement, car hire or a vehicle allowance, and medical indemnity coverage (though you should always confirm this). These inclusions can significantly boost the effective value of your daily rate.

Where Are Locum Cardiology Jobs in Demand?

Demand for locum cardiologists is spread across Australia, but certain regions consistently have more vacancies than others.

Regional Queensland

North Queensland, including Cairns, Townsville, and Mackay, has a persistent need for visiting cardiologists. Many regional hospitals in Queensland lack a permanent cardiology consultant and rely on locum cover to provide outpatient clinics, inpatient consultations, and echocardiography services.

Northern Territory

Darwin and Alice Springs regularly seek locum cardiologists to support their small but busy cardiac units. The NT offers some of the highest locum rates in the country, along with the opportunity to work with diverse patient populations including Indigenous communities with high rates of rheumatic heart disease.

Western Australia

Regional centres like Geraldton, Bunbury, and Kalgoorlie often require visiting cardiologists. Perth's tertiary hospitals also use locum specialists to cover leave and manage waitlist pressures.

Rural New South Wales and Victoria

Smaller base hospitals in rural NSW and Victoria frequently need locum cardiology cover. Towns like Dubbo, Orange, Wagga Wagga, Bendigo, and Ballarat have growing cardiac patient loads but limited permanent specialist staff.

Tasmania

Both Launceston and the North West Regional Hospital in Burnie have recurring needs for locum cardiologists, particularly for non invasive cardiology and heart failure management.

What You Need to Work as a Locum Cardiologist

AHPRA Specialist Registration

To work as a locum cardiologist in Australia, you need specialist registration with the Medical Board of Australia through AHPRA. For Australian trained cardiologists who have completed RACP Advanced Training in Cardiology (a three year program following Basic Physician Training), this is straightforward.

Pathways for International Medical Graduates

If you trained overseas, there are several pathways to specialist registration. The Standard Specialist Assessment Pathway involves a comprehensive assessment by the RACP, which may require a period of supervised practice or additional examinations. The Accelerated Specialist Pathway, available for experienced specialists with at least five years of post fellowship practice, allows a faster route to recognition. Because cardiology is on the Skills in Demand List, IMGs may also benefit from expedited visa processing under subclass 482 (Temporary Skill Shortage) or subclass 494 (Skilled Employer Sponsored Regional) visas.

Hospital Credentialing

Beyond AHPRA registration, each hospital will require you to go through a credentialing and privileging process. This typically involves providing your CV, evidence of qualifications, references, and proof of medical indemnity insurance. The process can take anywhere from two to eight weeks, so it is worth starting early. Having your documents organised and ready to go can significantly speed things up.

Medical Indemnity Insurance

You will need appropriate medical indemnity cover for your scope of practice. If you are working through an agency, check whether indemnity is included in the placement package. If you are contracting directly with a hospital, you will need to arrange your own cover through providers like MDA National, Avant, or MIPS.

Interventional vs Non Invasive Cardiology Locum Work

It is worth noting that the type of cardiology work you do will influence both the placements available to you and the rates you can negotiate.

Interventional Cardiology

Interventional cardiologists who perform procedures such as coronary angiography, percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI), and device implantation are in particularly high demand. However, these placements are typically limited to larger hospitals with catheterisation laboratories. Cities like Darwin, Cairns, Townsville, and select regional centres with cath lab facilities regularly seek interventional locum cover.

Non Invasive Cardiology

Non invasive cardiologists, including those skilled in echocardiography, Holter monitoring, stress testing, and outpatient heart failure management, have a broader range of placement options. Smaller regional hospitals that lack a cath lab still need cardiology input for diagnostic work, risk stratification, and ongoing management of chronic cardiac conditions.

Tips for Getting Started in Locum Cardiology

If you are considering your first locum cardiology placement, here are a few practical steps to set yourself up for success.

Start by ensuring all your documentation is current. This includes your AHPRA registration, medical indemnity certificate, CPD records, and at least two recent professional references. Having a well organised credential file will make the onboarding process much smoother.

Consider starting with a shorter placement of one to two weeks to get a feel for the locum lifestyle before committing to longer assignments. This gives you time to adjust to working in unfamiliar environments and with new teams.

Be open to regional placements. While metropolitan hospitals are appealing, the best locum rates and the most inclusive packages are found in regional and remote areas. These placements also tend to offer a broader scope of practice, which many cardiologists find professionally rewarding.

Build relationships with hospitals where you have had positive experiences. Many locum cardiologists develop ongoing arrangements with specific hospitals, returning for regular blocks of cover throughout the year. This provides income stability while still maintaining the flexibility that locum work offers.

Finally, register your profile on platforms that connect you directly with hospitals. Cutting out the middleman can mean better rates and more control over your schedule.

How StatDoctor Can Help

StatDoctor is built specifically for the Australian locum market, connecting doctors directly with hospitals and clinics without the traditional agency markup. If you are a cardiologist looking for locum work, StatDoctor lets you browse available shifts, set your preferred locations and rates, and get matched with hospitals that need your skills.

By removing the agency from the equation, StatDoctor helps you earn more per shift while giving hospitals access to a transparent, cost effective way to fill their cardiology vacancies. Whether you are an experienced locum looking for your next placement or a permanent cardiologist exploring locum work for the first time, StatDoctor makes the process simple.

Download the StatDoctor app today and start browsing locum cardiology opportunities across Australia.
fieldData.post-summary: Locum cardiology is one of the highest paying specialty markets in Australia. With daily rates reaching $3,500 and growing demand driven by an ageing workforce and rising cardiovascular disease rates, 2026 is an ideal time to explore locum cardiology placements across the country.

fieldData.post-body:
The end of financial year is fast approaching, and if you have been working as a locum doctor in Australia, now is the time to get your affairs in order. Whether you operate as a sole trader or through a company structure, the weeks between now and June 30, 2026 are critical for maximising your deductions, topping up your superannuation, and making sure your records are airtight before tax time. This EOFY checklist is tailored specifically for locum doctors, covering the key actions you should take to avoid nasty surprises when you lodge your return.

Why EOFY Preparation Matters More for Locum Doctors

Locum doctors face a unique set of tax circumstances compared to permanently employed medical professionals. You are likely managing income from multiple hospitals or clinics, tracking travel between sites, paying your own professional indemnity insurance, and handling GST obligations if your turnover exceeds $75,000. Without a payroll department doing the heavy lifting, the responsibility for accurate reporting falls entirely on you. Getting your EOFY preparation right can mean the difference between a healthy refund and an unexpected bill from the ATO.

Review All Your Income Sources

The first step in your EOFY checklist is to compile a complete picture of every dollar you earned during the 2025 to 2026 financial year. As a locum, you may have worked at five, ten, or even twenty different facilities over the past twelve months. Each one should have issued you payment summaries or provided records through Single Touch Payroll (STP).

Log into your myGov account and check your ATO online services portal. Most employer payment summaries will appear here automatically if the facility reported through STP. For any income you received as a contractor, make sure you have invoices and bank statements to match. If you earned income through a locum agency, check whether they withheld tax on your behalf or whether you received gross payments. This distinction matters because it determines how much provisional tax you may owe.

Don't Forget Other Income

If you earned interest on savings, received dividends, had rental income, or did any telehealth consulting on the side, include all of it. The ATO's data matching programme is extensive, and discrepancies between what you report and what third parties report will trigger a review.

Maximise Your Tax Deductions Before June 30

One of the biggest advantages of locum work is the range of legitimate deductions available to you. But here is the catch: expenses must be incurred by June 30, 2026 to count towards this financial year. If you have been putting off purchases, now is the time to act.

Common Deductions for Locum Doctors

Professional registration fees are one of the most straightforward deductions. Your AHPRA registration, medical college fees, and any specialist society memberships are all fully deductible in the year you pay them. If your renewal falls in July, consider paying it early before the end of June.

Professional indemnity insurance is another significant deduction. Whether you hold your own policy or pay through a medical defence organisation like MDA National, Avant, or MIGA, the premiums are fully deductible.

Travel expenses between work locations are deductible if you travelled directly from one workplace to another during the same day, or if you travelled to a temporary workplace. Keep a logbook or use an app to track your kilometres. The ATO's cents per kilometre rate for 2025 to 2026 is 88 cents per kilometre, up to a maximum of 5,000 business kilometres. For claims above that, you will need a logbook.

Continuing professional development (CPD) costs, including conference registrations, online courses, medical textbooks, and journal subscriptions, are deductible when they relate directly to your current work. If you attended a conference interstate, your flights, accommodation, and meals during the event may also be claimable.

Equipment and tools you purchased for work, such as a stethoscope, diagnostic instruments, or a laptop used for clinical notes and medical research, can be claimed. Items costing less than $300 can be written off immediately. For items over $300, you depreciate them over their effective life. However, if you are a small business entity with aggregated turnover under $10 million, you can instantly write off assets costing less than $20,000 under the current instant asset write off rules.

Home Office Deductions

If you used a dedicated space at home for administrative tasks like invoicing, CPD, or managing your locum bookings, you can claim home office expenses. The ATO offers two methods: the fixed rate method at 67 cents per hour, or the actual cost method where you calculate the business portion of your electricity, internet, phone, and depreciation on office furniture. Choose whichever gives you the higher deduction, but make sure you have records to support your claim.

Top Up Your Superannuation Before EOFY

Superannuation is one of the most overlooked EOFY strategies for locum doctors. As a contractor or sole trader, no employer is making super contributions on your behalf unless your contract specifically requires it. This means you need to make voluntary contributions yourself.

The concessional contributions cap for 2025 to 2026 is $30,000. Concessional contributions are taxed at just 15% inside the fund, compared to your marginal tax rate which could be as high as 47% (including the Medicare levy). Making a $30,000 concessional contribution could save you nearly $10,000 in tax if you are in the top bracket.

To claim a tax deduction on personal super contributions, you need to lodge a "Notice of Intent to Claim a Deduction" form with your super fund before you lodge your tax return. The contribution must arrive in your super fund's bank account on or before June 30. Do not leave this to the last week, as bank transfers can take several business days to clear, and super funds may have their own cut off dates.

Carry Forward Unused Cap Amounts

If your total super balance was below $500,000 on June 30, 2025, you may be eligible to carry forward unused concessional cap amounts from up to five previous financial years. This can allow you to contribute well above $30,000 in a single year and claim the full amount as a deduction. Check your ATO online account to see your available cap space.

Lodge Your BAS and Reconcile GST

If you are registered for GST (which you must be if your annual turnover is $75,000 or more), make sure all your Business Activity Statements are up to date. Your April to June quarter BAS will be due on July 28, 2026, but you should reconcile your GST collected and GST paid for the full financial year now.

Cross check your accounting software against your bank statements to make sure every invoice has been recorded and every GST credit has been captured. Common GST credits that locum doctors miss include the GST on professional indemnity insurance premiums, accounting fees, medical equipment purchases, and even the GST component of your mobile phone bill if you use it for work.

If you use cloud accounting software like Xero or MYOB, run a GST reconciliation report to check for discrepancies. If your records are mostly manual, this is a good time to consider investing in proper accounting software before the new financial year begins.

Organise Your Records for the ATO

The ATO requires you to keep records for five years from the date you lodge your return. For locum doctors, this means holding onto contracts, invoices, payment summaries, bank statements, logbooks, receipts for deductible expenses, and super contribution statements.

Digital records are perfectly acceptable. Scan your paper receipts and store them in a cloud folder organised by financial year. If the ATO audits you, having well organised records will make the process significantly less stressful.

Key Records to Have Ready

Before you sit down with your accountant or start your tax return, gather the following: all payment summaries and invoices for locum work completed during the year, your AHPRA registration receipt, medical indemnity insurance premium notices, a travel logbook or app export showing work related kilometres, receipts for CPD courses, conferences, textbooks, and subscriptions, super contribution statements and your Notice of Intent form, home office expense records including hours worked from home, and any income from other sources such as investments or rental properties.

Consider Prepaying Deductible Expenses

If you are a sole trader or operate through a company that qualifies as a small business entity, you can prepay certain expenses for up to 12 months in advance and claim the deduction in the current financial year. This can be a powerful strategy if you expect your income to be lower next year or if you simply want to bring forward deductions to reduce this year's tax bill.

Common prepayments include professional indemnity insurance for the next year, journal subscriptions, income protection insurance premiums, and professional membership fees. Just make sure the prepayment covers a period of 12 months or less beginning before June 30.

Book a Meeting With Your Accountant Now

If you use an accountant who specialises in medical professionals, book your EOFY planning meeting as soon as possible. The weeks leading up to June 30 are the busiest time for tax professionals, and availability fills up quickly. A good accountant can identify strategies you may have missed, advise on your business structure for the year ahead, and ensure your provisional tax instalments are set correctly for the 2026 to 2027 year.

If you do not yet have an accountant and have been managing your own tax, this might be the year to invest in professional advice. The complexity of locum income, multiple payers, GST obligations, and contractor arrangements means there is a higher risk of errors. An accountant experienced with locum doctors can often save you far more than their fee.

How StatDoctor Can Help

While getting your tax affairs sorted is essential, finding your next locum shift should be the easy part. StatDoctor connects locum doctors directly with hospitals and GP clinics across Australia, cutting out the agency middleman and putting more money in your pocket. With no agency commissions eating into your rate, every dollar you earn is yours to keep, invest, or contribute to super.

Our platform makes it simple to browse available shifts, manage your credentials, and get booked quickly. Whether you are planning your locum schedule for the new financial year or looking for last minute shifts before June 30 to boost your income, StatDoctor has you covered. Download the StatDoctor app today and take control of your locum career and your finances.
fieldData.post-summary: The end of financial year is approaching fast. Here is a practical EOFY checklist for locum doctors in Australia, covering tax deductions, superannuation strategies, GST reconciliation, and record keeping tips to help you maximise your return before June 30, 2026.
fieldData.author: 68e360c1b6fe0fae2425baeb
fieldData.name: End of Financial Year Checklist for Locum Doctors in Australia: What to Do Before June 30

fieldData.post-body:
Australia has become one of the most popular destinations for Malaysian-trained doctors looking for better pay, lifestyle, and long-term career options. With persistent doctor shortages across the country, a well-defined registration framework, and growing demand for international medical graduates (IMGs), the pathway from Malaysia to Australia is clearer than many expect. If you are a Malaysian doctor considering the move in 2026, here is everything you need to know about registration, visas, and getting started with locum work.

Why Malaysian Doctors Are Choosing Australia in 2026

Australia's healthcare workforce is stretched thin. Rural and regional areas have been dealing with chronic doctor shortages for years, and even metropolitan hospitals are struggling to fill rosters. For Malaysian doctors, this translates into genuine opportunity. Locum day rates in Australia typically range from AUD $1,500 to $2,200 per day for GPs and registrars, with regional and remote placements pushing well above $2,500 per day. Specialists can earn between $2,000 and $3,500 per day depending on specialty and location.

Beyond the financial upside, Australia offers a familiar medical culture. English is the working language, clinical governance frameworks are robust, and hospital systems operate in ways that Malaysian-trained doctors generally adapt to quickly. The country also provides a clear pathway from temporary work rights to permanent residency, which is a significant draw for doctors thinking long term.

AHPRA Registration: The Standard Pathway for Malaysian Doctors

Every doctor working in Australia must be registered with AHPRA (the Australian Health Practitioner Regulation Agency). Your registration pathway depends on where you trained and what qualifications you hold.

Malaysia is not on Australia's "competent authority" list, which means most Malaysian doctors will follow the Standard Pathway. Unlike doctors from the UK, Ireland, Canada, the US, or New Zealand, you will need to sit the AMC exams. Here is what that involves.

Step 1: Set Up Your AMC Candidate Account

Start by creating an account with the Australian Medical Council (AMC). You will need to initiate Primary Source Verification (PSV) of your medical degree through ECFMG's EPIC system. This confirms directly with your Malaysian university that your qualification is genuine. PSV can take several weeks, so begin this as early as possible.

Your medical degree must be listed in the World Directory of Medical Schools (WDOMS) to be recognised. Most Malaysian medical schools with MMC (Malaysian Medical Council) accreditation are included, but it is worth confirming before you start.

Step 2: English Language Proficiency

You will need to demonstrate English language proficiency, even if your medical training was conducted in English. The Medical Board of Australia requires one of the following:

  • IELTS Academic: minimum 7.0 in each band
  • OET: minimum B in each component

Results are typically valid for two years. Many Malaysian doctors find OET more relevant because it is designed for healthcare professionals, but either test is accepted. If English was the language of instruction throughout your medical degree and you have practised in an English-speaking environment, you may be eligible for an exemption, but this is assessed case by case.

Step 3: AMC CAT MCQ Examination

The AMC CAT (Computer Adaptive Test) is a multiple-choice knowledge exam that covers clinical medicine broadly. It tests your understanding across surgery, medicine, obstetrics, paediatrics, psychiatry, and other core disciplines. The exam adapts to your ability level as you progress through questions.

Preparation typically takes three to six months of focused study, depending on how recently you completed your clinical training. There are several preparation courses available both online and in Australian capital cities.

Step 4: AMC Clinical Examination

After passing the CAT MCQ, you sit the AMC Clinical Examination. This is an OSCE-style assessment with multiple clinical stations testing your history-taking, examination skills, clinical reasoning, and communication. The pass rate for this exam sits around 20 to 25 percent, making it the most challenging part of the process.

Many Malaysian doctors choose to attend a bridging or clinical preparation course in Australia before attempting this exam. Courses in Melbourne, Sydney, and Brisbane offer simulated OSCE practice with experienced examiners. Getting some supervised clinical exposure in Australia before the exam also helps you become familiar with Australian clinical norms and communication styles.

Alternatively, you may be eligible for a Workplace-Based Assessment (WBA) in an approved setting, which replaces the clinical exam with structured assessments during supervised practice. This option is available at selected sites and is worth exploring if you prefer an on-the-job assessment format.

Step 5: Supervised Practice and General Registration

Once you pass both AMC exams, you can apply for provisional registration with AHPRA. This allows you to work under supervision in an approved position for a minimum of 12 months (47 weeks full-time equivalent). During this period, your supervisor will assess your clinical competence and professional conduct.

After successfully completing supervised practice, you apply for general registration. At this point, you are fully registered to practise medicine in Australia independently.

Specialist Registration for Malaysian Doctors

If you are a specialist, the pathway is different. You apply through the relevant Australian specialist college, which assesses your qualifications, training, and experience against Australian standards. This may involve interviews, workplace-based assessments, or additional examinations depending on the college.

For high-demand specialties like Psychiatry, Anaesthetics, Obstetrics and Gynaecology, and General Practice, Australia introduced the Expedited Specialist Pathway in late 2024. Eligible specialists under this pathway complete just six months of supervised practice plus orientation training before gaining specialist registration. This represents a significant reduction compared to previous timelines.

Note that Malaysian Masters qualifications are not automatically recognised in Australia. Your specialist qualification will be assessed individually by the relevant college.

Visas and Work Rights

Most Malaysian doctors enter Australia on an employer-sponsored visa. The main options are:

  • Subclass 482 (Skills in Demand) visa: the most common route. Valid for up to four years and tied to your sponsoring employer. As of 2026, the experience requirement has been reduced to just one year. Hospitals and health services across Australia actively sponsor this visa for doctors.
  • Subclass 186 (Employer Nomination Scheme) visa: a pathway to permanent residency. You can apply after two years on a 482 visa (reduced from three years since November 2023), and the time is portable across multiple approved sponsors.

Your employer or their migration agent will generally handle most of the visa paperwork. Given the scale of doctor shortages, many regional hospitals are highly motivated to sponsor, which makes securing a position less difficult than you might assume.

The 10-Year Moratorium and DPA Locations

One thing every IMG needs to understand is Section 19AB, commonly known as the 10-year moratorium. This rule means that, as an overseas-trained doctor, you will generally need to work in a Distribution Priority Area (DPA) for the first 10 years of your Medicare provider number being active. DPA locations include most of rural and regional Australia, as well as some outer metropolitan areas.

In practical terms, this means your early years in Australia will likely be spent outside the major city centres. The upside is that DPA locations often come with the highest pay rates, relocation support, and accommodation packages. Many locum doctors find that rural and regional placements offer a better overall lifestyle than they expected.

For a detailed breakdown of how DPA classifications work, our guide on understanding DPA and Modified Monash classifications is a useful starting point.

Setting Up for Locum Work

Once you have general registration, you can work as a locum doctor anywhere your registration and DPA obligations allow. Before your first shift, you will need:

  • A Tax File Number (TFN) from the ATO
  • An Australian Business Number (ABN) if working as a contractor, which most locums do. Our step-by-step ABN guide walks you through the process.
  • An Australian bank account, ideally opened soon after you arrive
  • Medical indemnity insurance through a provider like MDA National or Avant
  • A Medicare provider number, which determines where you can bill

How StatDoctor Can Help

StatDoctor is Australia's direct-connect platform for locum doctors and hospitals. Instead of going through traditional agencies that charge hospitals 20 to 30 percent in commission, StatDoctor lets you connect directly with hospitals and clinics looking for locum cover. That means better rates for you and lower costs for the facilities you work with.

Whether you are newly registered and looking for your first locum shifts or an experienced doctor exploring opportunities across different states, StatDoctor makes it simple to find, book, and manage your locum work from your phone. Download the StatDoctor app today and start exploring locum opportunities across Australia.
fieldData.post-summary: Planning to move from Malaysia to Australia as a doctor? This guide covers the AMC exam process, AHPRA registration via the Standard Pathway, visa options, the 10-year moratorium, and how to start locum work in 2026.
fieldData.author: 68e360c1b6fe0fae2425baeb
fieldData.name: Moving from Malaysia to Australia as a Doctor: AMC Exams, AHPRA Registration, and Locum Work in 2026

fieldData.post-body:
Australia's ageing population is growing at a pace that the healthcare workforce simply cannot keep up with. By 2031, nearly 20 per cent of the population will be aged over 65, up from around 16 per cent today. The number of Australians aged 70 and over is projected to increase by 2.3 million over the next two decades. For geriatricians and physicians with an interest in elderly care, this creates one of the most secure and rewarding locum markets in the country.

Why Geriatric Medicine Is One of Australia's Fastest Growing Locum Specialties

The demand for geriatricians has surged in recent years, and 2026 is no exception. Australia is facing a shortage of at least 110,000 direct aged care workers within the next decade, and that figure could balloon to over 400,000 by 2050 without significant intervention. While much of the public conversation focuses on nursing and personal care roles, the shortage of specialist geriatricians is just as critical.

Hospitals across the country, particularly in regional and outer metropolitan areas, are struggling to fill consultant geriatrician positions on a permanent basis. That means locum geriatricians are in high demand to cover gaps in rosters, provide specialist input for complex patients, and support aged care liaison services.

The Royal Commission into Aged Care Quality and Safety made it clear that a generalist approach is no longer sufficient to meet the increasingly complex health profiles of older Australians. Residents are entering aged care facilities with multiple chronic conditions, cognitive decline, polypharmacy issues, and frailty syndromes that require genuine geriatric expertise. This has only increased the pressure on hospitals and aged care facilities to secure specialist geriatric input wherever they can.

What Does a Locum Geriatrician Actually Do?

Locum geriatric medicine roles can vary significantly depending on the facility, the location, and the length of the placement. In general, you can expect to work across some or all of the following areas.

Inpatient geriatric medicine: Managing acute admissions of elderly patients in hospital wards, including falls, delirium, cognitive decline, and functional deterioration. Many hospitals have dedicated Aged Care or Geriatric Evaluation and Management (GEM) units.

Orthogeriatrics: Providing perioperative care for elderly patients with hip fractures and other orthopaedic injuries. This is one of the fastest growing subspecialty areas within geriatric medicine, and many hospitals now run formal orthogeriatric services.

Outpatient clinics: Running memory clinics, falls clinics, continence clinics, and general geriatric outpatient assessments. Locum placements that include outpatient work tend to offer a more predictable schedule.

Aged care facility consultations: Providing specialist geriatric consultations to residents in residential aged care facilities (RACFs), particularly for complex medication reviews, behavioural and psychological symptoms of dementia (BPSD), and end of life care planning.

Rehabilitation: Overseeing inpatient rehabilitation programs for elderly patients recovering from surgery, stroke, or prolonged hospital stays.

Locum Geriatric Medicine Pay Rates in 2026

Geriatric medicine locum rates in Australia are competitive, though they tend to sit slightly below the highest paying procedural specialties like anaesthetics or emergency medicine. Here is what you can expect as a locum geriatrician in 2026.

Consultant geriatrician (metropolitan): $2,000 to $2,800 per day. Major teaching hospitals in Sydney, Melbourne, Brisbane, and Perth typically offer rates in this range for consultant level locum cover.

Consultant geriatrician (regional/rural): $2,500 to $3,500 per day. Regional hospitals and those in Modified Monash 3 to 7 areas generally offer higher daily rates to attract specialist cover. Travel and accommodation are almost always provided on top of the daily rate.

Advanced trainee or registrar: $1,200 to $1,800 per day. Advanced trainees in geriatric medicine who hold general registration with AHPRA can also pick up locum shifts, though these are typically supervised roles.

Subspecialty geriatrics (dementia care focus): Geriatricians who specialise in dementia care or psychogeriatrics are particularly sought after and can command rates at the higher end of the scale, reflecting the acute shortage of specialists in this area.

On an annual equivalent basis, locum geriatricians who work consistently can earn between $400,000 and $600,000 or more, compared to a typical salaried consultant position paying $280,000 to $450,000.

Where Are Locum Geriatric Medicine Jobs Available?

The short answer is everywhere. But some states and regions have particularly strong demand.

New South Wales

NSW has the largest elderly population in Australia and a corresponding need for geriatric services. Major hospitals in Western Sydney, the Hunter Valley, and regional centres like Dubbo and Wagga Wagga regularly advertise locum geriatrician roles. Sydney's major teaching hospitals also require locum cover during leave periods.

Victoria

Victoria's public hospital system has well established geriatric medicine departments, and locum demand is consistent across both metropolitan and regional hospitals. Ballarat, Bendigo, and the Latrobe Valley are frequent locum hotspots.

Queensland

Queensland's ageing population is concentrated along the coast, with strong demand in Townsville, Cairns, the Sunshine Coast, and the Gold Coast. Regional hospitals in inland Queensland also offer lucrative locum packages with travel and accommodation included.

South Australia and Western Australia

Both states have significant geriatric medicine workforce gaps, particularly outside of Adelaide and Perth. Locum placements in regional WA and SA tend to offer some of the highest daily rates in the country due to the difficulty of attracting permanent staff.

Tasmania and the Northern Territory

Tasmania has one of the oldest median age populations in Australia, making geriatric services essential. The Northern Territory, while younger on average, has a growing need for geriatric care in its hospital system, particularly for Indigenous elders with complex health needs.

Registration and Qualifications You Need

To work as a locum geriatrician in Australia, you will need the following.

AHPRA specialist registration: You must hold specialist registration with the Medical Board of Australia through AHPRA, with geriatric medicine listed as your specialty. This requires Fellowship of the Royal Australasian College of Physicians (FRACP) with advanced training in geriatric medicine.

For international medical graduates (IMGs): The RACP offers two main pathways for overseas trained geriatricians. The Accelerated Specialist Pathway is available for physicians with comparable qualifications from the UK, Ireland, India, Hong Kong, or Sri Lanka. The Standard Specialist Assessment Pathway is open to specialists from other countries and typically requires 6 to 24 months of supervised practice before full specialist recognition.

Medical indemnity insurance: You will need current medical indemnity cover. Most locum agencies and platforms will require proof of this before placing you in a role.

Working with Vulnerable People check: Depending on the state, you may need a Working with Vulnerable People (WWVP) or Working with Children Check, particularly if your role involves aged care facility visits.

CPD compliance: You must maintain your Continuing Professional Development (CPD) requirements as set by the RACP and the Medical Board of Australia.

Tips for Getting Started in Locum Geriatric Medicine

If you are a geriatrician looking to try locum work for the first time, or an IMG planning to work in Australia, here are a few practical tips.

Start with shorter placements. A one to two week locum is a great way to test the waters and get comfortable with a new hospital's systems and protocols before committing to longer contracts.

Be flexible on location. The best paying and most available locum geriatric medicine roles are often in regional areas. If you are open to working outside major cities, you will have far more options and higher earning potential.

Keep your credentials up to date. Hospital credentialing can take time, so make sure your CV, references, AHPRA registration, and medical indemnity are always current. Some platforms, like StatDoctor, allow you to store your credentials digitally so hospitals can verify them quickly.

Build relationships with hospitals. Many locum geriatricians find that once they complete a successful placement at a hospital, they are invited back regularly. Repeat placements mean less paperwork and a more familiar working environment.

Consider the tax implications. As a locum, you will likely be operating as a sole trader or through a Pty Ltd company. Make sure you understand your obligations around GST, BAS lodgement, and tax deductions for travel and accommodation. StatDoctor's blog has detailed guides on these topics to help you get set up.

How StatDoctor Can Help

StatDoctor connects locum doctors directly with hospitals and clinics across Australia, cutting out the traditional agency middleman. For geriatricians looking for locum work, this means you can browse available shifts, set your own rates, and connect with hospitals that need your expertise, all through the StatDoctor app.

Hospitals post their requirements directly on the platform, and you can respond to the ones that match your availability, preferred location, and rate expectations. There are no hidden fees or agency commissions eating into your earnings.

Whether you are a newly Fellowed geriatrician exploring locum work for the first time, or an experienced consultant looking for flexible placements that fit around your lifestyle, StatDoctor makes it simple to find your next role.

Download the StatDoctor app today and start browsing locum geriatric medicine opportunities across Australia.
fieldData.post-summary: Locum geriatric medicine is one of Australia's fastest growing specialty markets, driven by an ageing population and a critical workforce shortage. This guide covers 2026 pay rates, demand hotspots by state, registration requirements, and practical tips for getting started.
fieldData.author: 68e360c1b6fe0fae2425baeb
fieldData.name: Locum Geriatric Medicine Jobs in Australia: Pay Rates, Demand, and How to Get Started

fieldData.post-body:
If you have spent any time looking into locum work in Australia, you have probably come across the terms DPA and Modified Monash Model. These classification systems might sound like bureaucratic jargon, but they directly affect where you can work, how much you earn, and whether you can access a Medicare provider number. Whether you are an international medical graduate starting your Australian career or a local doctor exploring rural locum placements, understanding these systems is essential for making smart career decisions in 2026.

What Is the Modified Monash Model?

The Modified Monash Model, commonly referred to as MMM, is the Australian Government's system for classifying how rural or remote a location is. Every area in Australia falls into one of seven categories, ranging from MM 1 (major metropolitan cities like Sydney, Melbourne, and Brisbane) through to MM 7 (very remote communities).

The classification is based on two key factors: the population size of a town and its geographical remoteness from major centres. A town might be geographically isolated but still classified as MM 3 or MM 4 if it has a larger population base. Conversely, a small coastal town relatively close to a capital city could be classified as MM 5 if the population is small enough.

The Seven MMM Categories

MM 1 covers major cities with populations over 50,000 in metropolitan areas. MM 2 includes regional centres with populations between 15,000 and 50,000. MM 3 applies to large rural towns with populations between 5,000 and 15,000. MM 4 covers medium rural towns of 1,000 to 5,000 people. MM 5 includes small rural towns under 1,000 people. MM 6 refers to remote communities, and MM 7 covers very remote areas.

For locum doctors, the MMM classification of a location has a direct impact on earning potential. Facilities in MM 4 to MM 7 areas typically offer significantly higher daily rates to attract doctors. It is not uncommon for locum GPs in MM 5 to MM 7 areas to earn $2,000 to $3,500 per day, compared to $1,200 to $1,800 in MM 1 to MM 2 locations.

What Is a Distribution Priority Area?

The Distribution Priority Area classification, known as DPA, is a separate but related system that identifies locations in Australia where there is a measurable shortage of GP services. The Department of Health and Aged Care reviews DPA classifications annually, with the most recent update occurring in March 2025.

DPA status is determined by comparing the level of Medicare billed GP services in a community against a national benchmark. The calculation also takes into account the demographics of the local population, including age, gender, and socioeconomic factors. If a community falls below the benchmark, it receives DPA classification.

All areas classified as MM 2 through to MM 7 are automatically considered DPA. However, some MM 1 areas (metropolitan locations) can also receive DPA status if they are found to be underserviced. This means that even within major cities, certain suburbs may be classified as DPA due to a shortage of bulk billing GPs or high patient to doctor ratios.

Why DPA Matters for International Medical Graduates

If you are an international medical graduate or a foreign graduate of an accredited medical school, DPA classification is one of the most important factors shaping your early career in Australia. Under Section 19AB of the Health Insurance Act 1973, you are required to work in a DPA location to access Medicare billing for the first 10 years of your Australian practice (or until you gain Fellowship with an Australian specialist college).

This is commonly known as the 10 year moratorium. Without being in a DPA area, you cannot bill Medicare, which essentially means you cannot work in private general practice. The restriction applies to both permanent positions and locum work.

The Locum Exemption

There is an important exception for locum work. If you are providing locum services at a practice in a DPA location, you can be granted a 19AB exemption for that specific practice for a maximum of six months, provided you have not previously worked at that practice. This locum exemption is a practical pathway that allows IMG locum doctors to take on short term placements across different DPA locations while building their experience and meeting their moratorium obligations.

It is worth noting that the DPA status of a location can change from year to year. An area that qualifies as DPA in 2025 might lose that status in 2026 if the GP supply in that area improves. Before committing to a locum placement, always check the current DPA status using the Australian Government's Health Workforce Locator tool, which is freely available on the Department of Health website.

How DPA and MMM Affect Your Locum Pay

Both classification systems have a significant influence on locum pay rates, though they work in different ways. The Modified Monash Model affects pay primarily through supply and demand. Locations with higher MMM classifications (MM 4 and above) tend to offer higher rates simply because fewer doctors are willing to work there. Hospitals and clinics in these areas need to compete financially to attract locum cover.

DPA status influences pay indirectly by expanding the pool of available work locations for IMGs, which in turn drives demand for locum doctors in underserviced areas. Many DPA locations in outer metropolitan and regional areas offer competitive rates because they struggle to attract permanent staff.

To give you a practical sense of what this means, here is a general guide to locum GP daily rates by MMM classification in 2026. MM 1 areas typically offer $1,200 to $1,600 per day. MM 2 to MM 3 areas range from $1,500 to $2,200 per day. MM 4 to MM 5 areas offer $1,800 to $2,800 per day. MM 6 to MM 7 areas can reach $2,500 to $3,500 or more per day, particularly when accommodation and travel are included.

Specialist locum rates follow a similar pattern, with remote and very remote placements offering the highest returns. Psychiatry, anaesthetics, and emergency medicine locums in MM 5 to MM 7 areas regularly attract daily rates above $3,000.

Using the Health Workforce Locator Tool

The Australian Government's Health Workforce Locator is a free online tool that allows you to check the MMM classification and DPA status of any address in Australia. You simply enter a street address or suburb name, and the tool returns the current MMM category and DPA status for that location.

This tool is particularly useful when you are evaluating locum opportunities. Before accepting a placement, check the location to understand whether it qualifies as DPA (important for IMGs), what MMM category it falls under, and whether the offered rate is competitive for that classification level.

You can access the Health Workforce Locator at the Department of Health and Aged Care website. Bookmark it, because you will use it regularly as you plan your locum career.

Practical Tips for Navigating DPA and MMM as a Locum

For International Medical Graduates

Always confirm DPA status before accepting a placement. Your Medicare provider number is tied to working in DPA locations, and working outside of a DPA area could mean you cannot bill Medicare for your services. Keep records of all DPA locations where you have worked, as this documentation supports your 19AB exemption applications. Consider building a rotation between several DPA locations to diversify your experience while meeting your moratorium requirements.

For All Locum Doctors

Use MMM classifications to benchmark pay expectations. If a facility in an MM 5 area is offering you an MM 2 rate, you have grounds to negotiate. Factor in the full package when comparing opportunities in different MMM areas. Remote placements often include accommodation, flights, and a vehicle on top of the daily rate. These inclusions can add $300 to $500 in effective daily value. Look beyond the obvious. Some outer metropolitan suburbs in Sydney, Melbourne, and Perth hold DPA status and offer solid rates without requiring a long distance relocation. These can be ideal for locums who want higher earning potential while staying close to a major city.

How StatDoctor Can Help

Navigating DPA classifications, MMM categories, and Medicare requirements can feel overwhelming, especially when you are trying to focus on clinical work. That is where StatDoctor makes a real difference.

StatDoctor connects locum doctors directly with hospitals and GP clinics across Australia, cutting out the middleman and giving you full transparency over rates, locations, and requirements. Every listing on the platform includes the location details you need to check MMM and DPA status, so you can make informed decisions before you apply.

Whether you are an IMG looking for DPA compliant placements or an experienced locum chasing the best rates in regional and remote Australia, StatDoctor gives you the tools to take control of your career. Download the StatDoctor app today and start browsing locum opportunities that match your goals.
fieldData.post-summary: DPA and Modified Monash classifications directly affect where locum doctors can work, their Medicare access, and how much they earn. This guide breaks down both systems and explains what they mean for your locum career in 2026.
