fieldData.post-body:
Why Pakistani Doctors Are Looking to Australia in 2026

Australia is one of the most attractive destinations in the world for Pakistani medical graduates seeking better career opportunities, higher pay, and a stronger quality of life. With ongoing doctor shortages across regional and metropolitan Australia, the demand for skilled international medical graduates (IMGs) has never been higher. Hospitals and clinics across every state and territory are actively recruiting, and Pakistani trained doctors are well placed to fill critical gaps in the workforce.

Unlike countries with mutual recognition agreements, Pakistani MBBS holders will need to go through the Standard Pathway to gain registration. This means sitting the AMC exams and completing a period of supervised practice. While the process takes time and preparation, thousands of Pakistani doctors have successfully made the move, and many are now working as locum doctors, registrars, and consultants across Australia. In this guide, we will walk you through everything you need to know, from the AMC exams and AHPRA registration to visa options and finding your first locum job.

The AMC Exams: Your First Step to Practising in Australia

For Pakistani medical graduates, the Australian Medical Council (AMC) exams are the gateway to medical registration. The AMC assessment has two parts, and both must be completed before you can apply for registration with AHPRA.

AMC CAT (Computer Adaptive Test)

The AMC CAT is a computer based multiple choice examination that tests your clinical knowledge across all major medical disciplines including medicine, surgery, obstetrics and gynaecology, paediatrics, and psychiatry. The exam is adaptive, meaning the difficulty of questions adjusts based on your responses.

One of the biggest advantages for Pakistani doctors is that you can sit the AMC CAT while still in Pakistan. Pearson VUE test centres are available in Lahore, Karachi, and Islamabad, so you do not need to travel to Australia for this stage. The exam fee is approximately $2,920 AUD as of 2026. Most candidates spend three to six months preparing, and resources like AMC question banks, study groups, and online courses are widely used by Pakistani graduates.

AMC Clinical Exam (OSCE)

Once you pass the CAT, the next step is the AMC Clinical Examination. This is an Objective Structured Clinical Examination (OSCE) held at the AMC National Test Centre in Melbourne, Australia. The exam assesses your clinical skills, communication abilities, and clinical reasoning through a series of simulated patient encounters.

The clinical exam fee is approximately $3,000 AUD. You will need to travel to Melbourne to sit it, so factor in the cost of flights, accommodation, and living expenses during your preparation period. Many candidates arrive in Australia a few weeks early to acclimatise, practise with local study groups, and familiarise themselves with the Australian clinical context. Some candidates choose to do short clinical observerships at Australian hospitals before sitting the OSCE, which can be incredibly helpful for understanding how Australian doctors communicate with patients.

AHPRA Registration: Getting Your Licence to Practise

After passing both AMC exams, you can apply for registration through the Australian Health Practitioner Regulation Agency (AHPRA). Pakistani doctors follow the Standard Pathway, which involves a period of supervised practice before you can obtain full general registration.

Primary Source Verification

Before AHPRA processes your application, your medical qualifications must be verified through EPIC (the Educational Commission for Foreign Medical Graduates' Electronic Portfolio of International Credentials). EPIC contacts your Pakistani medical university directly to confirm the authenticity of your degree and academic records. This verification process typically takes two to four months, though it can take longer depending on your university's response time. It is a good idea to start this process early, ideally while you are still preparing for the AMC exams.

Provisional and Limited Registration

Once your qualifications are verified and your AMC exams are passed, AHPRA will grant you either provisional or limited registration depending on your circumstances. Provisional registration allows you to work in approved intern or supervised practice positions. Limited registration is typically granted for specific supervised roles in areas of workforce need, particularly in regional and rural Australia.

You will need to complete a minimum of 12 months of supervised practice in an approved position. During this time, your supervising doctor will assess your clinical competence, communication skills, and ability to work safely within the Australian healthcare system. After successfully completing your supervised period, you can apply to convert to general registration, which gives you full autonomy to practise medicine in Australia.

Registration Costs

The AHPRA annual registration fee for medical practitioners is approximately $890 AUD for limited registration and $890 for general registration in 2026. Combined with the AMC exam fees, EPIC verification, and other administrative costs, the total registration pathway costs roughly $7,000 to $8,000 AUD. While this is a significant investment, it pays for itself quickly once you start earning Australian locum rates.

Visa Options for Pakistani Doctors Moving to Australia

Securing the right visa is a critical part of your move to Australia. Medical practitioners are in high demand, and several visa pathways are available for doctors.

Skills in Demand Visa (Subclass 482)

The Skills in Demand visa is the most common pathway for doctors moving to Australia. Medical practitioners are listed on the Core Skills Occupation List, which means hospitals and clinics can sponsor you under this visa. The 482 visa is typically granted for up to four years and allows you to work for your sponsoring employer. A key benefit for doctors is that medical practitioners often receive exemptions from the usual employment restrictions, meaning you may be able to work for multiple employers or as an independent contractor.

To apply, you will need a job offer from an approved Australian employer, your AHPRA registration (or evidence that it is in progress), and evidence of at least two years of relevant work experience. Your employer handles the sponsorship component, while you submit your individual visa application through the Department of Home Affairs ImmiAccount portal.

Skilled Employer Sponsored Regional Visa (Subclass 494)

If you are willing to work in regional Australia, the subclass 494 visa is an excellent option. Regional placements consistently offer higher pay rates, and this visa provides a clear pathway to permanent residency after three years. Many Pakistani doctors find that regional placements offer a better work life balance, a lower cost of living, and a supportive community environment compared to major cities.

Permanent Residency Pathways

Once you have been working in Australia on a 482 or 494 visa, you may be eligible to transition to permanent residency through the Employer Nomination Scheme (subclass 186) or the Skilled Employer Sponsored Regional visa (subclass 191). Doctors working in areas of workforce need, particularly in regional Australia, often have access to faster PR pathways. Many Pakistani doctors who initially planned a short stint in Australia end up settling permanently once they experience the lifestyle and career opportunities available.

What Pakistani Doctors Can Expect to Earn as Locums

Once you have your registration and are ready to work, the earning potential as a locum doctor in Australia is substantial. Locum rates in 2026 vary by specialty, location, and seniority, but they are significantly higher than what most doctors earn in Pakistan.

As a general guide for 2026, GP locum rates range from $1,500 to $2,200 per day in metropolitan areas, with rural and remote placements offering $1,800 to $3,000 or more per day. Resident Medical Officers and registrars typically earn $140 to $180 per hour, translating to roughly $1,100 to $1,500 per day. Specialist locum rates sit between $2,000 and $3,500 per day depending on the specialty and location.

Rural and remote placements consistently pay the highest rates, often 30 to 50 percent more than their metropolitan equivalents. If you are open to working in regional areas, even for short periods, you can significantly accelerate your savings while gaining diverse clinical experience across different hospital settings.

Settling In: Life in Australia as a Pakistani Doctor

Australia is home to a large and thriving Pakistani community, with significant populations in Sydney, Melbourne, Brisbane, and Perth. You will find familiar food, cultural events, and community organisations that can help ease the transition. Many cities have active Pakistani medical professional networks that provide mentorship, career advice, and social connections for newly arrived doctors.

The Australian healthcare system operates differently from Pakistan in several important ways. Electronic medical records are used extensively, multidisciplinary team based care is the norm, and there is a strong emphasis on patient communication and shared decision making. These differences can take some adjustment, but most Pakistani doctors find that their strong clinical training provides a solid foundation for adapting to Australian practice.

If you are coming with a family, your dependants can be included on your visa application. Partners receive full work rights in Australia, and children can attend local schools. The Australian government's Child Care Subsidy helps eligible families manage childcare costs, and Medicare (Australia's public health insurance system) provides access to free or subsidised healthcare for visa holders who are eligible.

How StatDoctor Can Help

StatDoctor is an Australian platform built to connect locum doctors directly with hospitals and GP clinics, removing the agency middleman and putting more money in your pocket. If you are a Pakistani doctor planning your move to Australia, or you have already arrived and are ready to start working, StatDoctor lets you browse locum opportunities across every state and territory, see transparent pay rates without hidden agency fees, connect directly with hospitals and health services, and manage your availability and preferences all in one place.

Whether you are still preparing for your AMC exams or you have just received your AHPRA registration and are looking for your first placement, StatDoctor makes finding locum work simple and transparent. Download the StatDoctor app today on iOS or Android, or visit statdoctor.app to create your profile and start exploring opportunities.
fieldData.post-summary: A complete guide for Pakistani doctors considering the move to Australia in 2026. Covers the AMC CAT and Clinical exams, AHPRA registration through the Standard Pathway, visa options including the Subclass 482 and 494, locum pay rates, and practical tips for settling in.

fieldData.name: Moving from Pakistan to Australia as a Doctor: AMC Exams, Registration, and Locum Work in 2026

fieldData.post-body:
Intensive care medicine sits at the sharp end of hospital care, and hospitals simply cannot function without adequate ICU staffing. With growing patient acuity, expanding critical care infrastructure, and chronic workforce shortages, locum intensivists are among the most sought-after doctors in Australia. Consultant locum rates of $2,000 to $3,000 per day are standard, and demand shows no sign of slowing. Here is what ICU locum work looks like in Australia in 2026.

Why Locum Intensive Care Doctors Are in Such High Demand

Australia's ICU workforce has been under sustained pressure for years. The COVID-19 pandemic highlighted just how critical intensive care capacity is, and the aftershocks are still being felt. Hospitals expanded their ICU beds during the pandemic, but many have struggled to recruit the permanent staff needed to maintain those expanded units. The result is a heavy reliance on locum intensivists to fill roster gaps.

Several structural factors drive the ongoing demand:

  • Australia's ageing population is generating more complex medical presentations. Older patients with multiple comorbidities are more likely to require ICU admission, and their stays tend to be longer.
  • Regional and rural hospitals face the most acute shortages. Many cannot attract permanent intensivists and depend on locum cover to keep their ICUs operational. Without locum support, some regional units would have to divert critically ill patients to larger centres, adding hours of transfer time.
  • Training an intensivist takes a long time. The College of Intensive Care Medicine of Australia and New Zealand (CICM) fellowship program requires a minimum of six years of postgraduate training, including at least 36 months of core intensive care training. That pipeline cannot expand quickly.
  • ICU rosters are demanding. The 24/7 nature of intensive care means hospitals need deep rosters to cover day shifts, night shifts, and weekends. When a single consultant goes on leave, the gap is felt immediately.
  • Private hospitals are also expanding their critical care capacity, creating additional demand for intensivists across both the public and private sectors.

Locum Intensive Care Medicine Pay Rates in 2026

Intensive care is one of the higher-paying locum specialties in Australia. The rates reflect both the level of expertise required and the urgency hospitals face when filling ICU roster gaps.

  • Locum ICU consultant day rates: $2,000 to $3,000 per day. Metro placements tend to sit at the lower end, while rural, remote, and last-minute bookings command rates toward the top of the range.
  • Locum ICU registrar rates: $130 to $250 per hour, depending on seniority, location, and shift type.
  • Locum ICU resident rates: $85 to $145 per hour.
  • On-call and after-hours loading can significantly increase total earnings, particularly in facilities where the intensivist is the sole on-call consultant.
  • Permanent ICU consultant salary: approximately $250,000 to $500,000 per year, depending on hospital type, location, and seniority.

For context, a locum intensivist working four days a week at $2,500 per day for 46 weeks would gross around $460,000 before tax. That is competitive with or above most permanent positions, though it comes without paid leave, employer superannuation contributions, or the job security of a permanent contract.

Regional and remote placements frequently include additional benefits on top of the daily rate. Accommodation, flights, and car hire are commonly covered by the hospital or agency, and some remote facilities offer daily travel allowances of $100 or more.

Where the Locum ICU Jobs Are

ICU locum demand exists in every state, but the distribution is uneven.

Queensland has some of the strongest demand, particularly in regional centres like Townsville, Cairns, Rockhampton, and Bundaberg. These hospitals serve large catchment areas and often function as the only ICU for hundreds of kilometres. Locum intensivists here manage a broad case mix, from trauma and sepsis to post-surgical complications and environmental emergencies.

New South Wales generates significant locum demand in both metro and regional settings. Western Sydney hospitals, the Central Coast, and regional centres like Dubbo, Orange, and Tamworth frequently seek locum ICU cover. Rural NSW placements tend to offer the highest rates.

Western Australia's vast geography creates unique challenges. Hospitals in Geraldton, Kalgoorlie, Bunbury, and the Kimberley region regularly need locum intensivists, and the remoteness premium pushes rates toward the top of the range. Perth metro hospitals also have gaps, though rates there are typically lower.

Victoria's major regional centres, including Geelong, Ballarat, Bendigo, and the Latrobe Valley, have ongoing ICU locum needs. Metro Melbourne hospitals tend to have larger permanent rosters but still require locum cover for leave and surge periods.

South Australia, Tasmania, and the Northern Territory round out the picture. Adelaide's major hospitals use locums to cover roster gaps, while Darwin and Hobart have smaller ICU units that can be significantly affected when even one consultant is unavailable.

What You Need to Work as a Locum Intensivist

The requirements for locum ICU work depend on your level of training and registration status.

For Specialists (Consultants)

You will need Fellowship of the College of Intensive Care Medicine (FCICM), or an equivalent qualification recognised by CICM. You must hold specialist registration with AHPRA, which allows you to practise independently. Most hospitals will also require current Advanced Life Support (ALS) certification, up-to-date CPD records, and relevant procedural skills documentation.

For Registrars

Registrars typically need to be enrolled in or eligible for the CICM training program, or hold equivalent experience. You must have general registration with AHPRA and the appropriate level of experience for the role. Some locum registrar positions require specific procedural competencies, such as central line insertion, arterial line placement, bronchoscopy, or ultrasound-guided procedures.

For International Medical Graduates

If you trained overseas, you will need to navigate the AHPRA registration process. Depending on your qualifications, you may be eligible for specialist pathway recognition through CICM, or you may need to complete AMC examinations. The Specialist Pathway allows suitably qualified international intensivists to gain specialist recognition in Australia, though the process can take several months. Area of Need positions in regional hospitals can offer a faster pathway, as these positions allow doctors to work in specific locations while completing their registration requirements.

Other Requirements

Regardless of your level, you will need professional indemnity insurance (most locum agencies arrange this or reimburse the cost), a current National Police Check, a Working with Children Check if your state requires it, and a Medicare provider number if you will be billing Medicare. Having an ABN is essential, as locum doctors are typically engaged as independent contractors.

What a Typical Locum ICU Shift Looks Like

Locum ICU work can vary significantly depending on the hospital and its size. In a large tertiary centre, you might be one of several consultants on the unit, managing a specific pod of patients with registrar and resident support. In a smaller regional hospital, you could be the sole intensivist covering the entire unit, making every admission, procedural, and end-of-life decision yourself.

A typical day shift in a regional ICU might involve a morning handover and ward round, reviewing overnight admissions, performing procedures such as central lines or chest drains, liaising with surgical and medical teams about patient management, conducting family meetings, and supervising junior staff. Night shifts and on-call periods can be quieter or extremely busy depending on the facility and season.

Locum intensivists are generally expected to hit the ground running. Hospitals rely on locums because they need someone who can function independently with minimal orientation. Strong clinical skills, adaptability, and the ability to work with unfamiliar teams are all essential.

Locum ICU Work vs Permanent ICU Positions

The decision between locum and permanent ICU work comes down to priorities.

Locum work offers higher daily rates, the ability to choose where and when you work, exposure to different hospitals and clinical environments, and the freedom to take extended breaks between assignments. For intensivists who value flexibility or want to explore different parts of Australia before committing to a location, locum work is an attractive option.

Permanent positions offer stability, employer-funded superannuation, paid leave, access to training and professional development programs, and the continuity of being part of a team. They also tend to come with clearer career progression and the opportunity to shape unit protocols and culture over time.

Many intensivists use a hybrid approach. They might hold a permanent fractional appointment at one hospital while doing locum shifts elsewhere to boost income or maintain clinical breadth. Others start their careers with a period of locum work to build experience across different settings before settling into a permanent role.

Tips for Getting Started with Locum ICU Work

If you are considering locum ICU work for the first time, here are some practical steps to set yourself up.

First, make sure your credentials are in order. Having your AHPRA registration, indemnity insurance, police check, and ABN sorted before you start looking for placements will speed up the process significantly. Hospitals and agencies move fast when they need ICU cover, and being credentialing-ready puts you at the front of the queue.

Second, consider starting with a facility you already know. Many intensivists begin their locum careers by picking up extra shifts at hospitals where they have already been credentialed. This reduces the orientation burden and lets you build a locum track record.

Third, be open to regional placements. The highest rates and the most consistent demand are in regional and remote areas. These assignments also tend to offer the broadest clinical scope, which many intensivists find professionally rewarding.

Fourth, negotiate your terms carefully. Daily rate is important, but accommodation, travel, car hire, and cancellation terms all affect the total value of an assignment. Make sure you understand what is included before accepting a placement.

How StatDoctor Can Help

StatDoctor connects locum doctors directly with hospitals and clinics across Australia, cutting out the traditional agency middleman. Instead of paying agency margins of 30% or more, hospitals on StatDoctor post their shifts directly and doctors apply. That means better transparency on rates, faster booking, and no hidden fees.

If you are an intensivist looking for locum work, StatDoctor gives you access to ICU shifts across metro and regional hospitals, the ability to set your own availability and preferred locations, direct communication with hospitals, and a streamlined credentialing process.

Download the StatDoctor app today and start browsing available ICU locum shifts in your area. Whether you are a seasoned locum or considering your first assignment, StatDoctor makes finding your next placement simple and transparent.
fieldData.post-summary: Intensive care medicine is one of the most in-demand locum specialties in Australia. With ICU consultant locum rates reaching $2,000 to $3,000 per day and persistent staffing shortages across regional and metro hospitals, locum ICU work offers serious earning potential and clinical variety.
fieldData.author: 68e360c1b6fe0fae2425baeb
fieldData.name: Locum Intensive Care Medicine Jobs in Australia: Pay Rates, Demand, and How to Get Started

fieldData.post-body:
General surgery is one of the most in-demand locum specialties in Australia, particularly in rural and regional hospitals where permanent surgical coverage remains a persistent challenge. With locum rates of $2,000 to $3,500 per day, a broad clinical scope, and strong demand across every state, general surgery offers locum doctors an excellent combination of earning potential and professional variety. Here is what you need to know about working as a locum general surgeon in Australia in 2026.

Why Locum General Surgeons Are in Such High Demand

General surgery sits at the core of hospital operations. Without a general surgeon on site, a hospital cannot run emergency laparotomies, manage acute abdomens, treat trauma patients, or maintain elective surgical lists. When a permanent surgeon goes on leave, retires, or simply cannot be recruited, the entire surgical service stalls. That is why hospitals turn to locum general surgeons to keep their theatres running.

Several factors are driving sustained demand in 2026:

  • Only 20% of general surgeons work in rural areas, despite 29% of Australia's population living outside major metropolitan centres. This geographical maldistribution creates constant locum vacancies in regional hospitals.
  • According to recent RACS data, 381 general surgeons worked rurally compared with 1,572 in metropolitan areas. The gap is widening as the overall surgical workforce grows but concentrates in cities.
  • Over 21% of rural general surgeons are aged over 55, meaning retirement will further reduce an already thin workforce in coming years.
  • International Medical Graduates make up 38% of rural general surgeons. While they are essential to the system, visa and registration complexities create turnover, and locum cover is needed during transition periods.

The 2025-26 Federal Budget allocated $1.8 billion to public hospitals to cut waiting lists and manage emergency department pressures, which is likely to increase demand for surgical locums as hospitals work to clear surgical backlogs.

What Locum General Surgeons Earn in Australia

General surgery locum rates are among the highest across all specialties, reflecting the critical nature of the role and the difficulty of recruitment.

  • Metro locum rates: $2,000 to $2,500 per day for elective and emergency general surgery lists in capital city public and private hospitals.
  • Regional locum rates: $2,500 to $3,000 per day in mid-sized hospitals in places like Dubbo, Bundaberg, Ballarat, and Mount Gambier.
  • Rural and remote rates: $3,000 to $3,500+ per day in locations like Mount Isa, Alice Springs, the Pilbara, and Far North Queensland. Rates can exceed this for last-minute or hard-to-fill placements.
  • On-call and after-hours loading can push daily earnings well above these base rates, particularly in hospitals where you are the sole surgeon on site overnight.

Annual salaries for permanent public hospital general surgery consultants typically sit between $300,000 and $450,000. A locum general surgeon working four days a week at $2,800 per day for 46 weeks would gross approximately $515,000 before tax and expenses. The premium reflects the trade-off: no paid leave, no employer superannuation contributions in most contractor arrangements, and the responsibility of managing your own tax, insurance, and continuing professional development.

Most rural locum contracts also include flights, accommodation, and a rental car at no cost to the surgeon, which effectively adds thousands of dollars in value to each placement.

Where the Best Locum General Surgery Opportunities Are

Rural and Remote Hospitals

The highest-paying and most readily available locum general surgery positions are in rural and remote Australia. Hospitals in the Northern Territory, western Queensland, outback New South Wales, rural Western Australia, and outback South Australia frequently advertise for locum general surgeons. These placements often involve a wide scope of practice, including emergency surgery, endoscopy, trauma management, and elective procedures. Research published in the Medical Journal of Australia shows that surgical outcomes in rural settings are comparable to metropolitan centres for most non-oncological conditions, so the clinical standard is high.

Regional Centres

Mid-sized regional hospitals offer a balance of clinical variety, competitive rates, and better access to amenities than truly remote locations. Towns like Wagga Wagga, Bendigo, Rockhampton, Geraldton, Launceston, and Port Macquarie all have hospitals that regularly require locum general surgery cover. These facilities often have one or two permanent surgeons and bring in locums to cover leave, conference attendance, or to address growing waiting lists.

Private Hospitals

Metropolitan and regional private hospitals hire locum general surgeons for elective lists, endoscopy sessions, and day surgery. Rates are generally lower than remote public hospital work, but the conditions are predictable: well-resourced theatres, defined operating lists, and no overnight on-call requirements in most cases.

Qualifications You Need to Work as a Locum General Surgeon

FRACS in General Surgery

The primary qualification for working as an independent locum general surgeon is Fellowship of the Royal Australasian College of Surgeons (FRACS) in General Surgery. This involves completing the Surgical Education and Training (SET) program, which spans 48 months of accredited surgical training after passing the Generic Surgical Sciences Examination (GSSE). The pathway culminates in the Fellowship Examination, which includes written papers and clinical viva components assessing competency equivalent to a consultant surgeon in their first year of independent practice.

If you hold FRACS with specialist registration through AHPRA, you are eligible for the full range of locum general surgery work across public, private, metro, and rural settings.

International Medical Graduates

If you trained overseas and hold a surgical fellowship from a recognised jurisdiction, you may be eligible for specialist registration through AHPRA via the Standard, Competent Authority, or Specialist pathway. The Competent Authority pathway allows surgeons from the UK, Ireland, Canada, and the US to register without sitting AMC exams, provided they have completed their fellowship and meet recency of practice requirements. The Expedited Specialist Registration pathway is also available for certain specialties where workforce shortages are acute.

Processing times through AHPRA can vary, but once you hold specialist registration, locum work is immediately available.

What You Will Need to Get Set Up

Before you take your first locum general surgery shift, you will need the following in place:

  1. AHPRA specialist registration in general surgery.
  2. An ABN, as most locum surgical work is contractor-based.
  3. Medical indemnity insurance that covers the full scope of your surgical practice, including emergency and after-hours work. Confirm your policy covers the specific procedures you expect to perform.
  4. Current CPD compliance through RACS. The RACS CPD program runs annually, and compliance is required to maintain your fellowship.
  5. Standard credentialing documents: national police check, Working with Children Check (state-specific), vaccination records, and at least two recent clinical referees.
  6. Evidence of recency of practice, particularly if you are transitioning to locum work after a career break or change in clinical role.

The Clinical Scope of Locum General Surgery

One of the appeals of locum general surgery is the breadth of clinical work. In rural and regional settings, you are often the only surgeon and the scope reflects that. You may find yourself performing appendicectomies, cholecystectomies, hernia repairs, bowel resections, endoscopies, and managing acute trauma all within the same week.

In metropolitan settings, the scope may be more focused. Private hospital locum work often centres on elective lists for specific procedures, while public hospital locums may involve a mix of emergency and elective cover depending on the roster.

This variety is a key reason many surgeons are drawn to locum work. Rather than the repetition that can come with a fixed permanent position, locum general surgery keeps your skills sharp across the full breadth of the specialty.

The Lifestyle Considerations

Locum general surgery offers financial rewards and clinical variety, but it is important to go in with your eyes open. Rural placements mean time away from home, potentially unpredictable hours, and being the primary surgical resource for an entire region. On-call responsibilities in smaller hospitals can be demanding, and there is no backup surgeon down the corridor.

On the other hand, locum work gives you control over your schedule. You choose which placements to accept, how many weeks you work per year, and where you want to go. Many locum surgeons structure their year around blocks of intensive work followed by extended breaks for travel, family time, or professional development. It is a different rhythm from permanent work, and one that suits many surgeons at various stages of their career.

How StatDoctor Can Help

StatDoctor connects locum general surgeons directly with hospitals across Australia, cutting out the agency middleman. You set your own rates, choose your own placements, and deal directly with the hospitals that need you. There are no agency markups eating into your earnings or the hospital's budget. Download the StatDoctor app to browse available general surgery locum shifts, set your availability, and start connecting with hospitals today.
fieldData.post-summary: Locum general surgery jobs in Australia pay $2,000 to $3,500+ per day. Learn about FRACS requirements, where demand is highest across rural and metro settings, and how to get started.

fieldData.name: Locum General Surgery Jobs in Australia: Pay Rates, Demand, and How to Get Started

fieldData.post-body:
Orthopaedic surgery is one of the highest paying and most in demand locum specialties in Australia right now. If you are a qualified orthopaedic surgeon or advanced trainee looking for flexible work, strong daily rates, and the chance to operate across a range of hospitals and regions, the locum market in 2026 has a lot to offer.

Between growing elective surgery waitlists, an ageing population placing increasing pressure on joint replacement and fracture services, and persistent staffing shortages in regional and rural hospitals, the need for locum orthopaedic surgeons shows no sign of slowing down. This guide covers what you can expect to earn, where the demand is, what you need to get started, and how to make the most of orthopaedic locum work in Australia.

What Locum Orthopaedic Surgeons Earn in Australia in 2026

Orthopaedic surgery sits at the top end of the locum pay scale. Daily rates for consultant orthopaedic surgeons doing locum work typically range from $3,000 to $5,000 per day, depending on the location, the complexity of the caseload, and whether the role includes on call responsibilities.

Here is a general breakdown of what locum orthopaedic surgeons are earning in 2026:

Metro hospital locum rates: $2,500 to $3,500 per day. Metropolitan placements in Sydney, Melbourne, Brisbane, and Perth tend to sit at the lower end of the range, though they often come with higher case volumes and access to subspecialty work.

Regional hospital locum rates: $3,000 to $4,500 per day. Regional centres like Townsville, Cairns, Bendigo, Bunbury, and Launceston frequently advertise orthopaedic locum positions with accommodation and travel included on top of the daily rate.

Remote and rural locum rates: $4,000 to $5,000+ per day. Hospitals in areas like the Pilbara, Far North Queensland, and outback South Australia pay premium rates. These placements usually include flights, accommodation, and a car.

For context, the average annual salary for a permanently employed orthopaedic surgeon in Australia sits around $589,000 per year, with experienced surgeons in mixed public and private practice earning well above $1 million annually. Locum work offers comparable or higher effective daily earnings, with the added flexibility of choosing when and where you work.

Why Demand for Locum Orthopaedic Surgeons Is So High

Several factors are driving the strong demand for orthopaedic locums across Australia in 2026.

Elective Surgery Backlogs

The elective surgery backlog that built up during the pandemic years has not fully cleared. Orthopaedic procedures, particularly joint replacements and arthroscopies, make up a significant portion of elective surgery waitlists nationally. State governments have been funding additional theatre sessions and dedicated catch up lists, and hospitals need locum surgeons to staff them.

An Ageing Population

Australia's population continues to age, and with that comes a rising incidence of osteoarthritis, fragility fractures, and degenerative spinal conditions. The Australian Institute of Health and Welfare reports that musculoskeletal conditions are one of the leading causes of disability in Australia, affecting around 7 million people. This trend is not going to reverse, meaning long term demand for orthopaedic services will continue to grow.

Workforce Gaps in Regional and Rural Areas

Many regional hospitals struggle to recruit permanent orthopaedic surgeons. Towns like Mount Isa, Broken Hill, Geraldton, and Alice Springs rely heavily on locum coverage to maintain their fracture and trauma services. Even mid sized regional centres with established surgical departments often have gaps in their rosters that need locum backfill, particularly for on call and weekend coverage.

Subspecialisation and Leave Cover

As orthopaedic surgery becomes more subspecialised (hand, shoulder, spine, arthroplasty, trauma, sports medicine), hospitals need locums with specific skill sets to cover planned leave. A locum with fellowship training in arthroplasty or upper limb surgery, for example, can command higher rates and access a wider range of placements.

Where Locum Orthopaedic Jobs Are Available

Locum orthopaedic work is available across every state and territory, but certain regions consistently have the most opportunities.

Queensland

Queensland is one of the busiest states for orthopaedic locum work. The state's vast geography means hospitals from Cairns to the Gold Coast and west to Mount Isa regularly need locum cover. Queensland Health facilities frequently advertise for orthopaedic surgeons to cover trauma rosters, elective lists, and outpatient clinics. The Townsville University Hospital, Cairns Hospital, and Rockhampton Hospital are common placements.

New South Wales

NSW has strong demand in both its metropolitan and rural Local Health Districts. Western NSW (Dubbo, Orange, Bathurst), the Hunter New England region, and the Murrumbidgee area are consistent sources of orthopaedic locum work. Sydney's major trauma centres occasionally seek locum cover as well, though competition for metro placements can be higher.

Western Australia

WA's mining regions in the Pilbara and Kimberley generate high rates of occupational and trauma related orthopaedic presentations. Hospitals in Karratha, Port Hedland, Broome, and Kalgoorlie frequently seek locum orthopaedic surgeons, and the rates reflect the remoteness. Perth's tertiary hospitals also use locums for roster backfill.

Victoria

Regional Victoria offers steady orthopaedic locum opportunities in centres like Ballarat, Bendigo, Shepparton, and the Latrobe Valley. Melbourne's outer suburban hospitals, including Frankston, Dandenong, and Sunshine, also run locum orthopaedic lists.

South Australia, Tasmania, and the Territories

SA's regional LHNs, particularly in the Eyre and Far North and Flinders and Upper North regions, have ongoing need for surgical locums including orthopaedics. Tasmania's two main hospitals in Hobart and Launceston use locum cover regularly. The Northern Territory, especially Royal Darwin Hospital and Alice Springs Hospital, consistently offers some of the highest paying locum orthopaedic positions in the country.

What You Need to Work as a Locum Orthopaedic Surgeon

Before you can start taking locum orthopaedic shifts, you will need to have the following in order.

AHPRA Registration

You must hold current registration with the Medical Board of Australia via AHPRA. For orthopaedic surgery, this means specialist registration as a Fellow of the Royal Australasian College of Surgeons (FRACS) in orthopaedic surgery. International medical graduates may be eligible through the Specialist Pathway, the Competent Authority Pathway (for surgeons from the UK, Ireland, US, Canada, and other approved countries), or the Expedited Specialist Registration pathway if your specialty is on the approved list.

Medical Indemnity Insurance

Orthopaedic surgery carries higher indemnity premiums than many other specialties due to the procedural nature of the work. You will need a policy that covers locum work specifically, and the premium will vary depending on whether your practice includes spinal surgery, trauma, or paediatric orthopaedics. Major providers like MDA National, Avant, and MIGA all offer locum specific policies.

Hospital Credentialing

Each hospital or health service where you work will require credentialing. This involves submitting your qualifications, training history, logbook data, referee reports, and proof of insurance. Start the credentialing process well in advance of when you want to begin, as it can take anywhere from two to eight weeks depending on the facility.

ABN and Financial Setup

Most locum orthopaedic surgeons operate as sole traders or through a company structure (Pty Ltd). You will need an ABN, and if your annual turnover exceeds $75,000 (which it almost certainly will), you must register for GST. Setting up the right business structure early can save you significant money at tax time.

Tips for Making the Most of Locum Orthopaedic Work

Build Relationships with Key Hospitals

Hospitals that have worked with you before will invite you back. Building a reputation as a reliable, skilled, and easy to work with locum surgeon is the fastest way to ensure a steady stream of placements. Once a hospital knows your work, the credentialing process for return visits is also much simpler.

Be Clear About Your Scope of Practice

Orthopaedic surgery is broad. Be upfront with hospitals about what you do and do not do. If you are a joint replacement surgeon who does not cover hand trauma, make that clear early. This protects you and the hospital, and ensures the placement is a good fit.

Consider Mixing Metro and Regional Placements

A common strategy among locum orthopaedic surgeons is to mix higher paying regional placements with metro work closer to home. This gives you the financial benefit of premium regional rates while maintaining your professional networks and access to subspecialty cases in the city.

Stay on Top of CPD

RACS requires all Fellows to meet Continuing Professional Development requirements, including locums. Make sure your CPD is current, as hospitals will check this during credentialing. The 2026 CPD framework requires a minimum of 100 hours across defined categories per year.

How StatDoctor Can Help

StatDoctor connects locum doctors directly with hospitals and clinics across Australia, cutting out the agency middleman. Instead of paying 20 to 30 per cent in agency commissions, you keep more of your earnings while hospitals get the coverage they need at a lower cost.

Whether you are an experienced orthopaedic surgeon looking for your next locum placement, or a newly Fellowed FRACS ready to try locum work for the first time, StatDoctor makes it simple to find, book, and manage your shifts.

Download the StatDoctor app today on the App Store or Google Play and start browsing orthopaedic locum opportunities near you. No agency fees, no middleman, just direct connections between doctors and hospitals.
fieldData.post-summary: Locum orthopaedic surgery jobs in Australia pay $3,000 to $5,000+ per day in 2026. With elective surgery backlogs, an ageing population, and persistent workforce gaps, demand for orthopaedic locums is strong across metro, regional, and rural hospitals.

fieldData.name: Locum Orthopaedic Surgery Jobs in Australia: Pay Rates, Demand, and How to Get Started

fieldData.post-body:
If you are working as a locum doctor in Australia, or thinking about making the switch, one of the most important skills you can develop has nothing to do with medicine. It is the ability to negotiate your rate. Unlike permanent positions where salaries are largely fixed by award agreements and enterprise bargaining, locum rates are negotiable. The difference between accepting the first offer and knowing how to push back can amount to tens of thousands of dollars over the course of a year.

This guide walks you through the practical steps to negotiate a better locum rate in 2026, with real market data, timing strategies, and tips that work whether you are dealing with an agency or going direct through a platform like StatDoctor.

Know the Market: What Locum Doctors Actually Earn in 2026

Before you negotiate anything, you need to know what the market is paying. Walking into a negotiation without data is like diagnosing without a history. Here is a snapshot of 2026 locum rates across Australia to give you a baseline.

For resident and registrar level doctors, average hourly rates sit between $140 and $200 per hour, with daily rates ranging from $1,100 to $1,600. Registrars in high demand specialties like anaesthetics or emergency medicine can push above $2,000 per day, especially in regional placements.

Consultant locum doctors are averaging around $230 per hour nationally, with daily rates between $1,800 and $3,500. The highest paying specialty for consultants right now is psychiatry, where average rates have climbed to $294 per hour following the NSW 20% pay increase introduced by the Industrial Relations Commission in late 2025.

Rural and remote placements consistently offer a premium. Locum doctors working in regional or remote areas can expect rates 30% to 50% higher than metropolitan equivalents. A GP locum earning $1,500 per day in Sydney might command $2,200 or more for a similar role in outback Queensland or the Northern Territory.

Timing Your Negotiation for Maximum Leverage

When you negotiate matters almost as much as how you negotiate. Hospitals and clinics experience predictable surges in locum demand throughout the year, and understanding these patterns gives you a significant advantage.

Peak Demand Periods

December and January are consistently the highest paying months for locum doctors in Australia. Holiday season means permanent staff are on leave, and hospitals scramble to fill rosters. If you are available over Christmas and New Year, you are in a strong position to negotiate premium rates.

The end of financial year (June) is another peak period. Budget cycles create urgency, and departments that have unspent staffing budgets may be willing to pay above market rate to fill gaps before the new financial year begins.

Specialty Shortages Create Leverage

If you work in a specialty experiencing acute shortages, your negotiating power increases substantially. In 2026, psychiatry, emergency medicine, anaesthetics, and obstetrics and gynaecology are all experiencing workforce pressures that show no sign of easing. The NSW government alone spent over $43 million on locum recruitment in each of the past two financial years, driven largely by psychiatrist vacancies. If you hold fellowship in one of these specialties, the market is firmly in your favour.

How to Negotiate Your Locum Rate: Step by Step

Step 1: Research Before You Respond

When you receive a rate offer, resist the urge to accept or reject immediately. Instead, take time to research. Check current market rates for your specialty, level, and the specific location. Platforms like Medlo publish live rate data, and StatDoctor allows you to compare offers directly. Ask colleagues in your specialty what they are being paid for similar roles. The locum community in Australia is more connected than you might think, and most doctors are happy to share rate information informally.

Step 2: Start Higher Than Your Target

A common negotiation principle applies here: your opening figure should be above what you would actually accept. If market data suggests a fair rate is $200 per hour for your role, consider opening at $230 to $240. This gives you room to come down while still landing at or above your target. The worst that can happen is they say no and counter with something closer to the market rate.

Step 3: Negotiate the Full Package, Not Just the Hourly Rate

Your hourly or daily rate is only one component of your total compensation. Smart locum doctors negotiate the full package, which can include travel and accommodation (flights, car hire, hospital housing or serviced apartments), meal allowances during placement, overtime and on call rates, penalty rates for weekends, public holidays, and night shifts, and the length and flexibility of the contract itself.

A placement that pays $180 per hour but covers all travel, accommodation, and meals can be worth significantly more than a $200 per hour role where you are covering those expenses yourself. Always calculate the net value of the total package before comparing offers.

Step 4: Be Direct and Professional

Australian hospitals and recruiters generally respond well to straightforward communication. You do not need to play games or use high pressure tactics. A simple approach works: "Based on current market rates for my specialty and the location of this placement, I would be looking for $X per hour. Is there flexibility on the rate?" If they cannot move on the rate, ask what else they can offer. Many facilities have more flexibility on accommodation, travel, or shift structure than they do on the headline rate.

Step 5: Get Everything in Writing

Once you have agreed on terms, make sure every detail is documented in your contract or engagement letter. This includes the agreed rate, shift times, on call expectations, accommodation arrangements, travel reimbursement, cancellation terms, and payment timelines. Verbal agreements are common in locum recruitment, but they leave you exposed if anything changes. A written contract protects both parties.

Common Mistakes That Cost Locum Doctors Money

There are a few patterns that consistently lead to locum doctors leaving money on the table. The first is accepting the first offer without questioning it. Agencies and hospitals almost always have room to move on their initial offer, even if it is only by $10 to $20 per hour. Over a 12 month period of regular locum work, that difference adds up to thousands.

Another common mistake is failing to account for superannuation. If you are working via ABN as a sole trader or through a Pty Ltd structure, the quoted rate typically includes your super obligation. That means you need to set aside 11.5% of your earnings for superannuation yourself. A rate that looks generous on paper can feel less impressive once you have factored in super, GST obligations, insurance, and other business costs.

Finally, many locum doctors undervalue their niche skills. If you have procedural skills, additional qualifications, or experience in areas like indigenous health, addiction medicine, or rural emergency care, these are worth a premium. Do not assume the standard rate applies to you if you bring something extra to the table.

Going Direct vs Using an Agency

How you find your locum work also affects what you earn. Traditional recruitment agencies take a margin on every placement, typically between 15% and 30% of the total billing rate. That means if a hospital is paying $300 per hour for your services, the agency may be taking $45 to $90 of that before you see a cent.

Platforms like StatDoctor are changing this model by connecting locum doctors directly with hospitals and clinics, cutting out the middleman and allowing more of the billing rate to flow through to the doctor. When you negotiate directly with a facility, both sides benefit. The hospital pays less overall, and you take home more. It is a straightforward value proposition that is driving a growing number of doctors away from traditional agency models.

How StatDoctor Can Help

StatDoctor is built to give locum doctors more control over their careers and their earnings. By connecting you directly with hospitals and GP clinics across Australia, StatDoctor removes the agency margin and puts the negotiating power back in your hands. You can browse available shifts, compare rates by location and specialty, and communicate directly with hiring facilities.

Whether you are a seasoned locum looking to maximise your income or a doctor considering your first locum placement, StatDoctor gives you the transparency and tools you need to make informed decisions about your work. Download the StatDoctor app today and start taking control of your locum career.
fieldData.post-summary: Not sure if you are being paid what you are worth as a locum doctor? This practical guide covers how to research market rates, time your negotiations, and leverage your skills to negotiate a higher locum rate in Australia in 2026.

fieldData.name: How to Negotiate Your Locum Rate in Australia: A Practical Guide for 2026

fieldData.post-body:
If you have ever lined up a locum placement only to wait weeks for hospital credentialing to come through, you are not alone. Credentialing is the process every locum doctor in Australia must go through before stepping foot on a hospital ward, and it can be one of the most frustrating parts of locum work. The good news is that once you understand what is involved and get your documentation in order, you can significantly cut down the waiting time and keep your schedule full.

This guide covers everything you need to know about hospital credentialing for locum doctors in Australia, from the documents you will need to practical strategies for speeding up the process.

What Is Hospital Credentialing and Why Does It Matter?

Hospital credentialing is the formal process by which a health facility verifies that a doctor has the qualifications, experience, and professional standing to practise safely within that facility. It is separate from your AHPRA registration. While AHPRA confirms you are legally allowed to practise medicine in Australia, credentialing confirms that a specific hospital or health service is satisfied you can work there.

Every public hospital and most private facilities in Australia require credentialing before a locum doctor can begin clinical duties. This applies regardless of your seniority or how many times you have worked at similar facilities. The process exists to protect patients, but it also protects you by ensuring you are covered under the facility's governance framework.

For locum doctors, credentialing can feel like a bottleneck. Unlike permanent staff who go through the process once, locums often need to be credentialed at each new facility. Some health districts share credentialing across their hospitals, but this is not universal. In many cases, you will need to submit a full application for every new placement.

The Standard Hospital Credentialing Requirements

While requirements vary slightly between states and individual hospitals, the core documentation is fairly consistent across Australia. Here is what you will typically need to provide.

AHPRA Registration

Your current AHPRA registration is the foundation of every credentialing application. Hospitals will verify your registration type (general, specialist, or limited), any conditions or undertakings, and your registration expiry date. Make sure your registration is up to date and that you can provide your AHPRA registration number and a current registration certificate.

Curriculum Vitae

A current, detailed CV is essential. Hospitals want to see your full employment history with no unexplained gaps. Include your qualifications, specialist training, current scope of practice, and any relevant procedural skills. Keep your CV updated after every placement so you are not scrambling to fill in details at the last minute.

Professional References

Most hospitals require a minimum of three professional references, including at least one from a recent supervisor or head of department. References should be from doctors who can speak to your clinical competence and professional conduct. Referees who are slow to respond are one of the most common causes of credentialing delays, so it is worth having a conversation with your referees in advance and keeping a list of at least four or five reliable contacts.

National Police Check

A current National Police Check (NPC) is required for almost every hospital placement. Most facilities accept an NPC that is less than 12 months old, though some may require one issued within the last three to six months. You can apply for an NPC through the Australian Criminal Intelligence Commission or an accredited body. Processing typically takes one to three business days for a clean result, but can take longer if there are common name matches or if you have lived overseas.

Working With Children Check

If your placement involves any contact with patients under 18, you will need a Working With Children Check (WWCC). The name and process vary by state. In NSW it is a Working With Children Check, in Victoria it is a Working With Children Check Card, and in Queensland it is a Blue Card. These checks can take several weeks to process, so apply well before you need it. A WWCC is typically valid for five years.

Immunisation Records

Hospitals require evidence of immunity to a range of infectious diseases. The standard panel includes Hepatitis B (with serology showing immunity), measles, mumps, rubella, varicella, pertussis (whooping cough), and tuberculosis screening. Some facilities also require annual influenza vaccination and COVID-19 vaccination records. Keep a centralised immunisation record that you can attach to every application.

Medical Indemnity Insurance

You will need to show evidence of current professional indemnity insurance that covers your scope of practice as a locum. If you are working through an agency, the agency may provide indemnity cover, but you should always verify the policy details, including the coverage limits, the insurer, and whether it covers the specific type of work you will be doing. If you are working independently through a platform like StatDoctor, you will typically arrange your own indemnity insurance through providers like MDA National, Avant, or MIPS.

Additional Documents

Depending on the facility, you may also need to provide your Medicare provider number, evidence of CPD compliance, specialist college fellowship certificates, proof of identity (passport or driver licence), and any relevant procedural logbooks or competency records. Some hospitals also require completion of their own orientation modules or online training packages before your first shift.

How Long Does Hospital Credentialing Take?

The honest answer is that it depends. A straightforward credentialing application with all documents in order can be processed in as little as five to seven business days at some facilities. However, the average turnaround across Australian hospitals sits closer to three to four weeks, and it is not unusual for the process to stretch to six weeks or longer at larger health services with high volumes of applications.

The most common causes of delay include incomplete applications (missing documents or unsigned forms), slow referee responses, expired police checks or immunisation records, and backlogs in the hospital's medical administration team. State health services that process credentialing centrally for multiple hospitals tend to have longer processing times simply due to volume.

If you are planning your locum calendar, it is wise to factor in a minimum four week lead time for credentialing at a new facility. For facilities you have worked at before, the recredentialing process is usually faster, often two to three weeks.

How to Speed Up the Credentialing Process

While you cannot control how fast a hospital processes your application, you can control how quickly and completely you submit it. Here are practical strategies that experienced locum doctors use to minimise credentialing delays.

Build a Credentialing Folder

Create a digital folder (cloud storage works well) containing up to date copies of every document you might need. This should include your AHPRA certificate, CV, police check, WWCC, immunisation records, indemnity certificate, Medicare provider number, ID documents, and specialist certificates. Update this folder regularly so you are always ready to submit a complete application within hours of confirming a placement.

Keep Your Police Check Current

Rather than waiting until a hospital requests it, renew your National Police Check every six months. This costs around $40 to $55 each time, but it eliminates one of the most common sources of delay. Some locum doctors set a calendar reminder to renew their NPC twice a year.

Pre-Brief Your Referees

Contact your referees at the start of each locum period and let them know they may receive reference requests. Give them a heads up about which hospitals might contact them and ask them to respond within 48 hours if possible. A referee who takes two weeks to return a form can single-handedly stall your entire credentialing process.

Apply to Multiple Facilities Early

If you know you want to work across several hospitals in a region, start the credentialing process for all of them at the same time. Do not wait until one placement ends to begin credentialing at the next facility. Running multiple applications in parallel means you always have options when one placement wraps up.

Ask About Mutual Recognition

Some Local Health Districts (LHDs) in NSW, Hospital and Health Services (HHSs) in Queensland, and health networks in other states have mutual credentialing arrangements. This means that if you are credentialed at one hospital in the network, you may be able to work at other hospitals in the same network without going through the full process again. Always ask about this when you are applying, as it can save you significant time.

Follow Up Proactively

Do not assume that submitting your application means everything is progressing. Follow up with the hospital's medical administration team one week after submission to confirm your application is complete and in the queue. A polite follow up email can flag any missing documents early and keep your application moving.

State by State Credentialing Differences

While the core requirements are similar nationwide, there are some state specific nuances worth knowing about.

In New South Wales, credentialing is managed at the Local Health District level. NSW Health has standardised forms and processes, but individual LHDs may have additional requirements. The NSW Health Compliance Certificate is a common requirement for locum placements in public hospitals.

In Victoria, credentialing and scope of clinical practice are governed by the Victorian Credentialling and Scope of Clinical Practice Framework. Health services in Victoria tend to have well structured processes, though processing times can vary significantly between metropolitan and regional facilities.

In Queensland, Hospital and Health Services manage their own credentialing. Queensland requires a Blue Card for working with children and young people, which can take several weeks to process. Some HHSs in Queensland have been early adopters of streamlined digital credentialing processes.

In Western Australia, the WA Health system manages credentialing through individual health services. WA has been working on standardising credentialing processes across its health services, which should make the process more consistent for locums working across the state.

In South Australia and Tasmania, the smaller number of health services means credentialing tends to be more centralised, which can work in your favour if you are planning to work across multiple facilities in these states.

In the Northern Territory, credentialing processes are managed by the NT Department of Health. Given the territory's reliance on locum doctors, particularly in remote areas, NT Health is generally efficient at processing credentialing applications, though you should still allow adequate lead time.

What Happens If Your Credentialing Is Rejected?

Credentialing rejection is uncommon for doctors with clean registration and appropriate qualifications, but it does happen. Common reasons include conditions or restrictions on your AHPRA registration that conflict with the intended scope of practice, gaps in your CV that cannot be satisfactorily explained, unsatisfactory references, or failure to meet immunisation requirements.

If your application is declined, you should receive written reasons from the facility. You can usually address the issues and reapply. In some cases, a facility may offer a more limited scope of clinical practice rather than an outright rejection, allowing you to work in a reduced capacity while outstanding issues are resolved.

The Future of Credentialing for Locums

There is growing recognition across the Australian healthcare system that the current credentialing process is inefficient and creates unnecessary barriers to workforce mobility. Several initiatives are underway to improve the situation.

Digital credentialing platforms are gaining traction, with some health services adopting electronic systems that allow doctors to maintain a verified digital credentials profile. The concept of a national portable credentialing system has been discussed at various levels of government, though implementation remains a work in progress.

Some forward thinking hospitals are also building pre-credentialed locum pools, maintaining databases of doctors who have already been through the credentialing process and can be deployed quickly when a need arises. This approach benefits both the hospital (faster access to locums) and the doctor (less paperwork and faster start dates).

How StatDoctor Can Help

One of the biggest advantages of using StatDoctor is that we simplify the connection between locum doctors and hospitals. When you create your profile on the StatDoctor app, you can upload all your credentialing documents in one place, making it easy to share them directly with hospitals when you apply for shifts.

Because StatDoctor connects you directly with hospitals and GP clinics rather than going through a traditional agency, you have more visibility into the credentialing process and can communicate directly with the facility's administration team. This direct relationship often means faster turnaround times compared to the traditional agency model, where your documents might sit in a queue before being forwarded.

StatDoctor also helps you keep track of document expiry dates so you are never caught off guard by an expired police check or lapsed indemnity certificate. Download the StatDoctor app today on the App Store or Google Play and take control of your locum career, starting with getting credentialed faster.
fieldData.post-summary: Hospital credentialing is one of the biggest hurdles for locum doctors in Australia. This guide breaks down exactly what documents you need, how long the process takes, and practical tips to get credentialed faster so you can start earning sooner.
fieldData.author: 68e360c1b6fe0fae2425baeb
fieldData.name: Hospital Credentialing for Locum Doctors in Australia: What You Need and How to Speed It Up

fieldData.post-body:
Internal medicine is one of the busiest and most consistently in-demand specialties across Australian public hospitals, and the locum market for general physicians reflects that reality clearly. Whether you are a Fellow of RACP looking for flexible work between permanent roles or an overseas-trained physician navigating your registration pathway, locum internal medicine jobs in Australia offer strong daily rates, genuine clinical variety, and the freedom to choose when and where you work. Demand has only intensified in 2026, with workforce data pointing to a shortfall of more than 13,000 doctors nationally and hospitals in every state actively seeking cover for general medicine wards. This guide covers what you need to know about pay, credentialing, demand hotspots, and how to land your first or next locum internal medicine placement.

Why Demand for Locum Internal Medicine Doctors Is Growing in 2026

General medicine sits at the centre of most public hospital workflows. Patients admitted through the emergency department without a clear specialty home are typically admitted under general medicine, and wards fill quickly during winter respiratory seasons, post-holiday periods, and any time a permanent consultant takes extended leave. The result is that hospital medical directors regularly need short-notice cover, often at competitive rates.

Australia's broader workforce picture adds further pressure. The country currently needs an estimated 13,000 additional doctors to meet existing demand, and the pipeline from domestic training programs has not kept pace. International Medical Graduates make up 32 per cent of Australia's medical workforce and more than half of all rural doctors, yet many still face delays in obtaining full AHPRA registration. That structural gap keeps locum demand elevated across most hospital categories, and internal medicine is no exception.

The ageing Australian population is another driver. Older patients typically present with multiple comorbidities and complex, prolonged admissions, which pushes up bed-days under general medicine and increases the total hours of consultant cover hospitals require. Unlike some surgical specialties where elective lists can be deferred, medical wards carry patients who cannot simply wait for a permanent appointment to become available.

What Locum Internal Medicine Jobs Look Like in Practice

Types of Roles Available

Most locum internal medicine positions fall into one of three categories. Ward-based consultant cover involves taking responsibility for a general medicine ward, attending daily ward rounds, managing admissions from ED, and handling after-hours calls on a rostered basis. You will typically work alongside registrars and resident medical officers, so your role is primarily supervisory and decision-making rather than procedural.

Outpatient clinic cover is the second common format. Some hospitals book locum consultants specifically for their ambulatory care clinics, managing follow-up patients and new referrals from general practice. These shifts are generally more predictable in hours and workload, and they can suit physicians who prefer clinic-based rather than ward-based work.

Short-term acute cover, sometimes called acute physician cover, is the third category. You act as the receiving consultant for new medical admissions, clearing a waiting list of patients from ED and doing clerking reviews before handing over to the primary team. Acute cover shifts are common in larger metropolitan hospitals and tend to attract slightly higher rates given the pace and complexity involved.

Shift Lengths and Typical Arrangements

Locum internal medicine assignments range from a single day through to blocks of several months. Single-day or two-to-three-day cover is common when a consultant goes on annual or sick leave and the hospital needs last-minute replacement. Block contracts of two to four weeks are increasingly popular for regional hospitals that are recruiting permanently but need to fill the gap while the process runs its course. Longer placements of three to six months are available and can be highly lucrative, particularly in regional and remote settings where daily rates and accommodation allowances both increase significantly.

Locum Internal Medicine Pay Rates in Australia 2026

Pay rates for locum internal medicine positions sit comfortably above the average for generalist hospital roles, reflecting the breadth of clinical responsibility involved.

Metropolitan Hospitals

In major city hospitals, locum general physicians typically earn between $2,000 and $2,500 per day for standard weekday consultant cover. Weekend and public holiday shifts attract loading, and it is common for rates to reach $2,800 to $3,200 per day on a Saturday, Sunday, or public holiday. Acute physician cover in fast-paced metropolitan EDs or acute medical units can command slightly higher rates given the intensity of the work.

Regional and Remote Placements

Regional and remote hospitals consistently offer the highest daily rates for locum internal medicine doctors. Daily rates of $3,000 to $4,000 are common in towns where the hospital has struggled to fill a permanent position, and some facilities add accommodation, meals, and return flights to the package. If you are willing to work in a more remote setting, the financial case is compelling. A four-week block at $3,500 per day represents gross earnings of approximately $98,000 before tax, and many associated travel and accommodation costs are deductible as business expenses if you are operating through an ABN or Pty Ltd structure.

It is worth noting that rates vary based on your seniority, your fellowship status, and whether an agency is taking a margin. On StatDoctor, there are no agency fees charged to doctors, which means the rate you see is the rate you actually receive.

Credentialing and Registration Requirements for Locum Internal Medicine Jobs

AHPRA Registration

To practice as a consultant physician in Australia you need to hold general or specialist registration with AHPRA. Specialist registration requires Fellowship of the Royal Australasian College of Physicians (RACP) in general medicine or an equivalent qualification assessed through the relevant specialist pathway. If you hold overseas specialist qualifications and are working towards full registration, some hospitals will offer supervised positions, but most locum internal medicine consultant roles require full unsupervised registration.

Hospital Credentialing

Beyond AHPRA, each hospital has its own credentialing process. You will typically need to provide your AHPRA certificate, your college fellowship certificate, evidence of professional indemnity insurance, a current curriculum vitae with referees, and a police check. For planned locum placements, this process can take two to four weeks, so starting your credentialing application early is important. StatDoctor connects you directly with facilities so you can manage your credentialing documentation without going through an intermediary.

Professional Indemnity Insurance

Locum consultants in internal medicine should carry medical indemnity insurance that covers their locum practice specifically. Most insurers offer locum-specific policies, and cover is generally required before any hospital will complete your credentialing. If you are working through your own ABN or Pty Ltd structure, make sure your policy reflects your business structure, not just your personal registration status.

Where Locum Internal Medicine Jobs Are Most in Demand

Every state and territory has general medicine ward vacancies, but certain areas consistently generate more locum demand than others.

New South Wales has the largest hospital system in Australia and generates steady demand across metropolitan facilities like Westmead, Royal North Shore, and Prince of Wales, as well as a significant number of regional hospitals in the Hunter, Central Coast, and western NSW.

Queensland sees sustained demand throughout the year, with Logan, Townsville, Cairns, and Mount Isa hospitals among the regular advertisers for general medicine cover.

Victoria has a large metropolitan hospital sector and a substantial number of regional hospitals in Geelong, Bendigo, Ballarat, and the Gippsland region that consistently struggle to retain permanent physicians long-term.

Western Australia offers some of the highest regional rates in the country, particularly for hospitals in the Pilbara, Kimberley, and Wheatbelt regions. Perth metropolitan hospitals also carry regular demand for general medicine cover.

South Australia and the Northern Territory both offer strong rates, particularly for block placements. NT remote hospital work often includes generous travel and accommodation packages on top of the daily rate.

How StatDoctor Can Help You Find Locum Internal Medicine Jobs in Australia

StatDoctor is an Australian-built platform that connects locum doctors directly with hospitals and GP clinics, cutting out the traditional agency model and the fees that come with it. For internal medicine physicians looking for locum work, this means you see the full rate being offered, with no agency margin removed before it reaches you.

You can browse available internal medicine locum positions, accept shifts that suit your schedule, and manage your documentation in one place. Hospitals post their requirements directly to the platform, which means response times are faster and you can secure a placement without going back and forth through a third party.

To get started, download the StatDoctor app, complete your profile with your AHPRA registration details and fellowship documentation, and start browsing available internal medicine positions in your preferred state or region. The platform covers metropolitan, regional, and remote placements, so whether you want a single day of cover in Sydney or a four-week block in Queensland, you can find it and accept it directly.

If you are a physician looking to take control of your locum career in 2026, StatDoctor gives you the transparency, flexibility, and direct access to hospitals that makes that possible. Download the app today and see what is available in your area.
fieldData.post-summary: Internal medicine is one of the most consistently in-demand specialties across Australian public hospitals, and the locum market reflects that clearly. This guide covers pay rates, credentialing requirements, demand hotspots by state, and how to find locum internal medicine jobs in Australia in 2026.
