fieldData.post-body:
Why Irish Doctors Are Choosing Australia in 2026

Australia has long been a popular destination for Irish doctors looking for better pay, a warmer climate, and a healthcare system that genuinely values their training. In 2026, with ongoing workforce shortages across regional and metropolitan hospitals alike, that demand is stronger than ever. Whether you are a junior doctor finishing your internship year or a specialist with years of experience, Australia offers clear pathways to registration, competitive locum rates, and a lifestyle that is hard to beat.

The good news for Irish trained doctors is that Ireland is recognised as a "competent authority" country by the Medical Board of Australia. This means you can skip the AMC exams entirely and move through a streamlined registration pathway. In this guide, we will walk you through exactly how to make the move, from AHPRA registration and visa options to finding your first locum job once you land.

AHPRA Registration for Irish Doctors: The Competent Authority Pathway

The single biggest advantage Irish doctors have when moving to Australia is access to the Competent Authority Pathway. Australia recognises medical training from Ireland (along with the UK, Canada, the United States, and New Zealand) as broadly comparable to its own. This means you do not need to sit the AMC CAT or Clinical exams that doctors from non-competent authority countries are required to pass.

How the Competent Authority Pathway Works

The process involves applying to AHPRA for provisional registration, which allows you to begin supervised practice in an approved position in Australia. You will need to complete a minimum of 12 months (at least 47 weeks full time equivalent) of supervised practice. Once your supervisor confirms satisfactory performance, you can apply to convert your provisional registration to general registration.

For specialists, the process is slightly different. If you hold a Certificate of Completion of Specialist Training (CCST) from the Irish Medical Council or equivalent specialist qualification, you may be eligible for specialist recognition through the relevant Australian medical college. Some specialties, including psychiatry, anaesthetics, and obstetrics and gynaecology, are also eligible for the Expedited Specialist Registration pathway, which can fast track your assessment.

What You Will Need to Apply

Before you submit your AHPRA application, make sure you have the following documents ready. Your primary medical qualification certificate from your Irish medical school. A Certificate of Good Standing from the Irish Medical Council, issued within the last three months. Evidence of your internship completion and any postgraduate training. A valid English language exemption (Irish trained doctors are generally exempt from English language testing as Ireland is a recognised English speaking country). Proof of identity, including your passport. Professional indemnity insurance arrangements, which your employer in Australia will typically organise.

Processing times can vary, but most applications are reviewed within 30 days for the initial assessment, with the full process taking four to six weeks once all documentation is submitted. Be aware that the period from October to February is AHPRA's peak processing season, so submitting your application outside this window can help avoid delays.

Visa Options for Irish Doctors Moving to Australia

Once you have your AHPRA registration sorted (or at least in progress), the next step is securing the right visa. The most common pathway for Irish doctors is employer sponsored, which means a hospital, health service, or GP clinic in Australia sponsors your visa application.

Skills in Demand Visa (Subclass 482)

The Skills in Demand (SID) visa, which replaced the old Temporary Skills Shortage visa in December 2024, is the most popular option for doctors. Medical practitioners are listed on the Core Skills Occupation List, which means you are eligible for sponsorship under the Specialist Skills stream. The visa is typically granted for up to four years and allows you to work for your sponsoring employer. One major benefit for doctors specifically is that medical practitioners are generally exempt from the usual employment restrictions on 482 visas. This means you can work part time, for multiple employers, or even as an independent contractor while holding the visa.

Permanent Residency Pathways

If you are thinking long term, the 482 visa can serve as a stepping stone to permanent residency. After working for your sponsor for a qualifying period, you may be eligible to transition to a subclass 186 Employer Nomination Scheme visa. Alternatively, doctors working in regional areas (classified as Modified Monash 2 to 7) may have access to additional visa concessions and faster PR pathways. Many Irish doctors who come to Australia for a year or two of locum work end up staying permanently once they experience the lifestyle and earning potential.

What Irish Doctors Can Expect to Earn as Locums in Australia

One of the biggest drawcards for Irish doctors is the earning potential. Locum rates in Australia are significantly higher than what most doctors earn in Ireland, even before you factor in the lower cost of living outside of Sydney and Melbourne.

As a general guide for 2026, GP locum rates typically range from $1,500 to $2,200 per day in metropolitan areas, with rural and remote placements offering $1,800 to $3,000 or more per day. Specialist locum rates sit between $2,000 and $3,500 per day depending on the specialty and location. Resident Medical Officers and registrars can expect $140 to $180 per hour, or roughly $1,100 to $1,500 per day.

Rural and remote placements consistently pay the highest rates, often 30 to 50 percent more than urban equivalents. If you are open to working in regional Australia, even for short stints, you can significantly boost your earnings while also experiencing parts of the country that most tourists never see.

Financial Considerations for Irish Doctors

Australia and Ireland have a Double Taxation Agreement, which means you will not be taxed twice on the same income. However, you will need to set up a few things when you arrive. An Australian Tax File Number (TFN) is essential and can be applied for as soon as you have a valid visa. If you plan to work as a sole trader (which many locum doctors do), you will also need an Australian Business Number (ABN). Superannuation (Australia's equivalent of a pension) is compulsory, and your employer must contribute 11.5% of your earnings into a super fund. You can choose your own fund or use the employer's default option.

It is worth speaking with an accountant who specialises in medical professionals early on. Locum doctors can claim a range of tax deductions including travel, accommodation, professional development, and medical indemnity insurance. Getting your structure right from the start, whether that is sole trader or a Pty Ltd company, can save you thousands of dollars over the course of a year.

Settling In: What to Know About Life in Australia as an Irish Doctor

The cultural adjustment from Ireland to Australia is relatively smooth. English is the primary language, the healthcare system shares many similarities with the HSE and NHS models, and there is already a large and well established Irish community across Australia, particularly in Sydney, Melbourne, Brisbane, and Perth.

Many Irish doctors start with locum work because it offers maximum flexibility. You can try different hospitals, clinics, and regions before deciding where you want to settle. Locum assignments can range from a single day to several months, and the variety of work keeps things interesting while you figure out your long term plans.

If you are coming with a partner or family, it is worth noting that 482 visa holders can include dependants on their visa application. Partners receive full work rights in Australia, and children can attend local schools. The cost of childcare in Australia is high compared to Ireland, but the government's Child Care Subsidy helps offset this for eligible families.

How to Find Locum Work in Australia from Ireland

The traditional route has been to sign up with one or more locum agencies, who take a commission (often 15 to 25 percent) from each placement. While agencies still play a role, a growing number of doctors are choosing to connect directly with hospitals and clinics through platforms like StatDoctor.

The advantage of a direct connection model is transparency. You can see the actual pay rate being offered, communicate directly with the facility, and avoid the markup that agencies add. For doctors coming from Ireland, this is particularly valuable because you can start browsing available positions and getting a sense of the market before you even leave home.

How StatDoctor Can Help

StatDoctor is an Australian platform built to connect locum doctors directly with hospitals and GP clinics, cutting out the agency middleman. If you are an Irish doctor considering the move to Australia, StatDoctor lets you browse locum opportunities across every state and territory, see transparent pay rates without hidden agency fees, connect directly with hospitals and clinics, and manage your availability and preferences all in one place.

Whether you are still in the planning stages or you have already landed in Australia and are ready to pick up your first shift, StatDoctor makes finding locum work straightforward. Download the StatDoctor app today on iOS or Android, or visit statdoctor.app to create your profile and start exploring opportunities.
fieldData.post-summary: A complete guide for Irish doctors considering the move to Australia in 2026. Covers the Competent Authority registration pathway, visa options including the Subclass 482, locum pay rates by specialty, financial setup tips, and how to find your first locum job.

fieldData.name: Moving from Ireland to Australia as a Doctor: Registration, Visas, and Locum Work in 2026

fieldData.post-body:
How to Become a Locum Specialist Doctor in Australia in 2026

A practical guide to becoming a locum specialist doctor in Australia in 2026, including demand, pay drivers, registration pathways, and the setup steps to start working.

Quick Summary

  • Where demand is strongest: Demand is strongest where permanent recruitment is harder, especially in rural, regional, and remote services. It also varies by specialty and seniority, with hospitals using locums to cover gaps in essential specialist care.
  • How pay works: There is no single national specialist locum rate. Pay varies mainly by seniority, specialty, state, and whether the quoted figure includes items such as superannuation.
  • Registration before you start: Doctors already appropriately registered in Australia may only need current documents and role-matched credentials. Overseas-trained specialists may need to use a Medical Board specialist pathway such as specialist recognition, area of need, or an expedited pathway if eligible.

Why specialist locum work is growing in Australia

Specialist locum work is growing in Australia because the wider doctor shortage is still a live issue heading into 2026. One widely cited projection points to a national shortfall of 13,000 doctors by 2026. At the same time, the medical workforce has been growing, which shows the problem is not simply a lack of new registrations. The gap is that supply, demand, and service needs do not line up evenly across the country.

The market is also uneven in ways that matter to specialist doctors. The shortage picture varies by geography, specialty, and seniority, so locum demand is not one national story with one simple rate. Pay data from 2025-26 shifts shows earnings differ by seniority level, specialty, and state. Consultant averages are higher than junior levels overall, but the source data still stresses that these are averages, not a universal specialist rate. In practice, a specialist considering locum work needs to think less in terms of "What does Australia pay?" and more in terms of "Where is my specialty needed, and under what conditions?"

That is why it helps to frame locum planning around four basics. First, look at demand in your own field and the kinds of locations where shortages are hardest to fill. Second, check how pay changes by state, specialty, and seniority instead of relying on a headline number. Third, confirm your registration position, especially if you are moving through a specialist pathway or considering roles with specific registration requirements. Fourth, make sure your operational setup is ready, including current paperwork and the practical items needed to start quickly. The rest of this guide focuses on those four areas so you can judge locum work realistically, not just optimistically.

  • Australia is entering 2026 with a well-publicised doctor shortage, which supports ongoing locum demand.
  • Specialist locum opportunities are shaped by geography, specialty shortages, and service gaps.
  • Pay is not fixed nationally. It varies by seniority, specialty, and state.
  • A practical way to assess locum work is to review demand, pay variation, registration status, and operational readiness.

Related reading:

  • Australia's doctor shortage in 2026
  • locum doctor pay rates in Australia

Where demand is strongest for locum specialists in 2026

Locum specialist demand in Australia is strong in 2026, but it is not spread evenly across the country or across specialties. The main national backdrop is a projected doctor shortfall heading into 2026, yet that headline number matters most when it shows up as hard-to-fill hospital rosters, open specialist vacancies, and gaps in senior coverage. In practice, the busiest locum markets are usually the places where services still need to run every day even when permanent recruitment is slow.

That is why rural, regional, and remote settings tend to rely more heavily on locum specialists than major metro centres. For a specialist considering locum work, demand is best read as a mix of three signals: local vacancy pressure, the need to keep hospital services operating safely, and specialty-specific bottlenecks that are harder to solve with the permanent workforce alone.

Related reading:

  • Australia's doctor shortage in 2026
  • Locum emergency medicine jobs in Australia

How pay works for locum specialist doctors

Locum specialist pay in Australia does not come from one fixed national rate. The strongest drivers in the source data are seniority, specialty, and state. Across all specialties and states in the cited 2026 shift data, average rates step up clearly by level: RMOs/HMOs average about $140 an hour, registrars about $170 an hour, and consultants about $230 an hour. For most specialist locum doctors, the relevant market is the consultant end of that range, but that consultant average is still only a starting point rather than a guaranteed rate for every job.

That matters because consultant work is not priced the same across every specialty or every location. The pay comparison source is built around consultant rates by specialty and rates by state, which shows that both factors move the number. In plain terms, a specialist doctor should expect different offers depending on what field they work in and which state the shift is in. This is why a single headline like "specialists earn X per hour" is usually too simple to be useful.

If one role lists a higher hourly number but includes super, and another lists a lower number but excludes super, the gap may not be as large as it first looks. For that reason, specialist locums should compare like with like instead of assuming the biggest hourly number is automatically the better offer.

A practical way to read a locum offer is to compare the whole package in the same order each time. First, place the role in the right seniority band: for a specialist doctor, that usually means comparing it with other consultant jobs, not with registrar or junior rates. Next, check the specialty and the state, because those are two of the clearest sources of variation in the available data. Then confirm whether the pay is shown as an hourly or daily figure and whether super is included. Using that approach gives you a more realistic view of earnings than chasing one national figure for all locum specialist work.

  • Use consultant averages as a market guide, not a promise for every shift.
  • Compare jobs within the same specialty and state where possible.
  • Check whether the quoted rate is super-inclusive or super-exclusive.

Related reading:

  • Locum doctor pay rates in Australia by state and specialty

What registration pathway may apply before you can locum

The registration question usually starts with one simple distinction: are you already appropriately registered to practise in Australia, or are you an international specialist entering through a Medical Board specialist pathway? If you already hold the right Australian registration for the specialist work you plan to do, the issue may be less about choosing a pathway and more about making sure your documents are current before you accept locum work. If you are coming from overseas as a specialist international medical graduate, the Medical Board of Australia sets out specific specialist pathway options that may apply before you can work.

For international specialists, the Medical Board describes several specialist pathway routes, including specialist recognition, area of need, and an expedited specialist pathway for eligible applicants. This matters for locum work because hospitals and clinics will usually need confidence that your registration status matches the role being offered.

Even when the pathway is clear, timing can still affect how quickly you can start locum work. Registration processes often depend on documentation, and delays in preparing paperwork can slow down your first placement. A practical approach is to confirm your likely pathway first, then gather the required documents as early as possible so you are not trying to solve registration issues after a good locum opportunity appears.

Related reading:

  • how to start as a locum doctor

Your practical setup checklist for landing the first role

The fastest way to get ready for a first locum specialist role is to remove the common setup delays before you apply. Start with a current CV that is concise and easy for hospitals to scan. StatDoctor's step-by-step guide says to keep it up to date and limit it to a single A4 page, with your recent experience, clinical skills, rotations, qualifications, publications, and awards. Organise references early as well, because hospitals may use them during credentialing and selection. If you are an international medical graduate or moving through a specialist pathway, also confirm that your registration pathway matches the kind of specialist work you want to do in Australia.

Next, sort the documents and business items that affect whether you can start work smoothly. The setup guide highlights medical indemnity insurance, core paperwork, an ABN, and a Medicare provider number as key items to check before taking shifts. This is also where specialist doctors should make sure their registration status, scope, and any pathway conditions line up with the hospital and position being offered, rather than assuming all specialist locum jobs have the same requirements.

Once your paperwork is in order, be selective about where you apply. The shortage picture is strong overall, but source data shows demand is not spread evenly across specialties, locations, and seniority levels. Pay also varies by specialty, state, and level, so a consultant specialist should not expect every role to look the same. A practical first move is to shortlist hospitals and regions where your specialty profile is more likely to fit current demand, then compare those opportunities against the rate structure and work conditions on offer. In simple terms: get your documents ready, confirm your registration and billing setup, and then target roles that match both your specialist scope and the parts of Australia where demand is strongest.

  • Update your CV and keep it concise.
  • Line up recent, relevant referees before hospitals ask.
  • Check indemnity, paperwork, ABN, and provider number needs.
  • Match your registration pathway and specialist scope to the role.
  • Apply selectively by specialty, seniority, state, and region.

Related reading:

  • how to start as a locum doctor
  • where doctor shortages are strongest
  • locum pay by state and specialty

FAQ

Do all locum specialist doctors in Australia earn the same rate?

No. The source material shows rates vary by seniority, specialty, and state, so consultant-level work does not carry one fixed national rate.

Can an overseas-trained specialist take locum roles straight away in Australia?

Not necessarily. An overseas-trained specialist may need to qualify through a Medical Board specialist pathway before taking locum work, depending on their background and the role.

Are metro hospitals the main source of specialist locum work?

Not usually. The strongest pressure is often outside major metro centres, where regional, rural, and remote services rely more heavily on locum specialists to keep coverage in place.

What should a specialist doctor organise before applying for a first locum role?

A current CV, referees, indemnity arrangements, registration documents, and practical setup items such as ABN or provider number requirements should be checked early so credentialing does not delay a placement.

Next Step

Get ready for your first locum role

Review your registration position, update your CV, and compare roles by specialty, state, and total package before applying.

See how to start as a locum doctor

References

  • How to Start as a Locum Doctor: Step-by-Step Guide
  • Locum Emergency Medicine Jobs in Australia: Pay, Demand, and How to Get Started

  • What To Know Before Your First Locum Job

fieldData.post-body:
Obstetrics and gynaecology is one of Australia's most in-demand medical specialties, and the locum market for O&G practitioners is as strong as ever heading into 2026. A retiring specialist cohort, growing population, and persistent staffing gaps across regional and rural hospitals have created genuine and sustained demand for O&G locums across the country. Whether you are a Fellow of RANZCOG already practising in Australia, or an internationally trained specialist exploring your options, this guide walks you through pay rates, where the opportunities are, what credentials you need, and how to land your first placement.

Why Demand for Locum O&G Doctors Is So High

Australia has been grappling with an O&G workforce shortfall for several years, and the situation has not eased. Women's health services are expanding, birth rates in outer-metropolitan and regional communities continue to generate demand for intrapartum care, and an ageing specialist cohort is creating gaps that are difficult to fill with permanent appointments alone.

According to workforce data, O&G specialists remain on Australia's skills shortage list, with regional and rural hospitals particularly affected. Public hospitals across Queensland, Western Australia, South Australia, and the Northern Territory regularly post locum vacancies, and the turnaround time for filling those positions can be short. Hospitals that cannot find permanent VMOs or specialists often turn to locums to maintain services, which keeps the locum pipeline active throughout the year rather than just at peak periods.

The Expedited Specialist Registration pathway, introduced from 5 March 2025, has also made it faster for internationally trained O&G specialists from comparable health systems to begin working in Australia. If you hold an MRCOG and a certificate of completion of training from the UK, Ireland, or Canada, you may be eligible to practise here far sooner than was previously the case.

Locum O&G Pay Rates in Australia

Pay rates for locum O&G work in Australia are competitive by global standards, and the gap between locum and permanent rates has remained wide enough to make locum work financially attractive for those who prefer flexibility.

Consultant and Specialist Rates

As a locum O&G consultant or VMO, you can expect to earn approximately $2,000 per day as a baseline, with rates at some regional and remote hospitals going considerably higher. Hourly rates for specialist O&G locums generally sit between $150 and $250 or more, depending on the complexity of the role and the urgency of the placement. Some contracts include on-call components, procedural loading for theatre sessions, and accommodation allowances, all of which can add meaningfully to your total package.

Visiting Medical Officer positions can, when structured with private billing rights, result in annual earnings well above $500,000. While this applies more to ongoing VMO arrangements than short-term locum placements, many locum O&G specialists use locum work as a bridge to VMO appointments at hospitals they enjoy working at.

Published enterprise agreement rates for locum consultants sit in the range of $132 to $182 per hour (excluding superannuation) at the specialist year 1 to 9 level, depending on the relevant state or territory health service.

Registrar Rates

If you are working as an O&G registrar or senior registrar, locum rates typically range from $100 to $180 per hour depending on your level of experience and the nature of the role. Registrar-level locums are also in solid demand, particularly at regional hospitals that need cover for gaps created by training rotations.

Regional and Remote Loading

Rural and remote placements consistently attract the highest rates, along with benefits such as free or subsidised accommodation, flights, and in some cases vehicle use. Hospitals in the Northern Territory, remote Western Australia, and rural Queensland regularly offer packages that make short stints extremely well compensated. If you are open to travel, a week-long rural O&G locum placement can generate income that compares favourably to a month of salaried work in a metropolitan hospital.

What Qualifications You Need to Work as a Locum O&G Doctor in Australia

Fellowship of RANZCOG

The standard specialist qualification for obstetrics and gynaecology in Australia and New Zealand is a Fellowship of the Royal Australian and New Zealand College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists, known as FRANZCOG. If you completed your O&G training in Australia or New Zealand through the RANZCOG five-year training program, you already hold this qualification and your pathway to specialist registration with AHPRA is straightforward.

International Medical Graduates

If you trained overseas, your route depends on where and how you trained. Doctors who completed O&G training in comparable health systems, including the UK, Ireland, Canada, and similar countries, and who hold a qualification such as the MRCOG alongside a certificate of completion of specialist training, are generally considered comparable by RANZCOG. From March 2025, these doctors became eligible for the Expedited Specialist Registration pathway, which allows assessment to happen more quickly without a full college comparison of your training program.

You will still need to obtain specialist registration with AHPRA, which requires evidence of your qualifications, your specialist college assessment outcome, evidence of English proficiency if applicable, and confirmation of professional standing from every jurisdiction where you have held registration. If you have not yet started this process, it pays to begin gathering documents early as the overall timeline can still take several months.

Junior and Non-FRANZCOG Doctors

If you are an O&G registrar, hospital medical officer, or RMO looking for locum shifts, you do not need FRANZCOG. You will need general registration with AHPRA at the appropriate level, credentialling at the hospital or health service where you plan to work, and relevant procedural competency sign-offs depending on the tasks required. Many regional hospitals actively recruit junior O&G doctors for overnight cover and antenatal clinic shifts.

Where the Locum O&G Opportunities Are in Australia

Demand for O&G locums exists across all states and territories, but certain areas consistently generate more opportunities.

Queensland Health is one of the largest employers of locum O&G specialists in the country, with both major regional centres and remote communities seeking cover across the year. New South Wales has substantial demand in its outer-metropolitan and Hunter Valley regions, as well as in western NSW towns where O&G services rely almost entirely on visiting or locum arrangements.

In Western Australia, the sheer size of the state means that towns like Kalgoorlie, Bunbury, and Albany regularly need specialist O&G cover, and the WA Country Health Service has some of the more competitive remuneration packages available. The Northern Territory and South Australia have persistent O&G workforce gaps, particularly at Darwin and Alice Springs hospitals, and in regional SA centres like Whyalla and Mount Gambier.

Metropolitan hospitals also use O&G locums, particularly to cover parental leave, study leave, or sudden departmental gaps, but the metro market is more competitive and rates tend to be lower than rural and regional placements.

Setting Up to Work as a Locum O&G Doctor

Before you accept your first locum O&G placement, you will need a few things in place. AHPRA registration at the appropriate level is non-negotiable. You will also need current professional indemnity insurance that covers locum work, including intrapartum and procedural care, as most hospitals will not credential you without this. Check your policy carefully and confirm that your insurer has been notified of your locum arrangements before you start.

You will need to apply for credentialling at each hospital or health service where you plan to work. Some larger health services have a centralised credentialling process that covers multiple sites, which can save time. It is worth keeping a complete credentialling portfolio readily accessible as a digital package, including your AHPRA certificate, indemnity certificate, RANZCOG or equivalent fellowship documents, immunisation records, and clinical references. Having these ready means you can respond quickly when a placement comes up at short notice.

If you plan to bill through an ABN rather than as a PAYG locum, you will need to register your ABN with the ATO and consider whether you need to register for GST, which is required if your annual locum earnings will exceed $75,000. For a more detailed guide to the financial side of locum work, the StatDoctor blog covers ABN setup, GST, sole trader versus Pty Ltd, and superannuation in dedicated articles.

How StatDoctor Can Help

StatDoctor is an Australian app built specifically to connect locum doctors directly with hospitals and GP clinics, removing the agency intermediary and the fees that come with it. For O&G specialists and registrars, this means more transparent rates, faster placements, and greater control over when and where you work.

You can create a profile in minutes, upload your credentials, and browse locum O&G positions across Australia. Hospitals post directly to the platform, so there is no third party managing your rate or reducing what the hospital pays. When a placement is the right fit, you can confirm it directly within the app.

Download StatDoctor on the App Store or Google Play and set up your profile today. Whether you are looking for a single cover shift at a regional hospital or a longer arrangement that lets you experience a new part of Australia, StatDoctor gives you the tools to find it on your own terms.
fieldData.post-summary: Obstetrics and gynaecology is one of Australia's most in-demand medical specialties, and the locum market for O&G practitioners is stronger than ever in 2026. This guide covers pay rates, where the demand is highest, what credentials you need, and how to land your first placement.

fieldData.post-body:
Why Australia Is Calling Filipino Doctors

The Philippines is one of Australia's top four source countries for international medical graduates, alongside the UK, Ireland, and India. With Australia facing a shortage of more than 13,000 doctors by 2026 and a GP deficit projected to exceed 5,500 by 2033, the demand for qualified overseas-trained doctors has never been higher. If you are a Filipino doctor considering a move to Australia, the opportunity is real and the pathway is well-established, though it does require careful preparation. This guide walks you through everything you need to know about moving from the Philippines to Australia as a doctor, including registration requirements, timelines, and the locum work opportunities waiting for you once you arrive.

Understanding the Australian Medical Registration System

Before you can practise medicine in Australia, you need to understand two key organisations: the Australian Medical Council (AMC) and the Australian Health Practitioner Regulation Agency (AHPRA).

The AMC is responsible for assessing the qualifications of overseas-trained doctors. If you trained outside Australia, the AMC is typically your first step. It verifies that your medical degree meets Australian standards and manages the examination process for doctors who need to demonstrate their clinical knowledge and skills.

AHPRA is the national registration body for doctors and 15 other health professions across all Australian states and territories. Once you have passed your AMC assessments, you apply to AHPRA for registration. Without AHPRA registration, you cannot legally practise medicine in Australia.

Which Pathway Applies to Filipino Doctors Moving to Australia

Australia has several registration pathways for international medical graduates, and the one that applies to you depends primarily on where you completed your specialist or general training.

The Philippines is not on Australia's list of Competent Authority countries. This means you will follow the Standard Pathway, which involves sitting the AMC examinations. The Competent Authority Pathway, which allows for faster specialist registration without AMC exams, is reserved for doctors who trained in countries such as the UK, Ireland, the USA, and Canada.

The Standard Pathway has three stages. First, you pass the AMC Computer Adaptive Test (CAT), a written examination that assesses clinical knowledge across all areas of medicine. Second, you pass the AMC Clinical Examination, which is an Objective Structured Clinical Examination (OSCE) that tests your practical clinical and communication skills. Third, you complete 12 months of supervised practice in an approved position in Australia. After successfully completing all three stages, you can apply for general registration with the Medical Board of Australia.

Step-by-Step: How to Register as a Filipino Doctor in Australia

Step 1: Primary Source Verification

The process begins with Primary Source Verification (PSV), which confirms that your medical degree is genuine. This is handled through the ECFMG's EPIC service, which contacts your university directly to confirm your graduation date, degree type, and other key details. PSV results are then sent to the AMC and AHPRA. You should start this step as early as possible, as it can take several months and must be complete before the rest of your application can progress.

Step 2: English Language Requirements

You will need to demonstrate English language proficiency before you can register. The Medical Board of Australia accepts results from the IELTS (Academic), OET, TOEFL iBT, and PTE Academic. The minimum score requirements are set by the Medical Board, so check the most current standards on the AHPRA website before you book your test. If English is your primary language of instruction and practice, exemptions may apply, but most Filipino graduates will need to provide a recent test result.

Step 3: Sit the AMC CAT Examination

The AMC CAT is a computer-adaptive written examination that you can sit in multiple countries, including the Philippines. You do not need AHPRA registration before sitting this exam, which means you can begin your preparation and sit the test before you arrive in Australia. The exam covers all clinical disciplines and is designed to test whether you have the knowledge standard expected of an Australian medical graduate. Many candidates invest six to twelve months in structured AMC CAT preparation, using the AMC's official materials alongside question banks and study groups.

Step 4: Sit the AMC Clinical Examination

Once you pass the AMC CAT, you progress to the AMC Clinical Examination. This OSCE is held in Australia at designated centres and tests your ability to take a history, examine a patient, communicate with patients and colleagues, and manage clinical scenarios. Candidates typically prepare for this exam in Australia, as it requires hands-on clinical preparation. Sitting this exam usually requires you to be in the country, so many Filipino doctors begin making visa arrangements around this stage.

Step 5: Apply for Provisional or Limited Registration

If you have passed both AMC examinations, you can apply to AHPRA for provisional registration, which allows you to complete your 12-month supervised practice period. If you have only passed the written AMC CAT, you may be eligible for limited registration in certain positions. AHPRA's initial review of your application takes up to 30 days, and the full assessment can take up to six weeks. You should apply at least three months before your intended start date.

Step 6: Complete 12 Months of Supervised Practice

Your supervised practice period must be completed in an AHPRA-approved position with an accredited supervisor. During this time, you will be working as a doctor in a clinical setting, gaining Australian-standard experience. At the end of the period, your supervisor will confirm that you have met the required learning outcomes, and you can then apply for general registration.

How Long Does the Process Take for Filipino Doctors Moving to Australia

For most Standard Pathway applicants from the Philippines, the entire process from beginning AMC preparation to obtaining general registration takes between 18 months and three years. The timeline varies depending on how quickly you complete each stage, how long PSV and AHPRA processing takes, and how soon you can secure a supervised practice position.

The steps with the longest lead times are PSV (which requires your university to respond) and the AMC Clinical Examination (which has limited sitting dates). Building in buffer time for these stages will reduce delays.

Visa Options for Filipino Doctors Relocating to Australia

You will need a visa to live and work in Australia. The Department of Home Affairs manages visa applications, and you apply through the ImmiAccount online portal. You need to have your AHPRA registration in place before applying for most skilled worker visas, as your registration number is a required document in the application.

The most common visa pathways for internationally trained doctors include the Temporary Skill Shortage (TSS) Visa (subclass 482), which is typically employer-sponsored, and the Skilled Independent Visa (subclass 189) or Skilled Nominated Visa (subclass 190), which are points-tested. Doctors are in high demand and appear on Australia's Skilled Occupation List, which improves your eligibility for skilled migration pathways.

Working in a regional or rural area of workforce shortage can significantly increase your points and visa options. The Regional Sponsored Migration Scheme (subclass 187) is also available for doctors willing to work in designated regional locations.

Locum Work Opportunities for Filipino Doctors in Australia

Once you hold general registration, you are eligible to work as a locum doctor in Australia. Locum work is particularly well-suited to doctors who are new to the country, as it lets you explore different settings, build your professional network, and earn at a higher daily rate than permanent roles.

Australia's doctor shortage means locum demand is strong across most specialties and locations. General practice has a shortage exceeding 2,400 full-time equivalent GPs nationally, and regional areas are particularly desperate for cover. Locum GP rates typically range from AUD$1,500 to AUD$2,200 per day, and rural and remote positions often pay up to 50% more than metropolitan rates. Many rural locum roles also include accommodation and travel allowances, which reduces your living costs while you are on placement.

Emergency medicine, psychiatry, and general medicine are also in very high demand. Locum psychiatrists can earn AUD$2,500 to AUD$4,000 per day, and locum emergency medicine registrars earn an average of AUD$194 per hour. The earning potential for locum doctors in Australia is substantial, and Filipino-trained doctors who have completed the Standard Pathway and obtained general registration are fully eligible to access these rates.

Key Things to Know Before You Start

Starting your AHPRA journey early is the most consistent advice from doctors who have been through the process. PSV in particular has long and unpredictable timelines, and delays at that stage hold up everything else. Begin your PSV application as soon as you are seriously considering the move.

Once you are working as a locum doctor in Australia, you will likely be operating under an Australian Business Number (ABN) as an independent contractor rather than as an employee. This means you are responsible for setting aside funds for tax, registering for GST once your income exceeds AUD$75,000, and lodging quarterly Business Activity Statements. Many locum doctors work with an accountant who specialises in medical professionals to manage these obligations.

Australia has a strong community of Filipino healthcare professionals, particularly in Sydney, Melbourne, and across regional Queensland and Western Australia. Connecting with this community early, through professional associations and online forums, can provide practical advice on finding supervised practice positions and locum work.

How StatDoctor Can Help

Once you have your AHPRA general registration in hand, StatDoctor connects you directly with hospitals and GP clinics looking for locum cover, without the agency fees that eat into your earnings. The platform is built for doctors, meaning you can browse available shifts, set your preferred locations and specialties, and manage your bookings from your phone.

For Filipino doctors who have invested years into the AMC pathway and AHPRA registration process, finding well-paid locum work quickly is a priority. StatDoctor makes that process straightforward by removing the middleman and putting you in direct contact with facilities that need your skills.

Download the StatDoctor app today and take the next step in your Australian medical career.
fieldData.post-summary: If you are a Filipino doctor considering a move to Australia, the pathway is well-established but requires careful preparation. This guide covers AMC examinations, AHPRA registration, visa options, and the locum work opportunities available once you arrive, including GP rates of AUD$1,500 to AUD$2,200 per day.
fieldData.author: 68e360c1b6fe0fae2425baeb
fieldData.name: Moving from the Philippines to Australia as a Doctor: AMC Exams, AHPRA Registration, and Locum Work in 2026

fieldData.post-body:
If you are an international medical specialist considering a move to Australia, 2026 may be the best year to make it happen. The Expedited Specialist Pathway, often referred to as the ESP, has expanded again, and the latest additions could open the door for thousands of qualified doctors from the UK and Ireland to fast track their specialist registration with the Medical Board of Australia.

Since its initial launch in late 2024, the ESP has been growing steadily. It started with General Practice, Anaesthetics, Obstetrics and Gynaecology, and Psychiatry. As of 19 January 2026, General Medicine and General Paediatrics have been added to the list of eligible specialties. With Diagnostic Radiology widely expected to follow, the pathway is becoming a genuine alternative to the longer, more expensive traditional assessment routes.

This guide covers everything you need to know about the ESP in 2026: which specialties are eligible, what qualifications you need, how to apply step by step, and how the pathway connects to locum work opportunities across Australia.

What Is the Expedited Specialist Pathway?

The Expedited Specialist Pathway is an initiative by the Medical Board of Australia and AHPRA designed to make it faster, safer, and cheaper for eligible specialist international medical graduates (SIMGs) to gain specialist registration in Australia. Rather than going through a prolonged comparability assessment or years of supervised practice under the traditional Standard Pathway, eligible doctors can apply directly for specialist registration.

According to AHPRA, the ESP was created to address critical workforce shortages across Australia's healthcare system while maintaining high standards of patient safety. In practical terms, it means that if your overseas specialist qualification has been assessed as substantially equivalent to, or based on similar competencies as, an approved Australian specialist qualification, you can get registered and start practising much sooner.

Which Specialties Are Eligible in 2026?

As of early 2026, the following specialties are covered by the Expedited Specialist Pathway:

  • General Practice (added October 2024)
  • Anaesthetics
  • Obstetrics and Gynaecology
  • Psychiatry
  • General Medicine (added January 2026)
  • General Paediatrics (added January 2026)

Diagnostic Radiology is currently being assessed by the Australian Medical Council (AMC) and is expected to be added in the near future. The Australian Government has signalled that more specialties will continue to be added as they are prioritised by the country's health ministers.

Eligibility: Who Can Apply?

The ESP is currently available to doctors who trained in the United Kingdom and, for some specialties, Ireland. The key requirement is that you hold a recognised specialist qualification together with a Certificate of Completion of Training (CCT) from the relevant Royal College.

Here is a breakdown of what is accepted for the two newest specialties:

General Medicine

At present, the General Medicine ESP is limited to UK trained specialists only. Eligible applicants must hold Membership of the Royal College of Physicians (MRCP UK) together with a CCT in General Internal Medicine. Importantly, this pathway is for doctors who completed the full CCT route, not CESR (Certificate of Eligibility for Specialist Registration). Dual training CCT pathways that include General Internal Medicine are also accepted.

General Paediatrics

The General Paediatrics ESP is open to both UK and Irish trained specialists.

For UK trained doctors, you need MRCPCH (awarded from August 2007 onwards) together with a CCT in Paediatrics issued by PMETB (pre 2010) or the GMC (post 2010), and completion of any recognised RCPCH curriculum.

For Irish trained doctors, the requirements include MRCPI in Paediatrics (awarded from July 2010 onwards), CSCST in Paediatrics, and completion of Specialist Training (SAT) under the RCPI Faculty of Paediatrics.

All General Paediatrics applicants must also demonstrate recent clinical experience in general paediatrics, hold a valid Advanced Paediatric Life Support (APLS) certification, and complete an Australian child protection course.

Other Specialties

For Anaesthetics, you need FRCA or FCAI together with a CCT. For Obstetrics and Gynaecology, MRCOG plus a CCT is required. For Psychiatry, MRCPsych plus a CCT is the standard. General Practice has its own set of accepted qualifications, including MRCGP and MICGP with the relevant certificates of completion.

Across all specialties, your primary medical degree must be from an institution recognised by both the AMC and the World Directory of Medical Schools (WDOMS). You also need to meet AHPRA's English language requirements through one of the accepted exams, such as the OET or IELTS.

How to Apply: A Step by Step Guide

The application process for the ESP involves several stages. While it is significantly faster than the Standard Pathway, it still requires careful preparation and documentation.

Step 1: Verify Your Qualifications

Start by applying to the AMC for primary source verification (PSV) of your primary and specialist medical qualifications. You will need to create an AMC account and portfolio. You will also need to establish a MyIntealth account through the ECFMG and set up an EPIC portfolio for verification purposes. This step can take several weeks, so it is worth starting early.

Step 2: Check the Accepted Qualifications List

Before applying, confirm that your specific specialist qualification and CCT appear on the Medical Board's Expedited Specialist Pathway accepted qualifications list. The list is updated periodically, so check the most recent version on the Medical Board of Australia's website.

Step 3: Secure a Position in Australia

You can secure a position either before or after your registration application. Many hospitals across Australia are actively recruiting through this pathway, particularly in regional and rural areas where specialist shortages are most acute. Having a confirmed position can strengthen your application, though it is not a strict prerequisite at the application stage.

Step 4: Apply for Registration

Submit your registration application online through the AHPRA portal. You will apply to the Medical Board for specialist registration under the Expedited Specialist Pathway. The application fee for 2026 is $1,096 AUD (including GST), with an additional assessment of comparability fee of $6,184 AUD (including GST).

Step 5: Complete Supervised Practice

Once your registration is granted, it will come with conditions. You will need to complete six months of supervised practice in an approved clinical setting. During this period, you and your supervisor will need to submit reports to the Board after three months of practice. You will also need to complete cultural safety education and an orientation to the Australian healthcare system.

Step 6: Apply to Remove Conditions

At the end of the six month supervised practice period, submit your orientation report, supervised practice report, and evidence of completed workplace based assessments. Apply to the Medical Board to have the conditions on your registration removed. Once the conditions are lifted, you hold full specialist registration.

ESP Registration vs Fellowship: What Is the Difference?

One important distinction to understand is that the ESP leads to specialist registration, but it does not automatically grant you Fellowship of an Australian specialist college (such as FRACP, FRACS, or FRANZCP). Fellowship is a separate process managed by the relevant college.

This matters because some roles, particularly locum positions and private practice opportunities, may prefer or require Fellowship. If this is relevant to your career plans, contact the relevant specialist medical college early to understand their assessment requirements. For General Medicine and General Paediatrics, the RACP also offers an Accelerated Specialist Pathway that supports applicants from a broader range of countries, including India, Hong Kong, and Sri Lanka.

What About Visas?

To work in Australia, you will need an appropriate visa. The most common options for international medical specialists include the Subclass 482 Temporary Skill Shortage (TSS) visa and the Subclass 186 Employer Nomination Scheme visa. Many hospitals, particularly those in areas of workforce need, will sponsor your visa as part of the recruitment process. Your employer or recruitment partner can usually guide you through the visa application alongside your registration.

How the ESP Connects to Locum Work

Once you hold specialist registration (even with conditions during your supervised practice period), you can begin working in your specialty. After your conditions are removed and you hold full specialist registration, you have the flexibility to take on locum positions across Australia.

Locum specialist work is in strong demand, particularly in regional and rural hospitals. Specialists in General Medicine, Paediatrics, Psychiatry, and Anaesthetics are among the most sought after, with daily rates ranging from $2,000 to $3,500 AUD depending on the specialty, location, and urgency of the placement. Remote and regional postings often come with additional incentives such as accommodation, travel allowances, and loading on top of the base rate.

For doctors who have come through the ESP, locum work offers an excellent way to gain broad experience across different Australian healthcare settings, build professional networks, and earn competitive income while deciding on a longer term career path.

How StatDoctor Can Help

If you are an international specialist who has come through the Expedited Specialist Pathway, or you are in the process of getting registered, StatDoctor can help you find your next locum opportunity. StatDoctor connects locum doctors directly with hospitals and GP clinics across Australia, cutting out the middleman and putting more money in your pocket.

With StatDoctor, you can browse available locum shifts, set your preferred locations and specialties, and get matched with hospitals that need your skills. Whether you are looking for your first placement during supervised practice or you are a fully registered specialist ready to take on locum work across the country, the platform makes it simple to find opportunities that fit your schedule and career goals.

Download the StatDoctor app today and start exploring locum opportunities across Australia.
fieldData.post-summary: Australia's Expedited Specialist Pathway has expanded in 2026 to include General Medicine and General Paediatrics, making it faster and cheaper for eligible international specialists to register and start working. Here is everything you need to know about eligibility, the application process, and how to start locum work through this pathway.
fieldData.author: 68e360c1b6fe0fae2425baeb
fieldData.name: Australia's Expedited Specialist Pathway in 2026: A Complete Guide for International Doctors

fieldData.post-body:
Australia's rural and remote healthcare system is facing a workforce crisis that shows no signs of slowing down. Research consistently shows that small rural towns have up to three times fewer doctors per capita than metropolitan areas, and the gap keeps getting wider. For locum doctors, this shortage is not just a headline. It is a career-defining opportunity. Rural locum rates are among the highest in the country, travel and accommodation are frequently covered, and the clinical variety is unmatched. Here is what the landscape looks like in 2026 and how you can make the most of it.

The Scale of the Rural Doctor Shortage

The numbers paint a stark picture. Metropolitan areas in Australia have roughly three times as many doctors per capita as small rural towns, and twice as many nurses and allied health professionals. A University of Wollongong study found that the greatest staffing shortfalls are concentrated in the smallest and most remote communities, which are the very places where access to healthcare matters most.

Several structural factors drive this shortage. Training pipelines are long and largely city-based, making it difficult to build a local rural workforce. Many hospitals in regional areas cannot offer permanent positions, so they rely on visiting or locum doctors to maintain services. And the ongoing retirement of older rural GPs is not being matched by new graduates choosing to practise outside capital cities.

What Rural Locum Doctors Actually Earn

Rural and remote placements consistently pay more than their metropolitan equivalents, and the gap is significant. While metro locum rates typically sit around $2,000 to $2,500 per day depending on the specialty, rural and remote rates regularly reach $3,000 to $4,000 per day. In particularly hard to fill locations like Mount Isa, Alice Springs, or the Pilbara, rates can climb even higher.

On top of the daily rate, most rural locum placements include flights, accommodation, and a travel allowance. The Rural Locum Assistance Program (Rural LAP) specifically recruits locum doctors for rural and remote placements, offering an hourly wage plus superannuation, a $100 travel allowance per day, and covering flights and accommodation. When you factor in these extras, the total package for a rural locum placement often exceeds what you would earn in a comparable metro role by a wide margin.

How the Maths Works Out

A locum doctor working four days a week at $3,000 per day for 48 weeks would gross around $576,000 for the year. That is well above the permanent equivalent. The trade off is that there is no paid leave, no employer super contributions in most contractor arrangements, and you need to manage your own tax, insurance, and CPD. But for many doctors, the financial upside more than makes up for it.

Where the Demand Is Highest

Not all rural areas are created equal when it comes to locum demand. Some regions consistently struggle to attract doctors and offer premium rates as a result.

Western Australia's Kimberley and Pilbara regions have some of the highest demand in the country, driven by vast distances and small populations spread across remote communities. Queensland's outback and Far North also face chronic shortages, particularly for GPs and emergency medicine doctors. The Northern Territory relies heavily on locum cover, especially in Aboriginal community controlled health services and remote clinics. And in regional New South Wales, smaller towns outside the main regional centres often cannot sustain permanent medical positions and depend entirely on locum rotations.

The demand is not limited to GPs either. Specialists in anaesthetics, psychiatry, emergency medicine, and obstetrics are all in high demand across regional and rural hospitals. If you hold a Fellowship or advanced training in any of these areas, you will likely find more work available than you can take on.

What You Need to Get Started

The requirements for rural locum work are broadly the same as for any locum role in Australia, but there are a few additional considerations worth knowing about.

Registration and Credentials

You will need current AHPRA registration with no restrictions that would prevent you from working in a rural setting. Most rural hospitals require a minimum of two years post graduation experience, and many prefer doctors with emergency or procedural skills. If you are an international medical graduate, you will need to ensure your visa conditions allow you to work in the locations you are considering.

Insurance

Medical indemnity insurance is a legal requirement for practising doctors in Australia. If you are working as a contractor (which most locum doctors do), you will need your own policy. Make sure your cover extends to the scope of practice you will be undertaking in a rural setting, which can sometimes be broader than what you would do in a metro hospital.

Practical Preparation

Rural locum work often involves a wider scope of practice than you might be used to. You could be the only doctor on site for an entire shift, handling everything from minor presentations to genuine emergencies. If it has been a while since you managed a trauma case or delivered a baby, it is worth brushing up on those skills before heading out. Many rural hospitals also operate differently from metro facilities in terms of equipment, staffing, and referral pathways, so being adaptable is key.

The Lifestyle Factor

Money is obviously a big drawcard, but plenty of locum doctors find that rural placements offer something that city work simply cannot. The pace is different. You are not just another doctor in a busy department. You become part of a small team that genuinely relies on you, and the patients and staff tend to be deeply appreciative of the fact that you are there.

There is also the lifestyle appeal of spending time in some of Australia's most beautiful and remote landscapes. Whether it is the red desert of central Australia, the tropical coast of Far North Queensland, or the rolling farmland of regional Victoria, rural locum work gives you a chance to see parts of the country that most people never visit.

Many doctors start with a single rural placement and end up going back again and again. Some eventually make the move permanently. The flexibility of locum work means you can test the waters without committing to a long term contract, which is ideal if you are curious about rural medicine but not ready to uproot your life.

How StatDoctor Can Help

Finding rural locum shifts used to mean going through an agency and paying significant commission. StatDoctor is changing that. Our platform connects locum doctors directly with hospitals and GP clinics across Australia, including in rural and remote areas. You can browse available shifts, see the rates upfront, and book directly without the middleman.

If you are a doctor looking to explore rural locum work in 2026, download the StatDoctor app and start browsing shifts today. The demand is there, the rates are strong, and the experience might just change the way you think about your career.
