fieldData.name: Locum Work in Rural Australia: Why the Workforce Shortage Is Your Biggest Opportunity in 2026

fieldData.post-body:
Starting locum work is exciting -- the flexibility, the pay rates, the independence. But the financial setup catches many new locums off guard. Unlike permanent hospital employment, nobody withholds your tax, pays your super, or organises your insurance. You are running a business from day one, and the ATO treats you accordingly. Here is the checklist you need to work through before your first shift.

Step 1: Register Your ABN

Your Australian Business Number is the foundation. Most locum doctors operate as sole traders, which means you register an ABN in your own name through the Australian Business Register. The process takes about 10 minutes online and is free.

You will need your tax file number, personal details, and a description of your business activity (medical practitioner services). Your ABN becomes your invoicing identity -- every invoice you send to a hospital or agency will include it.

If you are considering a company structure (Pty Ltd), hold off until you have spoken to a medical accountant. The personal services income (PSI) rules mean that most locum doctors cannot effectively shelter income in a company anyway. For a detailed comparison, see our guide to sole trader vs Pty Ltd for locum doctors.

Step 2: Register for GST

If your annual locum income will exceed $75,000 (and for most locums it will within the first few months), you must register for GST. You can register for GST at the same time as your ABN through the ATO.

Once registered, you charge 10 per cent GST on top of your locum fees. A $200 per hour rate becomes $220 including GST. You then lodge a Business Activity Statement (BAS) quarterly and remit the GST collected to the ATO.

One nuance worth knowing: many clinical GP services are GST-free under the health exemption when billed directly to patients. But your locum fee -- invoiced to a hospital or practice for your services -- is generally taxable. The rules vary by service type, so confirm the specifics with your accountant.

Missing a BAS lodgement carries penalties. The ATO's failure to lodge penalty is currently $330 per 28-day period the return is late, capped at $1,650 for individuals. Let a few quarters slip and you are paying fines on top of the GST you already owe.

For a thorough walkthrough of BAS obligations, read our guide to BAS and GST for locum doctors.

Step 3: Get Professional Indemnity Insurance

This is non-negotiable. You need medical indemnity insurance before you work a single locum shift. The three major providers in Australia are:

Avant -- the largest medical defence organisation, covering over 90,000 doctors and students

MDA National -- strong presence across all states, administers the government's Premium Support Scheme

Your premium depends on your specialty, seniority, gross private billings, claims history, and whether you are working publicly, privately, or as a locum contractor. According to the Australian Government Actuary, the median premium across all specialties is approximately 2.5 per cent of private practice income. Low-risk, non-procedural specialties pay significantly less than surgical specialties.

New practitioners are often eligible for discounts. Avant, for example, offers its Getting Started in Private Practice program with substantial premium savings in your first four years.

A standard policy includes up to $20 million in professional indemnity cover, disciplinary proceedings cover (AHPRA, Medical Board, Coroner), and public liability cover for contractor locums.

Step 4: Set Up a Separate Bank Account and Tax Reserve

This is the step most new locums skip, and it is the one that causes the most pain at tax time. When you invoice as a locum on an ABN, the full payment lands in your account. No tax is withheld. That money is not entirely yours.

The practical rule: set aside 30 to 35 per cent of every locum payment into a separate savings account the moment it arrives. If your combined income (salaried plus locum) exceeds $180,000, push that to 45 per cent. Do not touch that account until you lodge your tax return.

Open a dedicated business transaction account for your locum invoicing. Keep it separate from your personal spending. This makes BAS lodgement, tax returns, and expense tracking dramatically simpler.

After your first year of locum income, the ATO will likely enrol you in PAYG instalments -- essentially quarterly pre-payments of your estimated income tax. Check your myGov account for these and pay them on time.

Step 5: Sort Out Superannuation

As a sole trader locum, you are not legally required to pay yourself superannuation. But you should. Missing even a few years of super contributions early in your career has a compounding cost that is easy to underestimate.

The current concessional contributions cap is $30,000 per year (from 1 July 2024). Contributions are taxed at 15 per cent inside super, versus your marginal rate of up to 47 per cent outside super. If you can afford it, making the maximum concessional contribution is one of the most effective tax strategies available.

You can also carry forward unused concessional cap amounts from up to five prior years, provided your total super balance is under $500,000.

Choose a super fund, make regular contributions, and submit a Notice of Intent to Claim a Deduction to your fund before you lodge your tax return. For more detail, see our full guide to superannuation for locum doctors.

Step 6: Find a Medical Accountant

A specialist medical accountant is worth every dollar. They understand the PSI rules, locum-specific deductions, BAS lodgement, HECS-HELP repayment obligations, and the interaction between salaried and ABN income. A good one will also advise you on structuring your practice, timing concessional super contributions, and managing PAYG instalments.

Expect to pay $1,000 to $3,000 per year for a medical accountant to handle your BAS and annual tax return. Firms like DPM, Voyage Financial, Trinity Accounting, and Wealthmed specialise in medical professionals.

Some expenses you can claim as deductions through your accountant include:

Professional indemnity insurance premiums

Medical registration and AHPRA fees

CPD courses and conference attendance

Work-related travel (flights, accommodation, car expenses for locum placements)

Mobile phone and internet (work-related portion)

Medical equipment and reference materials

Accounting and legal fees

Income protection insurance premiums

For a detailed list, see our guide to tax deductions for locum doctors.

The Complete Checklist

Before your first locum shift:

1. Register ABN (free, 10 minutes)

2. Register for GST (if earning over $75,000)

3. Get professional indemnity insurance (Avant, MDA National, or MIPS)

4. Open a separate business bank account

5. Set up a tax reserve account (30-45 per cent of gross income)

6. Choose a super fund and set up contributions

7. Engage a medical accountant

8. Confirm your Medicare provider number is active

9. Review income protection and life insurance

10. Set up basic bookkeeping (Xero, QuickBooks, or a simple spreadsheet)

Getting this right from the start saves you thousands in penalties, missed deductions, and tax surprises. If you are ready to find your first locum shifts, download the StatDoctor app and connect directly with hospitals across Australia.

MIPS (Medical Indemnity Protection Society) -- uses individual risk-based pricing
fieldData.post-summary: Starting locum work in Australia? Here is the complete financial setup checklist: ABN, GST, insurance, super, tax reserves, accounting, and bank accounts.
fieldData.author: 68e360c1b6fe0fae2425baeb
fieldData.name: How to Set Up Your Finances as a New Locum Doctor in Australia

fieldData.post-body:
You have probably heard the headline: Australia needs 13,000 more doctors. That number, cited by the University of Queensland and widely reported in 2025, captures the projected gap between doctor supply and demand heading into 2026. But what does the shortage actually look like on the ground, which specialties and regions are worst affected, and what does it mean if you are working or considering working as a locum?

The National Picture

Australia's registered medical workforce has been growing. According to AHPRA's 2024-25 annual report, the total number of registered health practitioners grew 4.3 per cent to 959,838, with a record 111,294 new registration applications received. The medical practitioner workforce specifically has grown from 1,965 per 100,000 population in the ACT to 2,272 over five years, and similar growth patterns are visible nationally.

Yet the shortage persists because demand is growing faster than supply. Australia's population is ageing -- nearly one in six Australians is now over 65. Diagnostic imaging services grew 11.3 per cent year-on-year in early 2023. Emergency department presentations have risen nationally, with only 55 per cent of ED visits completed within the four-hour benchmark target in 2023-24, down from 73 per cent in 2014-15, according to the AMA's 2025 Public Hospital Report Card.

International medical graduates remain essential to plugging the gap. IMGs comprise 32 per cent of Australia's medical workforce and over half of all rural doctors. In 2024-25, 26,703 internationally qualified health practitioners gained registration in Australia through AHPRA, a 6.7 per cent decrease from the prior year's record but still a substantial number.

Where the Shortages Are Worst

The shortage is not evenly distributed. Three dimensions matter: geography, specialty, and seniority.

Geography

Rural and remote Australia bears the brunt. Around seven million Australians (roughly 28 per cent of the population) live in areas where access to health services is consistently lower than in major cities. The GP workforce gap in rural Queensland alone has increased 16 per cent since 2020, according to Health Workforce Queensland's 2024-25 annual report. And for the first time since 2015, overseas-trained GPs outnumbered Australian-trained GPs in Queensland's remote and rural areas.

Even capital cities are not immune. Canberra has 23 per cent fewer GPs per person than Sydney, Melbourne, or Brisbane, making it the Australian capital with the fewest specialist GPs per capita, according to the RACGP.

Specialty

Specialties identified as being in shortage or facing significant demand include general practice, psychiatry, emergency medicine, radiology, general medicine, and medical oncology. The projected national undersupply for psychiatry alone is 20.7 per cent by 2048. Emergency departments are under particular pressure, with one-third of patients triaged as "emergency" not being seen within the recommended 10-minute window in 2023-24, up from 25 per cent in 2018-19.

Seniority

Retention is a challenge at the mid-career level. In rural areas, 31 per cent of practitioners plan to stay less than three years in their current location. The pipeline of new GPs is growing -- a record 1,772 doctors began RACGP GP training in 2026, a 19 per cent increase on 2025 -- but it takes roughly 10 years to train a specialist GP.

Why the Gap Is Not Closing Quickly

Several structural factors keep the shortage persistent:

Reduced workforce effort. Average doctor working hours have been declining for years. In 1999, Australian doctors worked 45.6 hours per week on average. By 2009, that had dropped to 42.2 hours. The trend has continued as the profession feminises and work-life balance expectations shift, meaning raw headcount does not translate directly to workforce capacity.

Bureaucratic barriers for IMGs. Despite the 13,000-doctor gap, hundreds of qualified international medical specialists already living in Australia cannot practise due to repetitive assessments, unclear registration pathways, and high costs. The Kruk review recommended reforms to make registration simpler, quicker, and cheaper for internationally qualified practitioners, and AHPRA is implementing changes, but the process is slow.

Training bottlenecks. While medical graduate numbers have roughly doubled since 2000 (reaching over 4,000 per year), the number of specialist training positions has not kept pace in all specialties. Competition for training spots remains intense in fields like anaesthetics, radiology, and surgery.

Distribution failures. Increasing overall supply does not fix distribution. Metro areas attract more doctors per capita, while rural and remote communities remain underserved despite financial incentives and mandatory rural training placements.

What This Means for Locum Doctors

If you are working as a locum or considering it, the shortage data has direct implications:

Sustained demand. The structural factors driving the shortage -- ageing population, maldistribution, training lag, reduced workforce effort -- are not going away in the next five to ten years. Locum demand, particularly in rural areas and shortage specialties, will remain strong.

Negotiating power. When hospitals cannot fill rosters permanently, they turn to locums. That means competitive rates, particularly for psychiatry, emergency medicine, radiology, and rural generalist work. For current rate benchmarks, see our complete guide to locum doctor earnings.

Rural premiums. The data is clear: rural and remote placements pay substantially more and usually include flights, accommodation, and a vehicle. If you are willing to travel, the financial upside is significant. See our state-by-state guides for specific opportunities, including the Northern Territory, Western Australia, and Queensland.

IMG opportunities. If you are an international medical graduate, the shortage means opportunities, but the pathway matters. AHPRA registration, AMC exams, and visa requirements are non-negotiable. For a detailed breakdown, see our guide to AHPRA registration for IMGs.

The Bottom Line

Australia's doctor shortage is real, structural, and not going to resolve in the near term. For locum doctors, that is both a challenge for the health system and an opportunity for your career. The data points clearly to sustained demand, strong pay rates, and particular opportunity in rural areas and shortage specialties.

If you are ready to find locum shifts across Australia, download the StatDoctor app and connect directly with hospitals -- no agency fees, no middlemen.
fieldData.post-summary: Australia is projected to be short 13,000 doctors by 2026. Here is what the shortage data actually says and what it means for locum doctor demand and pay.
fieldData.author: 68e360c1b6fe0fae2425baeb
fieldData.name: Australia's Doctor Shortage in 2026: The Numbers, the Causes, and What It Means for Locums

fieldData.post-body:
The Australian Capital Territory is a small jurisdiction with a surprisingly active locum market. Three public hospitals, a well-documented GP shortage, and a population of around 466,000 mean that gaps in rosters are felt quickly and filled urgently. If you are considering locum work in the ACT, here is what you need to know about the hospitals, the pay, the demand, and the practicalities.

The ACT Health System at a Glance

Canberra Health Services operates the ACT's three public hospitals:

The Canberra Hospital is a 600-bed tertiary facility in Garran. It is the largest hospital in the region and provides trauma services, most major medical and surgical sub-specialties, and the Centenary Hospital for Women and Children. A new Critical Services Building opened in 2024, adding a larger emergency department (including a dedicated children's ED), a 48-bed intensive care unit, and separate ambulance entry.

North Canberra Hospital (formerly Calvary Public Hospital Bruce) is a secondary care facility in the northern suburbs. It has an emergency department, intensive care unit, general medicine and surgery, mental health services, obstetrics, cardiology, and orthopaedics. The ACT Government compulsorily acquired this hospital from Calvary Health Care in 2023, and planning is underway for a new northside hospital on the site.

University of Canberra Hospital is a 140-bed sub-acute facility focused on rehabilitation and mental health, with 75 day places and outpatient services. It is the largest purpose-built rehabilitation centre in the ACT.

Beyond these hospitals, the ACT has five Walk-in Centres providing free care for non-life-threatening conditions, seven community health centres, and Clare Holland House for specialist palliative care.

What Locum Roles Are Available

Locum demand in the ACT spans multiple specialties. Based on current agency listings and hospital recruitment activity, the most consistently advertised roles include:

  • Emergency medicine (RMO, registrar, and consultant level)
  • General medicine and physician roles
  • Psychiatry (both adult and older persons)
  • Anaesthetics
  • Surgery (general, plastics, orthopaedics)
  • Obstetrics and gynaecology
  • Cardiology and neurology registrar cover

The Canberra Hospital, as the region's tertiary centre, generates the bulk of specialist locum demand. North Canberra Hospital's ED and general medicine wards also regularly need locum cover, particularly for night shifts and weekend rosters.

If you are a GP, there is also significant demand for locum general practitioners across ACT clinics, though much of this work is through private practices and billed on a percentage-of-billings or hourly rate basis.

Pay Rates in the ACT

ACT locum rates are broadly competitive with NSW, which makes sense given Canberra's proximity to and shared labour market with south-eastern NSW.

Based on current data from Medlo and agency listings:

  • RMO/HMO: $130-$160 per hour, with night shifts attracting premiums toward the upper end
  • Registrar: $160-$190 per hour depending on specialty, with emergency medicine and anaesthetics at the top
  • Consultant: $200-$280 per hour, with psychiatry and emergency medicine paying the highest rates
  • GP locum (private practice): $150-$200 per hour, or a percentage of billings (typically around 70 per cent)

A key point to check in the ACT: always confirm whether the quoted rate includes or excludes superannuation. The ACT Public Sector Medical Practitioners Enterprise Agreement governs pay for employed medical staff, and locum rates are typically negotiated separately on top of these benchmarks.

For a broader comparison of how these rates stack up nationally, see our full guide to locum doctor pay rates by state and specialty.

The GP Shortage in Canberra

One of the most notable features of the ACT health landscape is the GP shortage. According to the Royal Australian College of General Practitioners, Canberra has 23 per cent fewer GPs per person than Sydney, Melbourne, or Brisbane. Both Darwin and Hobart have more GPs per capita than the national capital, making Canberra the Australian capital city with the fewest specialist GPs per person.

In 2025, only 23 out of 52 funded AGPT training spots in the Murrumbidgee and ACT region were filled -- a 44 per cent fill rate. The RACGP has called for the ACT Government to match Victoria's incentive programs that bridge the wage gap between hospital-based doctors and GPs training in the community.

At the national level, a record 1,772 doctors began RACGP GP training in 2026, including 841 on a rural pathway (a 44.2 per cent increase on 2025). But it takes roughly 10 years to train a specialist GP, so the pipeline will not solve Canberra's shortage quickly.

For locum GPs, this shortage translates into consistent demand and strong bargaining power. If you hold FRACGP or are a VR GP, the ACT is one of the tighter markets in the country.

Lifestyle and Practicalities

Canberra is a compact, planned city with a population under 500,000. The practical advantages for locum doctors include:

  • Short commutes. The Canberra Hospital is a 15-minute drive from most suburbs. North Canberra Hospital is similarly accessible from the north side.
  • Lower cost of living than Sydney or Melbourne, though housing costs have risen significantly in recent years.
  • Proximity to Sydney (about three hours by car or a one-hour flight) and the NSW South Coast and Snowy Mountains.
  • No state payroll tax issues for sole trader locums, though you still need your ABN, GST registration, and AHPRA registration in order.

One thing to be aware of: because the ACT is a single-city jurisdiction, the locum market is smaller than in states like NSW or Queensland. You may find fewer simultaneous opportunities, but the roles that do come up tend to fill quickly and pay well precisely because the pool of available locums is smaller.

‍

If you are an international medical graduate, note that many ACT specialist positions are classified as District of Workforce Shortage under the Australian Government's DWS framework, which may affect your visa and Medicare access obligations. Check the Health Workforce Locator for current DWS classifications by specialty.

‍

How to Get Started

To work as a locum in the ACT, you need:

  1. Current AHPRA registration with no restrictions that would prevent you from practising in the relevant specialty.
  2. An ABN registered as a sole trader, unless you are working through a company structure. See our step-by-step guide to getting an ABN.
  3. Professional indemnity insurance from a provider such as Avant, MDA National, or MIPS.
  4. A Medicare provider number if you are doing any billing-eligible work.
  5. GST registration if your annual locum income exceeds $75,000 (and it almost certainly will).

For a broader overview of the setup process, read our guide on how to start as a locum doctor.

‍

The Bottom Line

The ACT is a small but high-value locum market. You have three public hospitals within a compact city, a well-documented GP shortage, and rates that match or approach NSW levels. The lifestyle is appealing if you value manageable commutes, proximity to nature, and a city that does not feel overwhelming. If you are looking for locum shifts in the ACT or anywhere else in Australia, check out StatDoctor and connect directly with hospitals -- no agency fees, no middlemen.
fieldData.post-summary: Locum doctor work in the ACT offers competitive rates across Canberra's three public hospitals. Learn about pay, demand, GP shortages, and how to get started.
fieldData.author: 68e360c1b6fe0fae2425baeb
fieldData.name: Locum Doctor Work in the ACT: Pay Rates, Hospitals, and What to Expect

fieldData.post-body:
The short answer: locum doctors in Australia earn between $140 and $294 per hour depending on seniority and specialty, or $1,100 to $4,500+ per day. The long answer depends on whether you are an RMO, registrar, or consultant, which specialty you work in, which state you work in, and whether you are willing to travel to rural or remote areas. Here is the full picture using real shift data from 2025-26.

National Averages by Seniority

Based on Medlo's 2026 dataset covering over 49,000 locum shifts across five states:

Resident Medical Officer (RMO/HMO): $140.27 per hour, or $1,122 per day. Annualised at 38 hours per week for 50 weeks, that is approximately $266,500.

Registrar: $172.99 per hour, or $1,384 per day. Annualised: approximately $328,700.

Consultant: $229.36 per hour, or $1,835 per day. Annualised: approximately $435,800.

These are averages across all specialties and states. Night shifts pay more ($160/hr for RMOs, $192/hr for registrars). Last-minute shifts attract 20-40 per cent premiums. Rural placements consistently pay more than metro.

The Highest-Paying Specialties

At consultant level, the specialty spread is enormous:

Psychiatry: $294/hr ($558,600 annualised) -- the highest by a wide margin, driven by a projected 20.7 per cent national undersupply by 2048

Palliative Care: $278/hr

Emergency Medicine: $238/hr

General Practice: $223/hr

Anaesthetics: $213/hr

Paediatrics: $183/hr

Surgery: $177/hr

At registrar level, Emergency Medicine leads at $196/hr, followed by ENT ($180/hr) and Plastics ($178/hr). Paediatric registrars average $161/hr. The registrar  night shift premium is significant -- $192/hr average versus $166/hr for day shifts.

At RMO level, Anaesthetics and Emergency Medicine tie at $151/hr, with Gastroenterology at the bottom ($121/hr). The RMO specialty spread is narrower ($30/hr) than at consultant level ($166/hr).

Radiology sits outside the standard Medlo dataset because most radiology locum work is structured differently (daily rates through agencies rather than hourly hospital shifts). Locum radiologists earn $3,500-$4,500+ per day based on current agency listings.

How States Compare

State-level differences are significant and vary by seniority:

Seasonal Patterns

Timing matters. The highest-paying months by seniority level:

RMOs: December ($154/hr average) -- driven by summer holiday coverage demand

Registrars: February ($181/hr) -- start-of-year turnover before new trainees are onboarded

Consultants: March ($242/hr)

The lowest-paying months are typically August and June, when rosters are most stable. If you can time your locum blocks around seasonal peaks, you can add 10-15 per cent to your effective earnings.

What Affects Your Take-Home Pay

Gross earnings are not the full picture. As a locum doctor, you need to account for:

GST: If you earn over $75,000 (and you almost certainly will), you must register for GST and lodge quarterly BAS returns. Your invoices should include 10 per cent GST on top of your rate.

Income tax: Locum income is taxed at your personal marginal rate. At $300,000, you are paying 45 cents on every dollar above $190,000 plus the 2 per cent Medicare levy. Set aside 30-40 per cent of gross income for tax.

Superannuation: As a sole trader, you are not required to pay yourself super, but you should. At 12 per cent of gross income, the opportunity cost of not contributing adds up fast.

Medical indemnity insurance: $200-$5,000+ per year depending on specialty and scope.

ABN setup and accounting: $1,000-$3,000 per year for a medical accountant to handle BAS and tax returns.

No paid leave: Unlike permanent employees, you do not earn annual leave, sick leave, or long service leave. Every day off is unpaid.

For a detailed breakdown of the tax side, see our guides on BAS and GST for locum doctors and superannuation for locum doctors.

The Bottom Line

Locum medicine in Australia pays well -- significantly better than permanent positions at the same seniority level, particularly in rural and remote settings. A consultant psychiatrist working locum in Queensland can gross over $650,000. An ED registrar working four days a week across metro hospitals will clear $300,000+. Even an RMO picking up shifts between training terms can earn $266,000 annualised.

The trade-off is that you manage your own tax, insurance, super, and credentialing. There is no employer to handle the paperwork, no guaranteed continuity, and no paid leave. Whether that trade-off works depends on your career stage, your risk tolerance, and how much you value flexibility over security.

If you are looking for locum shifts, download the StatDoctor app and connect directly with hospitals across Australia -- no agency fees, no middlemen.

RMO rates: South Australia pays the most at $161.79/hr, followed by Victoria ($143.31/hr) and NSW ($139.71/hr). Western Australia is lowest at $120/hr (small sample).

Registrar rates: Victoria leads at $179.53/hr, followed by Queensland ($177.39/hr), SA ($174.60/hr), and NSW ($172.75/hr). WA is lowest at $148.77/hr.

Consultant rates: Queensland pays the most at $258.78/hr, followed by NSW ($225.52/hr). SA shows $114.97/hr but from a small sample that likely skews low.

Critical caveat: Victoria and Queensland rates are generally inclusive of superannuation (currently 12 per cent), while NSW typically pays super on top. A $150/hr NSW rate is worth approximately 12 per cent more than a $150/hr VIC or QLD rate in real terms. Always clarify whether a quoted rate is super-inclusive before accepting.

Rural vs Metro: How Much More Does the Bush Pay?

The rural premium is real and substantial. Based on industry data and agency listings:

GP locum metro: $1,200-$1,800 per day

GP locum rural clinic: $2,000-$2,500 per day

GP locum remote AMS (Aboriginal Medical Service): $2,500-$3,500 per day

FACEM locum metro: $2,500-$3,000 per shift

FACEM locum rural/remote: $3,000-$4,000+ per shift

Anaesthetist locum rural: $3,000-$4,000+ per day

Psychiatrist locum: $2,000-$3,050 per day (NSW capped at $3,050)

Rural placements almost always include flights, accommodation, and often a vehicle. The Northern Territory and remote WA/QLD offer the highest absolute rates. For more on specific state opportunities, see our locum guides for every state and territory.

State-level differences are significant and vary by seniority:

RMO rates: South Australia pays the most at $161.79/hr, followed by Victoria ($143.31/hr) and NSW ($139.71/hr). Western Australia is lowest at $120/hr (small sample).

Registrar rates: Victoria leads at $179.53/hr, followed by Queensland ($177.39/hr), SA ($174.60/hr), and NSW ($172.75/hr). WA is lowest at $148.77/hr.

Consultant rates: Queensland pays the most at $258.78/hr, followed by NSW ($225.52/hr). SA shows $114.97/hr but from a small sample that likely skews low.

Critical caveat: Victoria and Queensland rates are generally inclusive of superannuation (currently 12 per cent), while NSW typically pays super on top. A $150/hr NSW rate is worth approximately 12 per cent more than a $150/hr VIC or QLD rate in real terms. Always clarify whether a quoted rate is super-inclusive before accepting.

‍
fieldData.post-summary: How much do locum doctors earn in Australia in 2026? Complete guide with real data by seniority, specialty, and state -- from $140/hr RMOs to $294/hr psychiatrists.
fieldData.author: 68e360c1b6fe0fae2425baeb
fieldData.name: How Much Do Locum Doctors Earn in Australia? The Complete 2026 Guide

fieldData.post-body:
Radiology is one of the highest-paying locum specialties in Australia, and the demand shows no signs of easing. Diagnostic imaging services in Australia grew 11.3 per cent year-on-year in early 2023, and the workforce has not kept pace. The Royal Australian and New Zealand College of Radiologists reports 2,332 practising radiologists nationally, but persistent vacancies, uneven regional distribution, and rising burnout mean that locum radiologists can command premium rates across both public and private settings. If you hold FRANZCR, here is what the market looks like.

Why Radiology Locum Demand Is Growing

Several structural factors are driving demand:

  • Australia's population is ageing. The AIHW projects cancer diagnoses will increase by 22 per cent between 2021 and 2031, reaching 185,000 cases per year.
  • CT and MRI volumes are growing faster than the workforce. CT services increased 15.5 per cent and MRI 10.2 per cent in the January-March 2023 quarter.
  • Training takes over a decade. RANZCR data shows consultant growth at a CAGR of 4.0 per cent, but trainee growth at only 2.6 per cent.
  • Maldistribution is severe. While 70 per cent of Australians live in metropolitan areas, 87 per cent of radiologists do.
  • Burnout is real. Longer reporting lists and growing expectations for rapid turnaround are driving experienced radiologists to reduce hours or retire early.

What Locum Radiologists Actually Earn

‍

Based on current data from Charterhouse Medical, RANZCR job listings, and agency postings:

  • General locum radiologist: $3,500-$4,500+ per day. Regional and urgent-fill placements push higher.
  • Subspecialty premiums: MSK, breast, neuroradiology, nuclear medicine/PET, and interventional radiology command higher rates.
  • Tier A procedural radiologists: $4,000-$4,500+ per day for locums with interventional skills.
  • Teleradiology (remote reporting): Typically $150-$250+ per study depending on complexity.
  • Permanent public hospital consultant salary: Approximately $250,000-$500,000.

A locum radiologist working four days a week at $4,000 per day for 46 weeks would gross $736,000.

‍

Most regional locum placements include return airfares, accommodation, hire car, and mileage reimbursement.

How Locum Radiology Works in Practice

On-Site Reporting

You physically attend a hospital or imaging clinic, report studies on their PACS system, attend MDT meetings, and perform procedures if credentialed.

Teleradiology

Remote reporting has expanded options significantly. You report from home or a remote workstation, accessing the facility's PACS remotely. Valued for after-hours reporting, supplementing on-site staff, and providing subspecialty cover. Requires appropriate IT setup and credentialing.

Mixed Models

Many locum radiologists combine on-site blocks with teleradiology reporting in between. This maximises income while reducing travel fatigue.

What You Need

AHPRA specialist registration and FRANZCR.

ABN -- required for contractor-based locum work.

Medical indemnity insurance -- must cover diagnostic reporting, procedural work, and teleradiology.

Radiation licences -- state-specific requirements apply for fluoroscopy and CT-guided procedures.

CPD compliance -- RANZCR runs its own CPD program.

National police check and Working with Children Check.

Credentialing -- requires evidence of reporting volumes, procedural logbooks, and two recent referees.

Where the Best Opportunities Are

Regional Australia offers the highest rates and the most consistent demand. Hospitals in Western NSW, regional Victoria, Far North Queensland, rural SA, and Tasmania regularly advertise locum radiology positions. Metro private imaging groups also hire locums at somewhat lower rates.

If you are looking for radiology locum shifts, download the StatDoctor app and connect directly with hospitals across Australia.
fieldData.post-summary: Locum radiologist jobs in Australia pay $3,500 to $4,500+ per day. Learn about the workforce shortage, teleradiology options, and what FRANZCR holders need.
fieldData.author: 68e360c1b6fe0fae2425baeb
fieldData.name: Locum Radiology Jobs in Australia: Pay Rates, Demand, and How It Works

fieldData.post-body:
Almost a quarter of Australian children starting school are facing developmental difficulties in at least one area, according to the 2024 Australian Early Childhood Development Census. The RACP has called for more paediatricians and more paediatric training places to address growing waitlists for assessment and intervention. For locum paediatricians and paediatric registrars, that unmet demand translates into consistent work across metro, regional, and rural Australia.

The Paediatric Workforce Shortage

The shortage is driven by several converging factors. Demand for paediatric services -- developmental assessments, behavioural paediatrics, chronic disease management, and neonatal care -- is growing faster than the workforce. Families are facing long waitlists for the recommended early childhood development checks at 18 months, 2, 3, and 4 years of age.

RACP President of Paediatric and Child Health, Dr Niroshini Kennedy, has stated that "governments must create more paediatric training places, including in regional and rural areas, to ensure the necessary workforce pipeline for the future demand." The RACP's 2026-27 pre-budget submission calls for investment to address workforce gaps, with specific attention to specialist care in regional, rural, and remote areas.

The shortage is worst outside major cities. Rural and regional hospitals struggle to recruit permanent paediatricians, and many rely on visiting or locum specialists to maintain services.

What Locum Paediatricians Actually Earn

Based on Medlo's 2026 shift data and agency listings:

RMO/HMO in paediatrics: $130 per hour average (154 shifts sampled). This sits at the lower end of the RMO specialty range.

Paediatric registrar: $161 per hour average (1,690 shifts sampled). Night shifts push to approximately $192/hr.

Consultant paediatrician: $183 per hour average (168 shifts sampled), equating to approximately $347,700 annually.

Locum consultant paediatrician (agency rates): $2,200-$3,000 per day, with rural placements at the higher end plus flights and accommodation.

The locum premium in paediatrics is more moderate than in shortage specialties like psychiatry ($294/hr for consultants) or anaesthetics ($213/hr). This is partly because paediatric locum demand, while growing, is concentrated in specific settings (regional hospitals, neonatal units, developmental clinics) rather than being systemic across every hospital.

Where the Demand Is

Regional and Rural Hospitals

This is where paediatric locum demand is most acute. Regional hospitals with maternity and paediatric units need consultant cover for inpatient paediatrics, neonatal resuscitation, and outpatient clinics. Towns like Dubbo, Wagga Wagga (NSW), Ballarat, Bendigo (VIC), Bundaberg (QLD), and Mount Gambier (SA) regularly advertise paediatric locum positions.

Neonatal Units

Special Care Nurseries and Neonatal Intensive Care Units in regional hospitals often rely on locum neonatologists or paediatricians with neonatal skills. This is specialised work requiring specific credentialing and experience.

Developmental and Behavioural Paediatrics

The waitlist crisis in developmental paediatrics means that any locum paediatrician with subspecialty interest in autism spectrum disorder, ADHD, developmental delay, or behavioural assessment is in particularly high demand. Some of this work can be done via telehealth.

Metropolitan Hospitals

Major children's hospitals (Royal Children's Melbourne, Westmead Children's Hospital Sydney, Queensland Children's Hospital, Women's and Children's Adelaide) have regular locum requirements for ward cover, ED, and outpatient clinics. Rates are lower than rural but shifts are more consistent.

What You Need

1. AHPRA registration -- specialist registration for consultant roles, general registration for registrars.

2. FRACP in Paediatrics and Child Health for consultant locum roles.

3. ABN -- required for contractor-based locum work.

4. Medical indemnity insurance -- must cover paediatric practice.

5. Working with Children Check -- mandatory in every state.

6. National police check -- within the last two years.

7. Neonatal resuscitation certification -- NLS or equivalent, required for most roles involving newborns.

8. Credentialing -- each hospital runs its own process.

Subspecialty Demand

The subspecialties with strongest locum demand:

General paediatrics (inpatient and outpatient): The bread and butter of paediatric locum work.

Neonatology: High-acuity, high-responsibility. Regional SCN/NICU cover is consistently needed.

Developmental and behavioural paediatrics: Waitlist pressure creates demand for clinic sessions, including via telehealth.

Paediatric emergency medicine: Children's hospital EDs and mixed EDs with paediatric presentations.

The Practical Reality

Paediatric locum work has unique clinical demands. You will work with families, not just patients. Communication expectations are high -- parents are anxious, informed, and expect clear explanations. Regional placements often mean you are the sole paediatrician on site. After-hours calls, neonatal emergencies, and parental anxiety at 3am are part of the package.

If you are looking for paediatric locum shifts, download the StatDoctor app and connect directly with hospitals across Australia.
fieldData.post-summary: Locum paediatrics jobs in Australia pay $130-$183/hr depending on seniority. Learn about the growing shortage, where demand is highest, and requirements.

fieldData.post-body:
How much do locum doctors actually earn in Australia? The answer depends on three things: your seniority level, your specialty, and where you work. Using 2026 shift data -- drawn from over 10,000 locum shifts across five states -- here is a data-driven breakdown of what locum doctors earn at each level, how rates vary by specialty, and which states pay the most.

Locum Pay Rates by Seniority Level

The national averages across all specialties and states:

Resident Medical Officer (RMO/HMO): $140 per hour ($1,100 per day).

Registrar: $170 per hour ($1,400 per day).

Consultant: $230 per hour ($2000 per day).

These are averages. Actual rates vary significantly depending on specialty, location, shift type (day vs night), and how urgently the shift needs filling.

Night shifts typically pay $160+ per hour for RMOs.

Last-minute bookings routinely attract 20-40% premiums.

RMO/HMO Rates by Specialty

At the resident level:

Anaesthetics: $150/hr (highest)

Emergency Medicine: $150/hr

Psychiatry: $140/hr

General Medicine: $140/hr

Orthopaedics: $140/hr

Surgery: $130/hr

Obstetrics and Gynaecology: $132/hr

Paediatrics: $130/hr

Registrar Rates by Specialty

At registrar level, specialty differentiation becomes more pronounced:

Emergency Medicine: $200/hr

ENT: $180/hr

Plastics: $180/hr

Obstetrics and Gynaecology: $180/hr

Intensive Care: $170/hr

Geriatrics: $170/hr

Orthopaedics: $160/hr

Surgery: $160/hr

Anaesthetics: $160/hr

General Medicine: $150/hr

Psychiatry: $150/hr

Rehabilitation: $150/hr

Cardiology: $140/hr

Consultant Rates by Specialty

At consultant level, the differences are dramatic:

Psychiatry: $300/hr (

Palliative Care: $280/hr

Oncology: $250/hr

Emergency Medicine: $240/hr

General Practice: $210/hr

Anaesthetics: $210/hr

Urgent Care: $210/hr

General Medicine: $200/hr

Obstetrics and Gynaecology: $200/hr

Orthopaedics: $180/hr

Paediatrics: $180/hr

Surgery: $170/hr

Rates by State

RMO level:

South Australia: $160/hr (highest)

NSW: $140/hr

Victoria: comparable to NSW

Western Australia: $120/hr (lowest)

Registrar level:

Victoria: $180/hr (highest)

Queensland: $175hr

SA: $175/hr

NSW: $175/hr

WA: $150/hr (lowest)

Consultant level:

Queensland: $258.78/hr (highest)

NSW: $225.52/hr (4,098 shifts)

SA: $114.97/hr (lowest -- small sample)

Small sample sizes in some states mean these figures should be treated as indicative rather than definitive.

Super-Inclusive vs Super-Exclusive Rates

Victoria and Queensland rates are generally inclusive of superannuation, while NSW typically pays super on top. A $150/hr offer in NSW is worth approximately 12 per cent more in real terms than $150/hr in Victoria or QLD. Always ask whether a quoted rate is super-inclusive before accepting.

Seasonal Patterns

Locum rates follow predictable seasonal patterns:

December is the highest-paying month for RMOs ($154/hr average).

February is the highest for registrars ($181/hr).

March is the highest for consultants ($242/hr).

August and June tend to be the lowest-paying months across all levels.

Timing your locum blocks around these seasonal peaks can add 10-15 per cent to your effective earnings.

What This Means in Practice

The data confirms what experienced locum doctors already know: specialty, location, and timing all matter. A consultant psychiatrist working locum in Queensland earns roughly three times what an RMO in general medicine earns in WA. Choosing where, when, and what you locum in is the single biggest lever on your income.

If you are looking for locum shifts, download the StatDoctor app and connect directly with hospitals across Australia.
fieldData.post-summary: Locum doctor pay rates in Australia compared by state and specialty. Real data from 17,000+ shifts covering RMOs, registrars, and consultants in 2025-26.
fieldData.author: 68e360c1b6fe0fae2425baeb
fieldData.name: Locum Doctor Pay Rates in Australia: A Comparison by State and Specialty

fieldData.post-body:
The United States is one of six Competent Authority countries recognised by the Medical Board of Australia. If you have passed all three USMLE Steps and completed at least two years of ACGME-accredited residency training, you can register with AHPRA without sitting the AMC exams. That makes the US-to-Australia pathway one of the more accessible international routes -- though it still involves paperwork, timelines, and a supervised practice period. Here is how it works.

The Competent Authority Pathway: Category D (USA - ECFMG/USMLE)

The USA has two Competent Authority categories:

Category D (ECFMG/USMLE) -- for MDs:

Assessment: Passed USMLE Steps 1, 2 CK (Clinical Knowledge), and Step 3. All three steps must be completed.

Experience: Completed a minimum of two years of graduate medical education within an ACGME-accredited residency program.

Category G (NBOME/COMLEX-USA) -- for DOs:

Assessment: Passed all levels of COMLEX-USA (Level 1, Level 2-CE, Level 2-PE, and Level 3).

Experience: Completed two years of ACGME or AOA-accredited residency training.

The key requirement that catches some US doctors off guard: you need two full years of residency, not just one. A doctor who has completed medical school and passed all USMLE Steps but has not done residency is not eligible for the Competent Authority pathway.

Step-by-Step: From the US to Australian Registration

Step 1: Primary Source Verification via ECFMG (2-4 months)

The AMC requires Primary Source Verification (PSV) of your medical qualifications through the ECFMG's EPIC system. Since the ECFMG is also the US competent authority, this is a natural fit -- but the process still takes 2-4 months. Start this while you are still in the US.

Step 2: Apply for Provisional Registration with AHPRA

Once PSV is complete, apply for provisional registration through the AHPRA portal. You will need:

Evidence of Competent Authority eligibility (AMC recommendation)

A confirmed clinical position at an Australian health service

A nominated supervisor approved by the Medical Board

A completed Supervised Practice Plan (SPPA-30) and Supervisor's Declaration (DFLP-00)

AHPRA processing takes 4-12 weeks for complete applications.

Step 3: Complete 12 Months of Supervised Practice

You must complete a minimum of 12 months (47 weeks FTE) of supervised practice. This is paid work in a real clinical position. During this period:

First 3 months: complete an orientation to the Australian health system

At 3 months: submit orientation and work performance reports

Most US doctors complete this at a public hospital that has agreed to provide supervision. Rural and regional hospitals are often more willing to take on supervised IMGs.

Step 4: Apply for General Registration

After completing supervised practice, you apply for full general registration. This removes supervision requirements and gives you unrestricted ability to practise anywhere in Australia.

Visas

Most US doctors come to Australia on a Skills in Demand visa (subclass 482), which requires employer sponsorship. Medical practitioners are on Australia's Skilled Occupation List.

Key points:

You need your AHPRA registration number before applying for a visa.

The subclass 482 visa allows you to live and work in Australia for up to 4 years.

Permanent residency pathways exist through the Employer Nomination Scheme (subclass 186) or skilled regional visas (subclass 491/494).

What US Doctors Should Expect

The clinical systems are more similar than different. Australia and the US both run evidence-based medicine with multidisciplinary team structures and electronic health records. But there are meaningful differences:

Terminology changes. "Attending" becomes "consultant." "Resident" becomes "registrar." "OR" becomes "theatre." "ER" becomes "ED." "Tylenol" becomes "paracetamol."

The pay structure is different. US attending salaries are generally higher in absolute dollar terms. But Australia offers universal healthcare (no insurance billing complexity), lower malpractice risk, mandatory employer superannuation, and a work culture that genuinely values time off.

Medicare is not the same thing. In Australia, Medicare is the universal public healthcare system for all residents -- not a program for the over-65s.

The malpractice environment is dramatically different. Australia does not have the same litigation culture. Medical indemnity insurance premiums are a fraction of US malpractice costs, and defensive medicine is less prevalent.

Once Registered: Locum Work

Once you hold general registration, locum work is immediately available. You will need an ABN, medical indemnity insurance, and credentialing at each facility.

For US doctors accustomed to the complexity of insurance billing, Australian locum work is refreshingly simple: you invoice the hospital for your hours, they pay you, and you manage your own tax. No CPT codes, no prior authorisations, no insurance denials.

Locum rates range from $1,500-$3,000+ per day for GPs and $2,000-$4,000+ per day for specialists, depending on location and specialty.

If you are looking for locum shifts once registered, download the StatDoctor app and connect directly with hospitals across Australia -- no agency fees.
fieldData.post-summary: US doctor moving to Australia? Learn about USMLE-based Competent Authority pathway, ECFMG verification, AHPRA registration, visa options, and locum work.
fieldData.author: 68e360c1b6fe0fae2425baeb
fieldData.name: Moving from the USA to Australia as a Doctor: USMLE, Registration, and Locum Work

fieldData.post-body:
Most locum doctors in Australia start as sole traders. It is the simplest structure: you get an ABN, invoice hospitals directly, and pay tax at your personal marginal rate. But as your locum income grows -- and most locum doctors earn well above $180,000 -- the question of whether to incorporate as a Pty Ltd company becomes increasingly relevant. Here is a practical comparison of the two structures, including the critical Personal Services Income rules that limit what a company structure can actually do for you.

How the Two Structures Work

Sole Trader

As a sole trader, your ABN is linked to your personal tax file number. All income flows directly to you, and you pay tax at your personal marginal rate. For the 2025-26 financial year, that means 45 cents on every dollar above $190,000, plus the 2 per cent Medicare levy. On $300,000 of locum income, your effective tax rate (including Medicare levy) will be approximately 36-38 per cent.

The advantages are simplicity: one tax return, minimal compliance costs, no ASIC fees, and no separate company accounts. The disadvantage is that every additional dollar above $190,000 is taxed at the top marginal rate, and your personal assets have no legal separation from your business liabilities.

Pty Ltd Company

A proprietary limited company is a separate legal entity. It has its own ABN, its own tax file number, its own bank account, and its own tax rate. The company tax rate for base rate entities (aggregated turnover under $50 million) is 25 per cent -- significantly lower than the 47 per cent top personal rate.

The appeal is obvious: if you could route $300,000 of locum income through a company and pay 25 per cent instead of 47 per cent, you would save over $60,000 per year. But the ATO has rules specifically designed to prevent this for people whose income comes from their personal labour. Those rules are called Personal Services Income (PSI).

The PSI Problem: Why a Company Does Not Automatically Save You Tax

This is the part most accountants gloss over and most locum doctors do not understand until it is too late.

Under the Personal Services Income rules, if your income is produced mainly (more than 50 per cent) from your personal skills or efforts, it is classified as PSI. For locum doctors, this is almost always the case -- you are being paid to turn up and practise medicine.

When income is PSI, it must be attributed to you personally and taxed at your personal marginal rate -- regardless of whether it flows through a company or trust. You cannot retain profits in the company to access the lower 25 per cent rate. You cannot distribute income to family members to use their lower tax brackets.

This means that for most locum doctors, operating through a Pty Ltd does NOT reduce your income tax. The PSI rules override the company structure.

Can You Pass the Personal Services Business (PSB) Tests?

There is an exception. If you can demonstrate that you are operating a genuine Personal Services Business, the PSI restrictions do not apply. The key tests are:

1. Results test -- you are paid for producing a specific result, you provide your own tools, and you are responsible for rectifying defects. Most locum doctors fail this test because you are paid for time, not for a defined result.

2. 80 per cent rule -- less than 80 per cent of your income comes from a single client. If you work for multiple hospitals and agencies, you may pass this test.

3. Unrelated clients test -- you advertise and offer services to the public.

Passing one of these tests allows your company to retain profits and access the lower tax rate. But the ATO actively scrutinises medical professionals who claim PSB status. Get specific advice before relying on this.

When a Company Structure Does Make Sense

Despite the PSI limitations, there are legitimate reasons for a locum doctor to operate through a Pty Ltd:

- Asset protection: A company creates a legal separation between your business and personal assets.

- Non-PSI income: If you earn income from sources other than your personal labour -- such as employing other doctors or providing medical education -- that income may benefit from the lower company tax rate.

- Superannuation flexibility: A company can make employer super contributions on your behalf.

- Credibility and structure: Some hospitals prefer to contract with a company rather than a sole trader.

The trigger for most doctors to incorporate is not income alone -- it is when asset protection becomes relevant or when your practice grows beyond just your personal labour.

The Numbers: A Practical Comparison

Take a locum doctor earning $300,000 gross per year with $30,000 in deductible expenses.

As a sole trader: Taxable income of $270,000. Approximate tax (including Medicare levy) of $97,000-$100,000. Net after tax: approximately $170,000-$173,000.

Through a Pty Ltd (if PSI rules apply): Same result. The income is attributed to you personally. The company structure adds compliance costs (ASIC fee $310, separate tax return) without reducing your tax.

Through a Pty Ltd (if PSB tests are passed): Company taxable income of $270,000. Company tax at 25 per cent: $67,500. Potential saving of $30,000-$35,000 per year -- but only if you genuinely pass the PSB tests and can defend that position under ATO audit.

What to Do

If you are starting out as a locum doctor, begin as a sole trader. It is simpler, cheaper, and for most locum doctors, produces the same tax outcome as a company under PSI rules.

If your income exceeds $250,000 and you work for multiple hospitals, speak with a medical accountant about whether you pass the PSB tests. Do not incorporate based on generic advice -- get a specific ruling on your circumstances.

If you are looking for locum shifts, download the StatDoctor app and connect directly with hospitals across Australia.
fieldData.post-summary: Sole trader vs Pty Ltd for locum doctors in Australia. Compare tax rates, PSI rules, asset protection, and when it makes sense to incorporate.
fieldData.author: 68e360c1b6fe0fae2425baeb
fieldData.name: Sole Trader vs Pty Ltd for Locum Doctors: Which Structure Is Right for You?

fieldData.post-body:
Canada is one of six Competent Authority countries recognised by the Medical Board of Australia. That means if you hold the Licentiate of the Medical Council of Canada (LMCC) and have completed residency training, you can register with AHPRA without sitting the AMC exams. It is one of the most straightforward international pathways to practising medicine in Australia. Here is exactly how it works, including recent changes to the Canadian category that took effect in late 2024.

The Competent Authority Pathway: Category C (Canada)

Canada falls under Category C of the Competent Authority pathway. The Medical Council of Canada (MCC) is the recognised competent authority.

To qualify, you need to meet both an assessment component and an experience component:

Assessment component (one of the following):

Successful completion of both LMCC exams (MCCQE Part I and Part II), OR

Since November 2024: completion of MCCQE Part I PLUS either graduation from a Canadian medical school OR successful completion of the National Assessment Collaboration (NAC) examination

Experience component (one of the following):

Completion of 12 months of residency training in Canada in an accredited program, OR

Completion of 12 months of supervised training in another Competent Authority country (e.g. UK, USA, NZ), OR

Two years of graduate medical education within an ACGME-accredited residency program in the USA

The 2024 Change Explained

During COVID-19, the MCC discontinued the MCCQE Part II exam. This created an issue for Canadian doctors who had only completed Part I. In October 2024, the Medical Board of Australia updated Category C to address this. If you have only completed Part I, you must have either graduated from a Canadian medical school or passed the NAC examination to be eligible.

If you completed both MCCQE Part I and Part II before the discontinuation, you remain eligible under the original criteria.

Step-by-Step: From Canada to Australian Registration

Step 1: Primary Source Verification (2-4 months)

Before applying to AHPRA, you need Primary Source Verification (PSV) of your medical qualifications through the ECFMG's EPIC system. Create an AMC account and portfolio, then apply to ECFMG for verification. They will contact your Canadian medical school directly. This process takes 2-4 months -- start it before you leave Canada.

Step 2: Apply for Provisional Registration with AHPRA

Once your PSV is complete, you apply for provisional registration through the AHPRA portal. You will need:

Your AMC certificate or evidence of Competent Authority eligibility

A confirmed position at an Australian health service willing to provide supervision

A nominated supervisor approved by the Board

A completed Supervised Practice Plan (SPPA-30 form) and Supervisor's Declaration (DFLP-00 form)

AHPRA processing typically takes 4-8 weeks once your application is complete.

Step 3: Complete 12 Months of Supervised Practice

You must complete a minimum of 12 months (47 weeks full-time equivalent) of supervised practice in Australia. During this period:

At 0-3 months: complete an orientation to the Australian health system

At 3 months: submit orientation and work performance reports

Throughout: maintain your supervision plan and meet regularly with your nominated supervisor

This period is paid work in a real clinical position.

Step 4: Apply for General Registration

After satisfactorily completing 12 months of supervised practice, you can apply for full general registration. This removes supervision requirements and gives you unrestricted ability to practise anywhere in Australia.

Visas: Getting Permission to Work

Most Canadian doctors come to Australia on a Skills in Demand visa (subclass 482), which requires employer sponsorship. Medical practitioners are on Australia's Skilled Occupation List.

Key points:

You need your AHPRA registration before applying for a visa. Applying without an AHPRA registration number may delay processing.

The subclass 482 visa allows you to live and work in Australia for up to 4 years.

Permanent residency pathways exist through the Employer Nomination Scheme (subclass 186) or skilled regional visas (subclass 491/494).

Hospitals in rural and regional areas are often more willing to sponsor visa applicants.

What Canadian Doctors Should Expect

The clinical adjustment from Canada to Australia is relatively smooth. The healthcare systems share a lot of DNA: both are publicly funded, evidence-based, and structured around primary care referral pathways. But there are differences worth knowing:

Medical terminology differs. You will hear "registrar" instead of "resident," "consultant" instead of "attending," "theatre" instead of "operating room," and "super" instead of "pension." Drug names overlap but not completely -- familiarise yourself with the Australian PBS formulary.

Medicare is Australia's universal healthcare system, and it works differently from provincial health insurance. You will need a Medicare provider number at each location where you work.

The lifestyle adjustment is the easy part. Canada and Australia share similar values around work-life balance, outdoor culture, and multiculturalism. Many Canadian doctors report that the transition feels natural, with the addition of better weather.

Once Registered: Locum Work as an Option

Once you hold general registration, locum work is immediately available. You will need:

An ABN (Australian Business Number)

Medical indemnity insurance

Credentialing at each facility

Locum work is popular among international doctors in their first few years. It gives you the flexibility to explore different hospitals, cities, and regions while earning well. Rural and remote locum placements pay the highest rates -- $2,000-$3,000+ per day for GPs and significantly more for specialists.

If you are looking for locum shifts once registered, download the StatDoctor app and connect directly with hospitals across Australia -- no agency fees.
fieldData.post-summary: Canadian doctor moving to Australia? Learn about the Competent Authority pathway, LMCC requirements, visa options, and how to start working once registered.
fieldData.author: 68e360c1b6fe0fae2425baeb
fieldData.name: Moving from Canada to Australia as a Doctor: Registration, Visas, and Locum Work

fieldData.post-body:
General practice is the backbone of locum work in Australia. GPs fill more locum shifts nationally than any other specialty, working across private clinics, Aboriginal health services, rural hospitals, urgent care centres, and after-hours services. Rates range from $1,500 to $3,000+ per day depending on location and scope, with rural and remote placements consistently paying the most. Here is what you need to know about locum GP work, including the critical VR vs non-VR distinction that affects where you can work and how much you earn.

What Locum GPs Actually Earn

GP locum rates vary widely depending on location, scope of practice, and whether the role includes hospital or ED work:

Metro GP locum: $1,200-$1,800 per day, or approximately $150 per hour / 70 per cent of billings (whichever is greater). Many practices offer a guaranteed hourly rate for the first 3-4 months.

Rural GP locum (clinic only): $2,000-$2,500 per day. Travel and accommodation usually included.

Remote Aboriginal Medical Service (AMS): $2,500-$3,500 per day. Fly-in, fly-out placements with accommodation and flights covered.

GP ED / rural generalist (hospital + clinic): $1,800-$3,000 per day. These roles combine general practice with emergency department shifts and often include on-call.

After-hours / urgent care: Up to $3,500 per day for high-demand after-hours coverage.

For permanently employed GPs, income typically ranges from $200,000-$350,000 per year once Fellowed, and $250,000-$500,000+ in rural areas with incentives. Locum work at $2,000/day for four days a week across 46 weeks would gross $368,000 -- competitive with or exceeding permanent roles.

VR vs Non-VR: Why It Matters for Locum Work

The single most important distinction in Australian general practice is whether you are a Vocationally Registered (VR) GP or not.

VR GPs (Fellowed)

A VR GP has completed Fellowship with either RACGP (FRACGP) or ACRRM (FACRRM). VR GPs:

Bill at the higher A1 Medicare rebate rate for standard consultations

Can work in any location -- metro, rural, or remote -- without geographic restrictions

Are not subject to 19AA restrictions

Have access to the full range of locum GP opportunities

Command the highest locum rates

If you hold FRACGP or FACRRM, you are in the most in-demand category of locum GP.

Non-VR GPs

A non-VR GP has general AHPRA registration but has not completed Fellowship. Non-VR GPs:

Bill at the lower A2 (metro) or A7 (MM2-7 rural) Medicare rebate rate

Are typically restricted to working in Distribution Priority Areas (DPAs) or Modified Monash Model (MMM) regions

May be subject to 19AA restrictions requiring participation in an approved 3GA program to access Medicare benefits

Can access the A1 rebate if they work in designated rural/remote areas or after hours

Earn less per consultation than VR GPs due to the lower rebate

The practical impact: most metro locum GP positions require Fellowship. Rural and remote positions are more accessible to non-VR GPs.

Where the Locum GP Demand Is

GP locum demand exists in every state, but the highest-paying and most consistent work is rural and remote:

Northern Territory: Aboriginal health services pay $1,500-$2,000+ per day through the NT PHN locum program.

Rural WA: The Pilbara, Kimberley, and Goldfields regions have chronic GP shortages.

Queensland: Far North QLD, Central West, and North West HHSs have persistent demand.

NSW: Western NSW, Murrumbidgee, and Far West LHDs need GP locums year-round.

South Australia: Eyre and Far North, Flinders and Upper North, and Limestone Coast LHNs all advertise regularly.

Tasmania: The North West coast (Burnie, Devonport) is the state's locum hotspot.

Metro GP locum work is available in all capital cities but at lower rates and with more competition.

What You Need to Start

1. AHPRA registration -- unconditional general or specialist registration.

2. Fellowship (FRACGP or FACRRM) -- strongly preferred for most locum positions. Non-VR GPs should check location restrictions before applying.

3. ABN -- required for contractor-based locum work.

4. Medicare provider number -- you will need a provider number at each location.

5. Medical indemnity insurance -- must cover the scope of your locum work.

6. National police check and Working with Children Check (state-specific).

7. Vaccination records -- complete and current.

8. For rural/AMS roles: experience in Aboriginal health, emergency skills training, and a current manual driver's licence are commonly requested.

The Practical Side

Locum GP work is different from permanent practice. You will not have the continuity of knowing your patients. You will walk into clinics with unfamiliar software (Best Practice, Medical Director, Communicare), unfamiliar referral pathways, and unfamiliar staff. Adaptability is non-negotiable.

The best locum GPs build a reputation for reliability, flexibility, and clinical competence. Hospitals and clinics that trust you will book you back repeatedly, often at preferred rates. Your references matter enormously in this market.

If you are a GP looking for locum shifts, download the StatDoctor app and connect directly with hospitals and clinics across Australia.
fieldData.post-summary: Locum GP jobs in Australia pay $1,500 to $3,000+ per day. Learn about VR vs non-VR requirements, billing differences, and where rural demand is highest.
fieldData.author: 68e360c1b6fe0fae2425baeb
fieldData.name: Locum GP Jobs in Australia: Pay Rates, VR vs Non-VR, and How to Get Started

fieldData.post-body:
Tasmania spends more on locum doctors per capita than almost any other Australian state. Total locum costs tripled from $57 million in 2020-21 to $182 million in 2023-24, with medical locum costs alone hitting $105 million. In 2024-25, the state spent a further $105 million just on locum nurses -- a 600 per cent increase from $14.5 million four years earlier. The Rural Doctors Association of Tasmania estimates that the Mersey Community Hospital and North West Regional Hospital alone may have spent over $100 million on locums in the last financial year. For locum doctors, Tasmania represents some of the most consistent and well-paid work in Australia.

Tasmania's Hospital System

Tasmania has four major public hospitals:

Royal Hobart Hospital (RHH) -- the state's largest and only tertiary referral centre. Located in the south, it handles the most complex cases including trauma, cardiac surgery, and neurosurgery.

Launceston General Hospital (LGH) -- the main hospital for northern Tasmania. A busy general hospital with a substantial ED, surgical services, and medical wards.

North West Regional Hospital (NWRH) -- located in Burnie, the primary healthcare facility for the North West coast.

Mersey Community Hospital -- in Latrobe, near Devonport. Provides secondary services and has been a major consumer of locum staffing.

These four hospitals are operated by the Tasmanian Health Service. The three regional areas (South, North, North West) each have their own workforce challenges, but the North West has been the most reliant on locum doctors.

What Locum Doctors Earn in Tasmania

Tasmania's locum rates are competitive nationally. Based on current listings and industry data:

FACEM/ED consultant (Hobart private ED): $275 per hour or 70 per cent of billings.

GP ED/rural generalist: $1,500-$2,200 per day for clinic work, up to $3,000 per day for combined GP-ED roles.

RMO/HMO locum: Rates broadly align with the national average of approximately $140 per hour, with night shift premiums around $160 per hour.

Specialist locums (anaesthetics, psychiatry, general medicine): $2,000-$3,500 per day depending on specialty, urgency, and location.

Travel and accommodation are typically included for placements outside Hobart.

Why Rates Stay High

The state's heavy locum spend is itself a signal. It costs between two and three times more per position to employ a locum compared to a permanent staff member. Despite this, Tasmania continues to rely on locums because it cannot fill permanent positions quickly enough. Regional hospitals often prefer locum specialists over permanent rural generalists, even when locally trained generalists are available -- a policy choice that inflates costs further.

Where the Locum Demand Is

North West Tasmania

Burnie and Devonport are the locum epicentre. NWRH and Mersey Community Hospital have the highest locum reliance in the state. Emergency medicine, general medicine, anaesthetics, and surgical cover are all regularly filled by locums.

Launceston and the North

Launceston General Hospital has regular locum requirements across ED, medicine, and surgical specialties. The surrounding district hospitals also need GP and generalist locum cover. Launceston offers a good balance -- a sizeable regional city with reliable amenities.

Hobart and the South

Royal Hobart Hospital has the largest permanent workforce in the state, so locum demand is lower in absolute terms. However, there are still regular openings in ED, psychiatry, and surgical specialties. Hobart also has private hospital locum opportunities, including private ED shifts paying $275 per hour for FACEMs.

Rural and Island Tasmania

Queenstown (West Coast), King Island, Flinders Island, and other rural centres depend on visiting and locum doctors. These placements offer genuine rural medicine -- broad scope, minimal backup, and communities who genuinely need you.

‍

What You Need to Locum in Tasmania

1. Current, unconditional AHPRA registration.

2. ABN -- required for contractor-based locum work.

3. Medical indemnity insurance -- confirm your policy covers Tasmania specifically. Historically, standard public hospital indemnity covered all states except Tasmania. Check this with your insurer.

4. Tasmanian Working with Children Check -- state-specific and separate from other states' checks.

5. National police check -- within the last two years.

6. Vaccination records -- complete immunisation history.

7. For GP roles: Fellowship with RACGP or ACRRM, or vocational registration.

Practical Tips

Tasmania's size is an advantage. Hobart to Launceston is a two-hour drive. Hobart to Burnie is about three and a half hours. You can realistically drive between major centres without needing flights, though King Island and Flinders Island require air travel.

The state recorded its strongest retention of locally trained doctors in early 2026, with 80 first-year doctors beginning placements. But it will be years before this translates into reduced locum dependency.

Tasmania's climate is temperate -- cooler than the mainland, no cyclones, no extreme heat. Winters are cold, particularly inland and in the highlands.

If you are looking for locum shifts in Tasmania, download the StatDoctor app and connect directly with hospitals.
fieldData.post-summary: Locum doctor work in Tasmania: the state spent $182 million on locums in 2023-24. Learn about pay rates, key hospitals, and what you need to get started.
fieldData.author: 68e360c1b6fe0fae2425baeb
fieldData.name: Locum Doctor Work in Tasmania: Pay Rates, Hospitals, and What to Expect

fieldData.post-body:
If you earn more than $75,000 per year from locum work -- and most locum doctors do -- you are legally required to register for GST and lodge quarterly Business Activity Statements with the ATO. This is not optional, and getting it wrong can mean paying 10 per cent GST out of your own pocket instead of passing it on to the hospital. Here is a practical guide to how BAS and GST work for locum doctors, with actual numbers.

When Do You Need to Register for GST?

You must register for GST if your gross ABN income exceeds $75,000 in a 12-month period, or if you reasonably expect it will. For most locum doctors, this threshold is reached quickly -- a doctor working just 10 shifts at $1,500 per day hits $75,000 within about 50 working days.

Registration is done through the Australian Business Register (ABR) when you set up your ABN, or separately if you already have an ABN but were not previously registered for GST.

The critical point: register before you cross $75,000, not after. If you cross the threshold without being registered, the ATO may retrospectively assess GST on all your income above $75,000 -- and since you have not been adding GST to your invoices, that 10 per cent comes directly out of your earnings.

The Common Mistake That Costs Doctors Thousands

Many locum doctors assume that medical services are GST-free. This is understandable -- when a patient pays a GP directly, that service is usually GST-free under the GST-free health services provisions.

But locum work is different. You are not billing a patient for a medical service. You are invoicing a hospital or agency for your labour under a contract. That is a taxable supply. GST applies.

Here is what happens if you get this wrong. Say you invoice a hospital $2,000 per day for 100 days, totalling $200,000. If you were GST-registered, you would have invoiced $2,000 plus $200 GST (i.e. $2,200 total), collected the $200 from the hospital, and remitted it to the ATO. Your take-home remains $2,000 per day.

If you were not GST-registered and the ATO catches up, they can assess GST of $18,182 on your $200,000 (calculated as 1/11th of the total, since GST is embedded). You never collected that $18,182 from the hospital, so it comes straight out of your pocket. That is a $18,182 bill you did not plan for.

Register early. Add GST to your invoices from day one.

How BAS Works: What You Lodge and When

Once you are GST-registered, you must lodge a Business Activity Statement each quarter. The BAS reports:

GST collected on sales (what you charged hospitals on your invoices)

GST paid on purchases (what you paid on business expenses like insurance, equipment, travel)

PAYG income tax instalments (quarterly prepayments towards your annual income tax)

BAS is lodged and paid quarterly. The standard lodgement dates are:

Quarter 1 (July-September): due 28 October

Quarter 2 (October-December): due 28 February

Quarter 3 (January-March): due 28 April

Quarter 4 (April-June): due 28 July

If you use a registered tax agent, you may get extended lodgement dates.

What You Actually Pay

The GST component of your BAS is the difference between GST collected and GST paid. If you invoiced $50,000 plus $5,000 GST in a quarter, and paid $500 GST on business expenses, you owe $4,500 to the ATO for that quarter.

The PAYG instalment component is a prepayment of your estimated income tax. The ATO calculates this based on your previous year's tax return. For your first year of locum work, there may be no PAYG instalment; from year two onward, expect quarterly tax prepayments.

What You Can Claim GST Credits On

Common GST-claimable expenses for locum doctors include:

Medical indemnity insurance premiums

Accounting and tax agent fees

Professional development and CPD courses

Medical equipment (stethoscope, ophthalmoscope, reference books)

Work-related travel (flights, car hire, fuel for work travel)

Professional memberships (AHPRA registration fee is GST-free, but some college fees include GST)

Home office expenses (if you have a dedicated workspace)

You cannot claim GST credits on items that are GST-free or input-taxed. Keep all tax invoices -- the ATO requires them to substantiate your claims.

PAYG Instalments: The Other Part of Your BAS

PAYG instalments are the ATO's way of ensuring you do not face a massive tax bill at the end of the financial year. After your first tax return with ABN income, the ATO will calculate a quarterly instalment amount based on your previous year's income.

For a locum doctor earning $250,000 gross, PAYG instalments might be $15,000-$20,000 per quarter, depending on deductions and your marginal tax rate. This is not an additional tax -- it is a prepayment of your annual liability.

If your income changes significantly, you can vary your PAYG instalment amount through your BAS. Speak with your accountant before doing this.

Record Keeping and Software

The ATO requires you to keep records of all income and expenses for five years. For locum doctors, this means:

Every invoice you issue to a hospital or agency

Every tax invoice for business expenses

Bank statements showing income received and expenses paid

A logbook if you claim motor vehicle expenses using the logbook method

Cloud accounting software (Xero, MYOB, or QuickBooks) makes BAS lodgement dramatically easier. At a minimum, open a separate business bank account for your ABN income. Do not mix personal and locum finances.

Common Mistakes to Avoid

1. Not registering for GST before crossing $75,000. Register proactively.

2. Assuming locum medical services are GST-free. They are not -- you are invoicing a hospital for labour, not billing a patient for healthcare.

3. Not setting aside money for tax. Expect to pay 30-40 per cent of your gross income in combined income tax and GST.

4. Lodging BAS late. The ATO issues late lodgement penalties starting at $313 per 28-day period overdue.

5. Forgetting HELP/HECS debt. If you have a HELP debt, it is not deducted from your locum income automatically. You must account for it in your annual tax return.

If you are looking for locum shifts, download the StatDoctor app and connect directly with hospitals across Australia.
fieldData.post-summary: BAS and GST explained for locum doctors in Australia. Learn when to register, how to lodge quarterly, common mistakes, and how to avoid ATO penalties.
fieldData.author: 68e360c1b6fe0fae2425baeb
fieldData.name: BAS and GST for Locum Doctors in Australia: What You Need to Lodge and When

fieldData.post-body:
Australia has a psychiatrist shortage that is projected to worsen for the next 25 years.
